Theory of the Motion of the Moon； containing a Neu Calculation of the Expres-sions for the Coordinates of thc Moon in terms of the Time. By ERNEST W. BROWN, M.A., Sc.D.

# INTRODUCTION.

TIE formation of numerieal expressions, deduced as a conscquenee of the Newtonian laws of motion and gravitation, which shallrepresent the position of the Moon at any time, may be roughly divided into three stages.As a first step we eonsider cach of the three bodies-the Sun,the Earth,and the Moon-as a sphere of mass equal toits actual mass and arranged in eoncentrie spherical layers of equal density. The Earth (or the eentre ofmass of the Earth and Moon) is supposed to move round the Sun in a certain idealeliptie orbit, and all disturbanees of this orbit and of the Moon from any other souree than the ideal Sun and Earth are neglected. This first stage eonstitutes nearly the whole of the labour of solving the problem of three bodies as far as the particular configuration of the Sun-Earth-Moon system is eoncerned. When this is done we proeed to the second step, whieh involves the determination of the effects due to the diference between the actual and ideal motions of the Earth and Sun,to the influences exerted by the other bodies of the solar system,and to the differenees between the real and ideal arrangemcnts of the masses of the bodies.The calculations so far may,theoreticallyat least,be made without any knowledge of the configuration of the system at any given time or times beyond a general idea of the order of magnitude of certain of the eonstants involved. The third and final stage consists in a determination by observation of the various constants which liave cntered into the theory, and the substitution of their values so as to obtain numerical expressions for the coordinates in terins of the time.

In actual practice these lines of division cannot be satisfactorily kept, partly from the length and complexity of the calculations necessary to obtain algebraical cxpressions snfficiently complete,and partly from a sinilar difculty in comparing large numbcrs of observations with the results of the theory for the purposc of obtaining the values of the constants involved. Certain of the latter,however, particularly those most frequently occurring, namely,the mean motions of the Sun (or Earth） and Moon, can bc obtained, with vcry little knowledge of the theory, with sufficient accuracy to enable ns to ise their numerical values at the outset,and so to save a large part of the labour. The first.and sceond steps may bc separated without much difficulty,and it is the first which forms the chief objeet of this Memoir.

Of the methods which have been devised to solve the first part of the problem, those of HANsEN and DELAUNAY must take the first place, not because they were intrinsically the best adapted to the purpose,but because in the hands of their authors tley were actually carried out with a degree of detail greater than any other. It is not necessary to discuss here their respeetive merits ; it is suffcient to recall the fact that HANsEN's was entirely numerieal, and was made the basis of the tables of the Moon used at the present time，although the latter were published forty years ago，while DELAUNAY's was, owing to the method used, entirely algebraical. Further, HANSEN embraced the whole problem in his thcory；DELAUNAY only lived long enough to complete the first step ；his work,however, has been carried on by othcr writers,and will attain its completion in the tables now in process of formation. The earlier inethod of LAPLAcE,adopted also by DAMOISEAU and PLANA,and that of LUBBOCK and DE PoNTECOULANT, although perhaps unsuitable to obtain the accuracy required at the present day, attained results which were at the time as much in advance of those previously published as those of HANsEN and DELAUNAY were over all earlier thcories.

But if we look only to suggestions of methods of treatment, quite apart from the extent to which they were actually carried out, the most fruitful contributions to the frst part of thc problem wcre undoubtedly those of EvLER, who preceded all the writers just mentioned. His two treatises of $^ { 1 7 5 3 }$ and $\mathbf { 1 } 7 7 ^ { 2 }$ contain three distinct mcthods ；and it is not a little remarkable that the thcories of HANsEN and DELAUNAY may be said to be ultimately based on two of them,while the third forms thc foundation of the method developed below. Amongst the points of correspondence betwcen the first theory of EuER and the theoryof HANsEN,it issufficient to note the manner in which the truc longitude and the radius vector of thc Moon are expresscd. HANsEn, however,covered iip most of thc traces of any snch connection by his peculiar method of using a so-caled “ variablc time”and by developing his formule with the aid of the method of variation of arbitrary constants. His formule can be otherwise obtained,as HAxsEn himself and others havc shown;even if it were not so,wc owc thc application of the arbitrary constants method to celcstial mcchanics in tle first place to EvLER. This latter method, which is contained in the appcndix to EuLER's volume of 1753, was rendcred a practical one under thc masterly treatmcnt of DELAUNAY ； but it is doubtful if anyone but the originator of thc method would have had the courage to undertake the laborious calculations ncessary to bring the work to a successful conclusion.

The theory of EuLER, published in 1772, is of particular interest hcre, since it suggested the method used below. It is based on the use of rect-angular axes,of which two movc in their own plane with constant angular velocity,and on a division of the inequalities into classes according to the composition of their coefficicnts. This work of EuLER seems to havc received but little attention,apart from thc practical results obtained ；no attempt was made to develop his method further. It was reserved for Dr. G. W.HiLL,over a hundred years latcr, to take tlic next step by so altcring the forms of the equations,while prescrving the original ideas on which they wcre constructed, that they might bc madc availablc for accurate calculation. His two papcrs,“ Researches in the Lunar Tlcory,” published in tle first volume of the American Journal of Mathematics, and ${ \mathfrak { s } } _ { 0 { \mathfrak { n } } }$ the Part of the Motion of the Lunar Perigee which is a Function of the Mean Motions of the Sun and Moon,” published separatcly,and also in the eighth volume of the Acta Mathematica, showed what the method was capable of effcting, and opened out a new region for practical calculations and theoretical researches.

“ Dans catte euvre,” says M. PoINcARE in thc preface to the first volume of his Mecanique Celeste, “il est permis d'apercevoir le germe de la plupart des progres que la science a faits depuis.”

In this colnection the work of ADAMs must be mentioned. Starting from an entirely different point of view,that of effecting an accurate determination of the mean motions of the principal arguments, he obtained the corresponding part of the motion of the node by a mcthod somewhat similar to that used by Dr. HILL for the perigee. One of the main difficulties in the latter case-the reduction of the equations to a suitable form-does not occur in the case of the node,and there is no indication in the singlc paper which ADAMs published on that subject in the Monthly Notices for 1877 that he contemplated the use of moving rectangular coordinates. But that he Was investigating the properties of the latter may be inferred from another paper on the lunar theory in the same volume of the Monthly Notices.

During the last six years I have been attempting to develop the ideas contained in the “ Researches ” by calculating the coefficients of terms witli certain definite characteristics.\* Dr.HILL had obtained those which had the characteristic nnity, that is, which were functions of the mean motions of the Sun and Moon only,and also that part of the motion of the perigee which was a function of the same quantities ；ADAMs had done the same thing for the motion of the node. It therefore remained to obtain tlie general equations,to put them into forms suitable for calculation,and to show how the other parts of the motions of the perigee and node might be obtained. Experiments were made with the inequalities wlose characteristics are the first,second,and third powers of the ratio of the mean parallaxes of the Sun and Moon,and the same powers of the eccentricity of the Moon. The forms of thc equations which were there used were,however,troublesome, chiefly from thcir liability to produce errors in the actual calculations. In a paper which will bc hereafter referred to,for thc sake of brevity,as the “ Investigations,”+ I showcd how this diffculty might be avoided without, I think,causing any incrcasc in the labour of making the calculations,and it has certainly diminished the actual time required for their performance. In fact, at least three fourths of the calculations might be performed by a computer whose stock-in-trade amountcd to little more than a thorough knowledge of logarithms.An effective control over the computations can be kept at alimost every step ； and as the operations whicl would be turned over to the computer are always the same, he would soon be able to do his work with very little supervision.

It is intended to apply the method so as to completely solve the problem of the Moon's motion as far as it is affected by the Sun and Earth alone, the action of the' Moon only on these bodies being included,and the three bodies being treated as particles of equal masses. The degree of accuracy aimed at is that the coefficients of all periodic terms in longitude,latitude,and parallax shall be includcd which are greater than $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot _ { O I } }$ ,and that they shall be correct to this amount. The number of terms required is undoubtedly very great. The calculation of coefficients up to the sixtl order inclusive with respect to e,k—the lunar eccentricity and inclination-will be necessary ； those of the seventh order may be replaced by their elliptic values. The corresponding orders for $\ell ^ { \prime }$ ， $\pmb { a }$ -the solar ecccntricity and thc ratio of the mean parallaxesare deduced fron the fact that $\ell ^ { ? 2 }$ is roughly of thc order $\mathrm { e ^ { 3 } }$ or $\mathrm { k } ^ { \mathrm { * } }$ and that $^ { a }$ is of the order $\mathrm { e ^ { 2 } }$ or $\mathrm { k ^ { 2 } }$

To obtain the coeficients with the above-mentioned degree of accuracy it will be necessary to calculate terms contained in about one hundred characteristics. These will include about five hundred periodic terms, and will require the actual calculation of perhaps two thousand separate coefficients. The rcsults now published contain the terms present in fourteen characteristics-that is to say, about onc-seventh of the wlolc. Notwithstanding the fact that the number of terms contained in the higher characteristics is mucl greater than that in the lower ones, the work donc so far probably amounts to more than one-fifth of the whole. This is due to the fact that a much higher degree of accuracy is raquired for the lower terins than is actually necessary to obtain the corresponding coefficients correctly to $\mathrm { { \odot } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { { O 1 } } } }$ ; the presence of small divisors causes a loss of accuracy, which has to be continually borne in mind in judging of the number of places of decinals which are to be calculated. For example, the term， the mean moticn of whose argument is twice the diffrence between the mean motions of the perigee and node, requires that the calculations be actually carried three placcs of dccimals further than would be neccssary for a term of the same order with no small divisor.Fortunately, however,thc majority of the terms which cause the most trouble, due to the presencc of small divisors,arc those whieh contain both e,a in their charaeteristics,and therefore the number of eharacteristics of this nature to be considered is much smaller than would otherwise be the case.

The thcory will be an algebraieal one throughout, with the single and important cxception that the numerical value of the ratio of the mean motions of the Sun and Moon is substituted. The reasons for this may be briefly stated as follows :-First, slow convergence of thc series which represcnt the coeffieients arranged according to powers of m or m,\* e, k, d,a, takes plaee mainly along powers of in ； secondly,the valuc of m is known from observation with great aecuracy ；thirdly,estimates would have to be made of the values of the eonstants e,k used in this theory from the values of differently defined constants of eccentricity and latitude used in other theories ； fourthly, wery litle, if any， extra trouble is caused by leavinge, k,e,a arbitrary. Thus the theory,while remaining to a large extent algebraical, will possess all the advantages of a purely numerical theory. It may be also mentioned that,by combining the results of this theory with Dr. HLL's modification of DELAUNAY, it can be effeetively used for researches by the method of the variation of arbitrary constants. The procedure is. intrinsically contained in my paper “On the Theoretieal Values of the Secular Acceleration in the Lunar Theory ” in thc Montlly Notices for March 1897.

Of the four chapters which are now published, the first contains the whole theory，with certain exceptions, so far as it is necessary for the con\* tinuous development of the numerical results. The cxceptions are those parts of the theory which refer to numerical results previously obtained and which arc not necessary for those which follow. For example, all details of purely theoretical interest are omitted,and no account is given of Dr. HiLL's method for the determination of the intermediate orbit used here (which I have called the “ variation curve"),or of tlose methods used by him and ADAMs for finding the principal parts of the motions of the perigee and node. All that is necessary is a quotation of the numerical results,and thcy will be found in their propcr places with the sources from which they have been obtained. The following is thc table of contents :-

Chapter I.General Development of the Theory.

Section (i). An invcstigation of the disturbing function used, with the necessary corrections.

Section (i). The two forms of the equations of motion.

Section (ii). Development of the disturbing fuuction according to powers f $\mathbb { I } / a _ { \mathit { \Pi } } ^ { \prime } , z , e ^ { \prime }$

Section (iv). The forin of the solution. Thc general system of notation adopted to represent the cocfficients, argumcnts, &c.

Section ${ \bf \Xi } ( \bf { v } )$ . Mcthod of solution.Preparation of the equations of motion.

Section (vi). Exact definitions of the arbitrary constants uscd in the theory.

Section (vii). Methods used for the solution of thc equations of condition satisfied by the coeffeients. Thc long and short period terms which givc risc to small divisors. Manner of obtaining thc higher parts of the motions of the perigee and node.

Section (vii). Detaiis concerning the numerical calculations and the methods used to vcrify them.

Section (ix). Transformation to polar coordinates.

Chapter II.-Terms of zero order.\* Numerical results.

Chapter III.-Numerical results for terms of the firstorder.

Chapter IV.-Numerical results for terms of the second order.

Future chapters will contain thc terms of thc third, fourtli ...orders.

With regard to the calculations, no tronblc has been spared to secure correciness. Errors are of two kinds, those which are merely numerieal and those which are partly algebraical-i.e. duc to tle usc of a wrong scries of factors or to the omission of some series of terms.To test thc former, equations of verifieation were computed at evcry step,and the nature of the method rendercd thesc very numerous. An error of the later kind may escape such a verification,and will generally produce a large discordance ; the results were therefore tested bya rough comparison with those of another theory-say, that of DELAUNAY. One searching test of this kind has been applied to the majority of the terms now published,namely,the comparison of the motions of the perigee and node deduced therefrom with those deduced by HANSEN and DELAUNAY.

The results obtained so far point to certain appreciable errors in the theories of HANsEN and DELAUNAY. Dr. HiLL has shown \* that the last two terms of DELAUNAY's expression for the part of the motion of the perigee which depends on m only are wrong. The part of the motion of the perigee which depends on $\mathrm { e ^ { 2 } ~ J }$ have calculated in two quite different ways,and it appears that the last two, if not the last three, of these terms of DE1AUNAY's expression are seriously erroneous.t An crror in DELAUNAY's exprcssion for the part of the motion of the node which depends on $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ was actually traced down to an error of transcription in his theory.t HAysEN's theoretical value for the annual motion of the Iode appears to be at least one if not two seconds in error.§ Professor NEwcoMB in his discussion of the results obtained for the coefficient of parallactic inequality |l considers that HANsEN's value is about $\mathsf { o } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathsf { \cdot } _ { 3 } \mathsf { o }$ in error : this amount, though small in itself,is of importance if the coefficient be used to obtain the solar parallax. I hope before long to finisli the computation of this coefficient so as to obtain it within o"o2,and,at a later period, the values of the annual motions of the perigee and node, so far as these dcpend on solar action only,correct to $\mathrm { o } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { \Omega \mathrm { I } }$ .

# CHAPTER I

GENERAL DEVELOPMENT OF THE THEORY.

Section (i).-The Problem of Three Bodies and the Disturbing Function.

1. The disturbing function Ised by all investigators except HANSEN gives only a portion of the inequalitics produced by the Sun in thc motion of the Moon. This form of the disturbing function will be used below,and therefore we shall, in this section, investigate the small additions which must be made to it in order that the whole of the effect of the Sun on the Moon's motion niay be obtained. The method of this section is similar to that of Prof. NEwcoMB in “ The Actions of the Planets on the Moon ”(Amer. Eph. Papers, vol. v).

Let X, Y, z,r be the coordinates and distance of the Moon referred to axes fixed in direction and passing througl the centre of the Eartl ；α', y', ≥',r, those of tle Sun referred to parallel axes through the centre of mass of the Earth and Moon ；M, E,m', the masses of the Moon, Earth,and Sul reckoned in astronomical units. The x-coordinates of the Moon, Earth, and Sun referred to parallel axes through the centre of mass of the three bodies are respectively

$$
\frac { E X } { E + \mathcal { M } } - \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } + E + \mathcal { M } } , - \frac { \lambda I X } { \bar { E } + \mathcal { M } } - \frac { m ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } + \bar { E } + \mathcal { M } } , \frac { ( E + \mathcal { M } ) \alpha ^ { \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } + E + \mathcal { M } } ,
$$

witl similar expressions for the other coordinates.

Let T be the kinetic energy of the system relative to the centre of mass. Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } &  \quad _ { 2 } T = \mathcal { M } \Big \{ \Big ( \begin{array} { c } { \frac { E } { K + \mathcal { M } } \frac { d X } { d t } - \frac { \omega ^ { \prime } } { w ^ { \prime } } + \frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { t + \mathcal { J } } \frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \dots + \dots + \dots \Big \} } \\ & { \quad + E \Big \{ \Big ( \frac { \mathcal { M } } { E + \mathcal { M } } \frac { d X } { d t } + \frac { \omega ^ { \prime } } { w ^ { \prime } + \mathcal { B } + \mathcal { J } } \frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \dots + \dots + \dots \Big \} } \\ & { \quad + m ^ { \prime } \Big \{ \Big ( \frac { E + \mathcal { M } } { m ^ { \prime } + E + \mathcal { M } } \frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \dots + \dots + \dots \Big \} } \\ & { \quad = \mu _ { 1 } ( \dot { X } ^ { 2 } + \dot { Y } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \bar { z } } ^ { 2 } ) + \mu _ { 2 } ( \ddot { x } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \bar { y } } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \bar { z } } ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { \quad \quad _ { \mu _ { 1 } = \frac { E \mathcal { M } } { \tilde { E } + \mathcal { M } } } , \quad \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { m ^ { \prime } \left( \dot { X } ^ { 2 } + \dot { X } \right) } { m ^ { \prime } + \tilde { E } + \mathcal { M } } . } \end{array} \end{array}
$$

where

Let F be the potential energy of the system. By LAGRANGE's equations we then have for the Moon's motion relative to the Earth-

$$
\mu _ { 1 } { \frac { d ^ { 2 } X } { d t ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial { \dot { X } } } } , \mu _ { 1 } ^ { d 2 } Y  = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial { \dot { Y } } } } , \mu _ { 1 } ^ { d 2 } { \widetilde { d t } } ^ { \prime } = { \widehat { \stackrel { \partial } { \partial { \dot { X } } } } } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and for the Sun's motion relative to the centre of mass of the Earth and Moon-

$$
\mu _ { 2 } \stackrel { d ^ { 2 } x ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \stackrel { \widehat { \sigma } F ^ { \prime } } { \hat { c } } , \mu _ { 2 } \stackrel { d ^ { 2 } y ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \stackrel { \widehat { \sigma } F ^ { \prime } } { \hat { \sigma } y ^ { \prime } } , \mu _ { 2 } \stackrel { \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } z ^ { \prime } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \stackrel { \widehat { c } F ^ { \prime } } { \hat { \sigma } \stackrel { \prime } {  } } ,
$$

$\boldsymbol { F }$ being expressed in terms of $X ; ~ Y ; ~ z , ~ x ^ { \prime } , ~ y ^ { \prime } ; ~ z ^ { \prime } .$

Let r'be the distance of the Sun from the Earth,△ its distancc from the Moon.Then

$$
F { = } \frac { E M } { r } { + } \frac { E m ^ { \prime } } { r _ { 1 } } { + } \frac { \mathcal { M } m ^ { \prime } } { \Delta } ;
$$

and

wherc

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r ^ { 2 } = X ^ { 2 } + Y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } , ~ \displaystyle r ^ { \prime 2 } = \alpha ^ { \prime 2 } + y ^ { \prime 2 } + z ^ { \prime 2 } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { { \displaystyle r _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } ^ { 2 } = r ^ { \prime 2 } + 2 \frac { M } { E + M } r ^ { \prime } r \mathrm { S } + \frac { M ^ { 2 } } { ( E + M ) ^ { 2 } } r ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \Delta ^ { 2 } = r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 \frac { E } { E + M } r ^ { \prime } r \mathrm { S } + \frac { E ^ { 2 } } { ( E + M ) ^ { 2 } } r ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { { \displaystyle r r ^ { \prime } } \mathrm { S } = X \alpha ^ { \prime } + Y y ^ { \prime } + \approx z ^ { \prime } . } } \end{array}
$$

S is therefore the cosine of the angle subtended by △ at the centre of mass of thc Earth and Moon.

2.We shall first consider the effect of the Moon's motion on the Sun and determine the deviation of the Sun's motion from an elliptic orbit in the plane of reference. For this purpose we may omit the first term of F, since it does not contain a', y',z'. Then

$$
\frac { F } { \mu _ { 2 } } { = } \frac { m ^ { \prime } + E + M } { E + M } \Big ( \frac { E } { { r _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } } { + } \frac { M } { \Delta } \Big ) .
$$

Expanding 1/ri', 1/△ in powers of r/r', we obtain

$$
\frac { F } { \mu _ { 2 } } = ( m ^ { \prime } + E + M ) \biggl [ \frac { \mathrm {  ~ I ~ } } { r ^ { \prime } } + \frac { E M } { ( E + M ) ^ { 2 } } \biggr . \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 3 } } \biggr ( \mathrm {  ~ S ~ } ^ { 2 } - \frac { \mathrm {  ~ I ~ } } { 2 } \biggr ) + \ \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggl . \biggr ] .
$$

The order of the second term in comparison with the frst is in the ratio

$$
M r ^ { 2 } : E r ^ { \prime 2 } { = } 1 : 1 2 , 0 0 0 , 0 0 0
$$

approximately. The order of the next term is in the ratio Mr3 : E/， a quantity which may and will be totally neglected. A sufficient correction to the elliptic motion of the Sun about the centre of mass of the Earth and Moon may therefore be obtained by using

$$
\left( m ^ { \prime } + E + M \right) \frac { E M } { ( E + M ) ^ { 2 } } \ \underset { r ^ { 3 } } { \overset { } { \mathop { r } ^ { 2 } } } \left( \underset { 2 } { \overset { } { \operatorname 3 } } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } - \underset { 2 } { \overset { \mathtt { I } } { \operatorname 4 } } \right)
$$

as the disturbing function, and substituting for the coordinates of the Mcon their elliptic values, together with the principal inequalities due to the Srn,

as found in Chaps. II., III. below.If $\mathbf { \Omega } _ { n } \mathbf { \Omega } _ { \prime } ^ { \prime }$ be the observed mean motion of the Sun, we define the mean distance $a ^ { \prime }$ by the equation

$$
m ^ { \prime } + E + M = n ^ { \prime 2 } \alpha ^ { \prime 3 } .
$$

3. Next, for the motion of the Moon we have, on expanding $\boldsymbol { F }$ and rejecting the useless term $m ^ { \prime } ( E + \mathcal { M } ) / \iota ^ { \prime }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \vec { F } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { \vec { E } + \mathcal { M } } { \tau } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } ( \vec { E } + \mathcal { M } ) } { \vec { E } \mathcal { M } \mathcal { L } } \Big ( \frac { \vec { E } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \mathcal { M } } { \Delta } \Big ) } \\ & { \quad = \frac { \vec { E } + \mathcal { M } } { r } + \frac { m ^ { \prime } r ^ { 2 } } { \vec { r } ^ { 3 - } } \Big [ \Big ( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Big ) + \frac { \vec { E } - \mathcal { M } } { E + \mathcal { M } } \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ( \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } \Big ) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { \vec { E } ^ { 2 } - \vec { E } \mathcal { M } + \frac { \mathcal { M } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } } { ( \vec { E } + \mathcal { M } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { \prime 2 } } \Big ( \frac { 3 5 } { 8 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 4 } - \frac { 1 5 } { 4 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } \Big ) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { \vec { E } ^ { 3 } - \vec { E } \mathcal { M } + E \mathcal { M } ^ { 2 } - \mathcal { M } ^ { 3 } } { ( \vec { E } + \mathcal { M } ) ^ { 3 } } \frac { r ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \Big ( \frac { 6 3 } { 8 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 5 } - \frac { 3 5 } { 4 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \mathrm { S } \Big ) + \dots \Big ] . } \end{array}
$$

The expansion is carried as far as will be necessary， since we shall neglect quantities of the orders $r ^ { 4 } / r ^ { \prime 4 } , M \mathrm { { } \mathrm { { } { \cdot } ^ { 3 } / E r ^ { \prime 3 } } }$

The force-function which we shall use is that ordinarily used, namely,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \otimes = \frac { E + M } { r } + m ^ { \prime } \biggl [ \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { r ^ { \prime } ^ { 2 } - 2 r ^ { \prime } \mathrm { S } ^ { - } + r ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { \prime } } - \frac { r \mathrm { S } } { r ^ { \prime } } \biggr ] } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { E + M } { r } + n ^ { \prime } 2 a ^ { \prime } \frac { 3 } { r ^ { \prime 0 } } \biggl [ \biggl ( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \biggr ) + \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime } } \biggl ( \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \biggr ) } } \\ { { + \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \biggl ( \mathrm { S } ^ { 5 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 4 } - \frac { 1 5 } { 4 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } \biggr ) } } \\ { { + \frac { r ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \biggl ( \frac { 6 } { 8 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 5 } { 4 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \biggr ) + \ . . . \biggr ] \qquad \ldots } } \end{array}
$$

In this, the elliptic values of $x ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } \left( z ^ { \prime } { = } \circ \right)$ are substituted and $m ^ { \prime }$ is put cqual to $n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 }$ instead of $n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } - E - M .$ It is necessary to consider what corrections must be made when & is used instead of F/μ.

4. The corrections arc of threc kinds and are so smallthat we need only consider their effects to the first order of the disturbance.

$( a )$ Correction due to putting $n ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime 3 } { = } n \ell ^ { \prime }$ instead of $m ^ { \prime } + E + { \mathcal { M } } .$ We must add to $\mathfrak { Q }$ the terms

- \left( E + \mathcal { M } \right) _ { \vec { r } ^ { \prime 3 } } ^ { r ^ { 2 } } \Big [ { } _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { r } { r ^ { \prime } } \Big ( { } _ { 2 } ^ { 5 } \mathrm { S } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { S } \Big ) \Big ] .

which very frequently bears the name of the“Lunar Theory.” It will be noticed from this investigation that the elliptic values of the solar coordinates to be used are those referred to the centre of mass of the Earth and Moon.

The equations (6), (7), (8) will be called the homogeneous equations. Their left-hand members are homogeneous and of the second degree with respect to u,s,z, while their right-hand members are, abstraction being made of C,of the 2nd, 3rd ... degrees with respect to the same variables.\*

Section (iv).-Form of the Solution. Notation.

Io.Let Vbe the true longitude in the plane of X Yor α y reckoned from the fixed axis of X and \$ the latitude above this plane. Also let D, l, ', F denote the same angles as in Delaunay's theory, Chap. xi. vol. ii. i.e. let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { D } { = } ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) t + \epsilon { - } \epsilon ^ { \prime } { = } \mathrm { H a l f ~ a r g . ~ o f ~ t h e ~ } ^ { \omega \cdot \mathrm { ( } \mathrm { v } \mathrm { r i a t i o n } , \mathrm { ' } } ) } \\ & { l { = } c n t { + } \epsilon { - } ~ { \varpi \mathrm { A r g . ~ o f ~ t h e ~ P r i n c i p a l ~ E l l i p t i c ~ T e r m , } } } \\ & { l ^ { \prime } { = } n ^ { \prime } t { + } \epsilon ^ { \prime } { - } \varpi ^ { \prime } \quad { = } \quad , \quad \qquad \mathrm { , ~ } \quad \qquad \epsilon _ { \mathrm { A n n u a l ~ E q u a t i o n , } } ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \mathrm { F } { = } g n t { + } \epsilon { - } \theta \quad { = } \quad , \quad \qquad \mathrm { , ~ } \quad \mathrm { P r i n c i p a l ~ t e r m ~ i n ~ L a t i t u d e } } \end{array}
$$

Here,as usual, ε, w, θ are the values of the mean longitudes of the Moon and of its perigee and node at time t=O ； e' is the mean longitude of the Sun at time t=o and α' the (constant） longitude of its perigee；(1-c)n, $( \mathfrak { r } - g ) \mathfrak { n }$ are thc mean motions of the perigee and node.

We then have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { X = \rho \cos \ V , \quad Y = \rho \sin \ V , \quad \approx = \rho \tan \varphi = r \sin \phi , } \end{array}
$$

Whenee, as we shal put ζ = exp. Dt, (§ 11),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x = \rho \cos { ( V - n ^ { \prime } t - \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) } = \rho \cos { ( V - n t - \epsilon + \mathrm { D } ) } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle y = \rho \sin { ( V - n ^ { \prime } t - \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) } = \rho \sin { ( V - n t - \epsilon + \mathrm { D } ) } } ; } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { w } & { = \rho \exp . \left( V - n t - \epsilon + \mathrm { D } \right) \iota } & & { s = \rho \exp . - \left( V - n t - \epsilon + \mathrm { D } \right) \iota } \\ & { u \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \rho \exp . \left( V - n t - \epsilon \right) \iota , } & & { s \zeta = \rho \exp . - \left( V - n t - \epsilon \right) \iota } \end{array} \biggr \} \begin{array} { r l } & { . . . } \end{array}
$$

It is well known that, with the limitations here imposed,r. V- nt-e,Φ are expressible by sums of periodie terms whose arguments are (algebraic) suns of multiples of the four angles D,l, l, F. Hence r, y, ε are expressible in the forms

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { \boldsymbol { x } } \\ { \boldsymbol { y } } \\ { \mathrm { \scriptsize { : = a \beth } } } \end{array} \left( \boldsymbol { i } \mathrm { D } + \boldsymbol { \bar { \rho } } \boldsymbol { l } + \boldsymbol { r } \boldsymbol { l } ^ { \prime } + \boldsymbol { q } \mathrm { F } \right) , \qquad \begin{array} { c c } { \mathrm { \boldsymbol { i } } , \boldsymbol { \bar { p } } , \boldsymbol { \eta } , \boldsymbol { r } = \boldsymbol { \circ } , \boldsymbol { \pm } \mathrm { \bf { r } } , \boldsymbol { \pm } 2 , \mathrm { \bf { \cdot } } \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \end{array}
$$

where a is a linear constant and A a coefficient ; the sign of summation £ denotes that the sum of all sueh terms must be taken. Remembering the definition of u, s, it is evident that u, s, cl may be therefore expressed in the form

$$
^ { \prime \ell , ~ s , ~ \hat { \times } \iota = \mathrm { a } \Sigma \boldsymbol { A } _ { i , p , \ell , r } \mathrm { e x p . } \mathrm { ( } i \mathrm { D } + p \mathrm { ' } + r \ell \mathrm { + } q \mathrm { F ) } \mathrm { ~ } \iota , }
$$

with certain limitations which wil be set down later.

There are four sets of notations required. First, for the cxponentials ; sceondly, for the constants of distanee, eceentrieity, latitude and ratio of the mean parallaxes ； thirdly, for the numerieal parts of the coefficients (therc parts are functions of m only,and the numerical value of m is used thrcnght); fourthly, for the terms of 1, s, & which are of a particular orler.

11. Notation for the Exponentials.-Recalling that m=n' / (n--n'), we may evidently write

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { D } { = } ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) \ ( t { - } t _ { 0 } ) , } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } l { = } \mathrm { c } \ ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) \ ( t { - } t _ { 1 } ) , } \\ & { l { = } \mathrm { m } \ ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) \ ( t { - } t _ { 3 } ) , } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ } \mathrm { F } { = } \mathrm { g } \ ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) \ ( t { - } t _ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\mathrm { ~ c ~ } ( n - n ^ { \prime } ) { = } c n , \qquad \mathrm { ~ g ~ } ( n { - } n ^ { \prime } ) { = } g n ,
$$

and the signifieation of $t _ { 0 } , ~ t _ { 1 } , ~ t _ { 2 } , ~ t _ { 3 }$ is obvious.

We have, in Seetion (ii),defined $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ by the equation

$$
\zeta { = } { \exp } . \left( n { - } n ^ { \prime } \right) \left( t { - } t _ { 0 } \right) \iota ,
$$

$$

$$

$$

where i, j, p are positive or negative integers.

Now, in the method pursued here, we shall always proeeed by equating to zero the eoefficients of like powers of &, & in equations which consist of .sueh expressions as that just written down, and it will never be neeessary to substitute the value of c in the indices ； its value is only substituted in the eoefficients. The above equation shows that the coeffieients will be the same whether we write $\zeta ^ { i } \zeta _ { \mathrm { { c } } } ^ { p \mathrm { { c } } }$ ，or $\zeta ^ { i + p \mathrm { { c } } }$ Further,as te suffix c always oeeurs in the index whenever it is present as a sufix, the suffix is unnecessary for purposes of distinction and we shall omit it in future. The same remarks apply to

$$

$$

We may therefore put

$$
\exp . ~ ( \downarrow \mathrm { D } + p l + r l ^ { \prime } + q \mathrm { F } ) \imath { = } \zeta ^ { i - p { \sigma } + r \mathrm { m } + 4 \updownarrow } ,
$$

12. Notation for the Arbitrary Constants and the Parameters.-There are six arbitrary constants present in the solution. Three of these-ε, w, 0,or to,t,t—have already been considered ；they are contained in D,l,F,or in , &,&g.The other arbitraries to be used will be denoted by

$$
n , { \mathrm { \bf ~ a } } , { \mathrm { \bf ~ e } } , { \mathrm { \bf ~ k } } ,
$$

connected by one relation. The exact definitions of e, k,-the constants of cccentricity and inclination-will be found in Section (vi) ； n has bcen defined as the observed mean motion ;the linear constant, a,is connected with n,e,k, E+M by a relation which will be defined in the same Section. In elliptic motion, this relation would be n²a=E+M；in thc actual case the relation differs a little from this.

The parameters in powers of which expansion will be made are

$$
\mathrm { m } , \mathrm { e } , e ^ { \prime } , \mathrm { k } , { a } = _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { \mathrm { a } } .
$$

The numerical value of m is used, but thc other parameters are left arbitrarv.

15. Characteristic and Order.The factor

$$
\mathrm { e } ^ { p + 2 p ^ { \prime } } e ^ { \prime r + 2 r ^ { \prime } } \mathrm { k } ^ { q + 2 q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { s ^ { \prime } }
$$

of thc general coefficient willbe called the characteristic part of the coeficient or, briefly, the characteristic.

The Order is the sum of the indices of ${ \mathrm { e } } , e ^ { \prime } , { \mathrm { k } } , a$ The order of the gcneral term is therefore

$$
p + { \sf z } p ^ { \prime } + r + { \sf z } r ^ { \prime } + q + { \sf z } q ^ { \prime } + \delta ^ { \prime } .
$$

The word “ order,” as uscd here, is thus independent of m-a necessary restriction, since the numerical value of m is substituted at the outset.

A few remarks and an example may make the notation laid down in S 14 clearer. It will be observcd that

$\epsilon ^ { \prime }$ are always associated with e ;   
3， $\eta ^ { \prime }$ are always associated with $e ^ { \prime }$ ；   
$k , k ^ { \prime }$ are always associated with $\mathbf { k }$ ；   
$\mathfrak { a }$ is always associated with $a = \mathbf { a } / a ^ { \prime }$ ；

while, as is well known, e is associated with the index c; $e ^ { \prime }$ with the index m； $\mathrm { k }$ with the index $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { \Sigma } } ^ { \mathrm { g } }$ ； and an odd power of $_ { \pmb { \alpha } }$ with an odd value of 2i.

If the numerical value of m had not been substituted at the outsct, we could further have denoted the particular power of m involved by inserting m insidc ()； the coefficient ()， would then bave been a positive power series in m, with a numerical factor for each power of m, which factor is always the ratio of two integers.

The actual arrangement of e+",e,...inside() is immaterial, but we shall, in general, rctain the arrangementof $\ S \ S _ { \ F }$

Eeample.-The numerical part of the coefficient of g8+2m-2g which has the characteristic $\ell ^ { ' 4 } \mathbb { k } ^ { 2 } \alpha$ in $\ell \ell \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ is denoted by

$$
( \eta ^ { 3 } \eta ^ { \prime } k ^ { \prime 2 } a ) _ { \scriptscriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } .
$$

For here,P=O,1/=O； r=2,r'=1 ；q=2, q=o with the indices of k,/' interchanged ; $2 i = 3 \ ; \quad \cdot ^ { \prime } = \mathbb { I }$ The series of terms in $\eta / \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ which will be found along with this term. are those of the same characteristic which are

obtained by putting $\zeta ^ { - 1 }$ for $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ and whose indices differ from the given index of $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ by even whole numbers.See $\ S 2 7$ below.They are

$$
\mathtt { a } e ^ { \prime 4 } \mathtt { k } ^ { 2 } a \mathtt { \le } _ { i _ { \mathrm { i } } } [ ( \eta ^ { 3 } \eta ^ { \prime } \bar { k } ^ { \prime \mathbf { 2 } } a ) _ { i _ { \mathrm { i } } } \zeta ^ { 2 i _ { \mathrm { i } } + 2 \mathrm { m } - \mathrm { \frac { 2 e } { \mathrm { e } } } } + ( \eta \eta ^ { \prime 3 } \bar { k } ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } a ) _ { i _ { \mathrm { i } } } \zeta ^ { \mathrm { 2 } i _ { \mathrm { 1 } } - \mathrm { 2 } \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { 2 g } } ] ,
$$

whcrc

$$
{ \bf \tau } _ { 2 } i _ { 1 } = \pm { \bf { r } } , { \bf { \tau } } \pm { \bf { \it { 3 } } } , { \bf { \tau } } \pm { \bf { \it { 5 } } } , { \bf { \tau } } \cdot { \bf { \sigma } } \cdot { \bf { \sigma } }
$$

16. Notation for terms with a given Characteristic or of a given Order.- It will frequently be convenient to specify such terms in a brief manncr ; this may be done by means of a suffix attached to $u , s , z$ Thus all terms in u with characteristic e² may be denoted by ue, those with characteristic $\mathit { e } ^ { \prime } \mathit { a }$ by $u _ { e ^ { \prime } a } ,$ and generally, those with characteristic $\lambda$ by $u _ { \mathsf { A } _ { \mathsf { + } } }$

The terms of a givcn order are denoted by numerical suffixes. Thus, $u _ { 3 }$ denotes all terms of the third order ； $u _ { 0 } .$ ，those of zero order ；and so on.

Section $\left( \mathbf { v } \right)$ .Method of Solution. Preparation of the Equations.

17.The general feature of tlc method consists in the successive determination of the terms of orders O, 1,2，.：.with respect to powers and products of e, e', k, a in the coordinatcs u,z. As will be seen from the rcsults of Section (ii),therc are two methods of procedure-one by the use of the equations (3),(4),and the other by the use of the homogeneous equations (6),(7),(8). At the same time it may be stated that we need by no means confine ourselves to either of the two sets of equations,but use one or the other or both as may seem most convenient for the particular elass of inequalities under consideration at any stage of the approximations.

18.Terms of Order Zero.-These terms,the coefficients of which are functions of m only,constitute a closed orbit with reference to thc moving axes which is really the primary or“ intermediatc”orbit in the same sense as the elliptic orbit of the older thcories ；it may be called the Variatiom Curve since its principal periodic term is known as the “ Variation.” According to the notation of the last Section it is given by

$$
u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \Omega \Sigma \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i } , \qquad s _ { 0 } \zeta = \mathrm { a } \Sigma \alpha _ { - i } \zeta ^ { 2 i } . \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad 
$$

These values $u _ { 0 } , \ s _ { 0 }$ of $u , ~ s$ constitute $\mathbf { a }$ particular solution of thc equation

$$
( D + \mathrm { m } ) ^ { 2 } u + \frac { \mathrm { I } } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } u + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } s - \frac { \kappa u } { \rho ^ { 3 } } = \circ \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

Or, in the homogeneous form,of thc equations,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { D ^ { \circ } ( u s ) - D u \cdot D s - 2 \mathsf { m } ( u D s - s D u ) + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \mathsf { m } ^ { 2 } ( u + s ) ^ { 2 } = C ^ { \prime } \ \bigg \} } & { \qquad \ldots } \\ { D ( u D s - s D u - \mathsf { z m } u s ) + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathsf { m } ^ { 2 } ( u ^ { 2 } - s ^ { 2 } ) = \mathsf { o } } & { \bigg \} } & { \qquad \ldots } \end{array}
$$

since here, $\ d ^ { 3 } \cdot = 0$ ， $z = 0$ The constant $\boldsymbol { \mathcal { C } ^ { \prime } }$ is a function of a, m, while a is a function of $E + M _ { ☉ }$ ， $n$ ,m.

The values of $u , s$ being substituted in the equations of either form (by preference,the latter),we obtain a number of cquations of condition which suffice to determine $\mathbf { a } a _ { i }$ .In consequence of the presence of a, one of the $a _ { i }$ is arbitrary; we put ${ a } _ { 0 } = \mathbf { I }$ .The whole theory of these terms and the numerical results have been fully worked out by Dr. HILL in his paper“ Researehes in the Lunar Theory”(Amer. Jour. Math. vol.i.)； the results will be given in Clap. II.

The right-hand members of the cquations thus consist entirely of known terms.Putting $w = u _ { 0 } + \mathfrak { { a } } \mathfrak { { a } } _ { 1 } , \ s = { s } _ { 0 } + \mathfrak { { s } } _ { 1 } , \ z = { z } _ { 1 } ,$ and expanding thc last terms of the left-hand members,it is easily seen that the equations (3), (4） for $\mathcal { U } _ { 1 } , ~ \mathcal { Z } _ { 1 }$ become

where, putting $\rho _ { \downarrow } ^ { \ 2 } { = } \wr _ { 0 } \mathrm { \stackrel { \circ } { \cdot } } { _ \downarrow }$

$$

In each case the appropriate expres-sions for u1, s1, z1, are substituted,and cquations of condition for the unknown coefficicnts are obtained by equating the coefficients of the varions powers of § to zero ； they are then solvcd by continucd approximation.

In the equation for $\boldsymbol { u } _ { \mathrm { e } } ,$ the questions of the arbitrary constant of eccentricity and of the motion of the perigee arise,and in that for zk, the arbitrary constant of latitude and the miotion of the node ； these will bc dealt with in their proper place $( \ S \ S _ { 2 5 } , 2 6 )$ ·

The homogeneous forms of the equations may be considered in a similar manner. I do not give the developments here as they may be easily inferred from the more general treatment in § 22 of this Section. Further information is given in chapter xi. of the“ Treatise on the Lunar Theory”and in thc papcrs on the Parallactic and Elliptic Incqualities (Amer. Jour. Math. vols. xiv., xv.).

The numerical results for the terms of the first order are contained in Chapter III below.

20. Terms of the Second and Higher Orders. 21. Some remarks on these cquations are necessary. In the first place, the left-hand members of(3), (4） being linear with respect to u, s,and to z respectively (exception being made of the terins containing κ； these will be considered immediately), tle parts uo+∑uμ of u and ∑zμ of z cannot contribute anything to terms with characteristic X as far as the coeyficients of these periodic terms are concerncd. But thc operators D²,D cause tle coeffcients in uu,zμ to be respectively multiplied by factors of the forms

$$
( 2 i + p \mathrm { c } + r \mathrm { m } + q \mathrm { g } ) ^ { 2 } , \qquad ( 2 i + p \mathrm { c } + r \mathrm { m } + q \mathrm { g } ) ,
$$

and c,g contain powers and products of e²,e², k²,a2. Hence it will be necessary in some of thc terms, whose orders are ligher than the second, to include $D ^ { 2 } u _ { \mu } , D u _ { \mu } , D ^ { 2 } z _ { \mu }$ in tle equations.

In general,all the unknowns are contained in the left-hand members of (17）or (17') and (18) or (18'),while the terms of tle right-hand members are entirely known. Exception to the last statement only holds when we are determining an unknown part of c or g. It must be remembered that, in reality,c and g are supposed to represent the complete values at thc outset, but that, in forming the cquations of condition, all terms of higher orders in the valucs of c, g, than those actually undcr consideration are neglccted. Hence an unknown part of c or g will, in certain cases,arisc from the terms containing the operator D in the right-hand mcmbers. Thcse cases are more fully considcred in the next Section.

As to the terms involving &, since & is of the first order at lcast, apart from the order of the terms in u, we can cvidently sbstitute uo+∑u for u and $\boldsymbol { \Sigma } _ { \boldsymbol { z } _ { \mu } }$ for $z$ therein.

Therestofthesisfsisf $\frac { \kappa u \zeta ^ { - 1 } } { \eta ^ { 3 } } , \frac { \kappa z } { \eta ^ { 3 } }$ in powers of ∑up， Σεx.Those containing the first powers of these quantities arc omitted, since they evidently cannot producc terms with the characteristic λ. It will be noticed that the terms are written in two ways,(17),(18) and $\left( \mathrm { ~ I ~ } 7 ^ { \prime } \right) , \left( \mathrm { ~ I ~ } 8 ^ { \prime } \right)$

If we take thc frst forms,namely(17),(i8),for calculation,the process is to calcnlate each ∑u/uo, Σzμ/po (which consists in a single easy multiplication of each uγ-1 by so&/p² and of cach εμ by 1/po), to form the various products inside the parenthescs,and finally to multiply tle resulting series by kuo%-1/p and κ/po² in the respectivc cquations. For this purpose the values of

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathtt { a } \underbrace { \ s _ { 0 } \zeta } _ { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { ( 2 ) } } } , \quad \quad \frac { \mathrm { a } } { \rho _ { 0 } } , \quad \quad \frac { \kappa u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } { { \mathrm { a } } \rho _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { ( 3 ) } } } , \quad \frac { \kappa } { { \mathrm { a } } \rho _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { ( 2 ) } } } , } \end{array}
$$

expressed as even-power series in & are given at thc end of Chapter II.

If we takc the second forms, namely,(17'), (18'), the various multiplications which have to bc made arc evident,and the values of P, Q, . . . ncccssary for this, cxpressed as power serics in &, are givcn in the same place. There can be litlc doubt that the first forms give sliorter calculations, and they havc a further advantage in the fact that tle only trouble nessary for transferring to polar coordinates consists in the calculations of powers of ∑uμ/uo, ∑zμ/po (see Section ix),and this labour will thercfore have already been finishcd. Indced, the second forms would not have been given here at all,werc it not for the fact that I failed to scc thc great advantage of the first forms for the higher inequalities, and conscqucntly used the sccond forms in the calculation of all the second order inequalities and of a few of the third order.\*

A great advantage of these equations is that the chief labour-the multiplication of series-can be easily arranged for an ordinary computer, and much of tle merely mechanical labour may thereby be distributed (see Sect. vii).

The numerical results for terms of the second order are given in Chapter IV.below ； those for terms of the third and higher orders will be given in chapters to be published hereafter.

22. First and Higher Order Terms. Homogeneous Equations.-The separation of the homogeneous equations into known and unknown parts is effected in a similar manner. The substitutions for u,z are the same as in §2o. If the homogeneous equations be used for actually finding the coefficients, the forms obtained in equations (2o),(21),(22)are of no assistance, except as a guide； this will be evident by a glance at §§ 32-36. They have been，however,almost exclusively used for verifying the results obtained from the equations of § 2o,and they are given mainly for that purpose here.

They are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } &  \qquad \mathcal { D } ^ { \nu } ( \nu _ { ( s \theta _ { s } , \mathbf { k } _ { s } + \mathbf { \lambda } _ { s } , \mathbf { k } _ { s } ] } , - \mathcal { D } \nu _ { \mathrm { s } _ { s } } , \mathcal { D } \nu _ { \mathrm { s } _ { s } - \mathbf { \lambda } _ { s } , 2 n _ { 1 } } | \mathcal { D } \nu _ { s , n _ { - } - 2 n _ { 1 } } ( \nu _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \nu _ { \mathrm { s } _ { s } - \mathbf { \lambda } _ { s } , 0 } \mathcal { D } \nu _ { \mathrm { s } _ { s } + \mathbf { \lambda } _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \nu _ { \mathrm { s } _ { s } - \mathbf { \lambda } _ { s } , 2 n _ { 1 } } ) } \\ & { \qquad \quad + \frac { 2 } { \mathcal { D } \nu _ { s } ^ { 2 } ( \nu _ { s } ) } ( \nu _ { s \theta _ { s } } + \epsilon _ { s } ) } \\ & { = \mathrm { P a r t , ~ } \mathrm { d a r t } \cdot \lambda _ { s } \ln \left[ C ^ { \nu } - \frac { 5 } { \lambda _ { s } + \sigma _ { s } } ( 2 + 1 ) m _ { + } + \mathcal { D } ^ { \nu } \cdot \mathcal { D } \nu _ { s } \right] } \\ &  \quad - \left\{ \mathcal { D } ^ { \nu } ( \Sigma \nu _ { s } , \ \Sigma _ { s } ) - \mathcal { D } \Sigma \nu _ { s } , \quad \mathcal { D } \Sigma \nu _ { s } - 2 \ln ( \Sigma \nu _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \Sigma _ { s } , - \Sigma \nu _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \Sigma \mathbf { \nu } _ { s } ) + \frac { 9 } { 4 \pi } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } ( \Sigma _ { s } \alpha _ { s } + \Sigma _ { s } ) \cdot \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n _ { 1 } } \dots \dots \dots \dots \quad ( 2 \pi ^ { \lambda } } \\ & { \qquad \quad - \left\{ \mathcal { D } \Sigma \nu _ { s } \right\} ^ { - 3 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } m _ { + } ^ { \nu } ( \Sigma \nu _ { s } ) \right\} \Bigg ] } \\ &  \qquad \mathcal { D } ( \nu _ { s } , \Sigma _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \nu _ { s } , + \nu _ { s } , \mathcal { D } \nu _ { s } , - 2 m _ { + } \nu _ { s } - 2 \ln ( \nu _ { s } \mathcal { R } \nu _ { s } ) +  \end{array}
$$

\*From the remarks just made,it might have seemed an improvement to put u=uov, \$=sou,%=pow and to find c,v,w only. This, however,only throws some of the labour of forming u/uo,etc.,on to the solution of the equations of condition；the latter process is far less mechanical than the former and much more liable to error,and there will be no saving of labour.

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \qquad \mathcal { D } ( u _ { 0 } \mathcal { D } _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { h } } - \tilde { \alpha } _ { \lambda } D u _ { 0 } ) - 2 \mathrm { m } z _ { \lambda } \mathcal { D } u _ { 0 } - \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } u _ { 0 } \tilde { z } _ { \lambda } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \tilde { z } _ { \lambda } ( u _ { 0 } + s _ { 0 } ) } \\ & { = \mathrm { P a r t , ~ c h a r e } ^ { c } \cdot \lambda , \ \mathrm { i n } \ \bigg [ \frac { \gamma } { c } \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial s } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } u \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial z } } \\ & { \qquad - \left\{ D ( \Sigma u _ { \mu } \cdot \ D \Sigma z _ { \mu } - \Sigma z _ { \mu } \cdot D \Sigma u _ { \mu } ) - 2 \mathrm { m } \Sigma z _ { \mu } \cdot D \Sigma u _ { \mu } - \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \Sigma u _ { \mu } \cdot \Sigma s _ { \mu } \cdot \right. \left. \Sigma s _ { \mu } \right. } \\ & { \qquad \left. \qquad - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 } \Sigma z _ { \mu } ( \Sigma u _ { \mu } + \Sigma s _ { \mu } ) \right\} \bigg ] \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots } \end{array}
$$

As before, the left-hand members contain the unknown terms.The riglt-hand members consist entirely of klown terms, except when new parts of the motions of perigee or node are under consideration. In certain cases it will be necessary to suppose $u _ { 0 } , \ s _ { 0 }$ to be included in $\Sigma _ { \boldsymbol { u } _ { \boldsymbol { \mu } } }$ ， $\Sigma _ { \ l } s _ { \mu \ l }$ ； see tle remarks at the beginning of $\ S \ O _ { 2 } $ .In the terms involving $_ { \odot 1 } ^ { \prime \phantom { + } }$ ； we may substitute $u _ { 0 } + \Sigma _ { } u _ { \mu }$ ， $\Sigma _ { \mathfrak { s } _ { \mu } } ^ { \mathfrak { s } _ { z } }$ for $u , \ z ,$ respectively ； the method by wlich the calculations are to be actually performed will be given in Section (vii).

In the case of the frst order terms, $\begin{array} { r l } { \sum _ { \ell \ell _ { \mu } , } ^ { \prime } } & { { } \sum _ { \ell \ell _ { \mu } } ^ { \prime } } \end{array}$ are both zero,and tle limitations of $\ S _ { 1 }$ are the same as those given in $\ S { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } 9$

# Section (vi). Definitions of the Arbitrary Constants.

23. Of the six arbitrary constants of the solution, three have already been defined, namely,the three angular constants in the arguments D, l, F. $\mathrm { \bf A }$ fourth, $\mathscr { n }$ ,has also been defined as the observed mean motion. It remains to give an exact definition to a, the linear constant (which replaces the mass $E + M )$ ,and to e,k, the constants of eccentricity and inclination.

$^ { 2 4 }$ . Definition of a.-In elliptic motion, a is defined by means of the relation $n ^ { 2 } \mathrm { a } ^ { 3 } { = } E + M .$ ，For many purposes tlis is much the most convenient definition even when we proceed to detcrmine the solar inequalities in the lunar theory. But in the theory developed here,the calculations may be materially shortened by a different definition of a.

The value of $u _ { o }$ is given by

$$
u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \tt a \underline { { S } } _ { i } a _ { i } \zeta ^ { \geq i } , \qquad i = \tt o , \pm \mathrm { { r } , \ \pm \mathrm { { 2 } } , \ . \ . \ . ~ } .
$$

From the form of this it is evident that either a or $\boldsymbol { \mathcal { I } } _ { 0 }$ inay be chosen to be anything we wish.The most convenient definition is obtained by puting

$$
\boldsymbol { a } _ { 0 } = \boldsymbol { \mathtt { I } } ,
$$

so that

$$
\mathrm { a } { = } \Big ( { \overset { E { + } M } { \underset { \pi ^ { 2 } } { \longrightarrow } } } \Big ) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } f ( { \mathrm { m } } ) \ \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

where $f ^ { \prime } ( \mathfrak { m } )$ is a funetion of m whieh,in the ease of the Moon, is very nearly unity.

The definition must now be extended so as to eover the case when inequalities of any order are being eonsidered. The general form of all the inequalities whieh have arguments of the form $\zeta ^ { 2 i }$ (2i even),are given by $\bigl ( \ S \_ { 4 } \bigr )$

$$
u \zeta ^ { - 1 } { = } \mathrm { a } \Sigma _ { i } [ a _ { i } + \Sigma ( \epsilon ^ { p ^ { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime } { \bf p } ^ { \prime } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } k ^ { q ^ { \prime } } k ^ { \prime q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } ) _ { i } \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 p ^ { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 r ^ { \prime } } { \bf k } ^ { 2 q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } ] \zeta ^ { 2 i }
$$

where

$$
i = 0 , ~ \pm { \bf r } , ~ \pm _ { 2 } , ~ , ~ . ~ . ~ ; ~ p ^ { \prime } , ~ q ^ { \prime } , ~ \ q ^ { \prime } , ~ s ^ { \prime } = 0 , ~ { \bf r } , ~ 2 , ~ . ~ . ~ . ~ ( \mathrm { e x c e p t } ~ p ^ { \prime } = q ^ { \prime } = r ^ { \prime } = s ^ { \prime } = 0 ) ;
$$

and $\pmb { \Sigma } ( \mathbf { \Sigma } ) _ { i }$ denotes the sum of all sueh terms for the values of $p ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { \mathcal { q } } ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime } , \boldsymbol { \mathcal { s } } ^ { \prime }$ given. The eoeffieient of $\zeta ^ { 0 } \mathrm { ~ i n ~ } u \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ ,by means of which $\mathbf { a }$ is to be defined, is therefore, sinee $ { \mathcal { A } _ { \mathrm { o } } } = 1$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { a } \big [ \mathrm { \mathbf { I } } + \Sigma \big ( \epsilon ^ { p ^ { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime p ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { r ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } \mathring { k } ^ { q ^ { \prime } } \mathring { k } ^ { \prime q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } \big ) \mathrm { 0 } \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 p ^ { \prime } } e ^ { t 2 r ^ { \prime } } \big \mathbf { k } ^ { 2 q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } \big ] , } \\ & { \mathrm { ~ \ } = \mathrm { a } ( \mathrm { \mathbf { I } } + \nu \big ) , \qquad \mathrm { s u p p o s e } , } \end{array}
$$

so that $\nu$ is a small quantity of the seeond order at least.

There are two praetieal methods of defining $\mathbf { a }$ ,eaeh of whieh has its use aeeording to the equations we employ.

One of these is to so define a that every term in $\nu$ is zero,and therefore that the coeffieient of $\zeta ^ { 0 }$ is represented by a at every stage of the approximations. This definition requires the determination of some additional terms to a whenever we are finding inequalities of the form $\zeta ^ { 2 i }$ If we are using the homogeneous equations,this -is undoubtedly the best definition, for then the determination of the additional terms in a can be left till the end of the work, and as a only appears in the parallax and not in the longitude and latitude, a very great degree of aceuraey in its value is not required. In using the equations(3),(4), however, it would eause ineonvenience as we should then have to find a further approximation to a at eaeh step. As the latter equations are those mainly used lere, we shall adopt another definition better adapted to the ealeulations.

This seeond definition (whieh we shall use below) is to give to a the meaning whieh it reeeives from the intermediate orbit only and to retain it throughout. Thus a is defned by the cquation (23) making $\mathbf { a } \left( ( E + \mathcal { I } I ) / n ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - { \frac { 1 } { \prime } } }$ a numerical constant whieh never alters.The coeffcients $\nu$ are nou no longer zero,but are definite functions of $\mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } , \mathrm { e } ^ { \prime 2 } , \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } , \mathrm { \Delta } a ^ { 2 } ;$ ,being determined along with the other coeffcients of g in the ordinary way. This definition is necessary beause of the want of homogeneity of the equations (3), (4).\*In finding the parallax from the value of $u$ ,all that will be neecssary will be to find $\mathrm { a } / r$ and thcn to multiply all the terms by a numerical quantity (whieh approaehes unity very elosely) in order to obtain $[ ( E + \mathcal { M } ) / n ^ { 2 } ] ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } / r$ the quantity usually obtained by lunar theorists.

Hence,the linear constant a is defined to le the coefficient of $\zeta ^ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ in $u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } ,$ uhere ${ \boldsymbol { \mathcal { U } } } _ { 0 } { \boldsymbol { \zeta } } ^ { - 1 }$ represents the intermediate orbit or variation curve only. Its value is given in Chap.II.,and it retains this value throughout the whole of the approximations.

25. Definition of e. The first of equations (13) for the determination of the inequalities depending on the first power of eis, since $\varLambda { = } 0$

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathrm { m } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \mathrm { e } } + \mathcal { M } u _ { \mathrm { e } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal { N } s _ { \mathrm { e } } \zeta = \circ ,

u _ { 6 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \tt a e \Sigma _ { i } ( \epsilon _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \tt c } + \epsilon ^ { \prime } _ { i } \zeta ^ { 3 i - \tt c } ) , \qquad i = \circ , \pm \alpha _ { 1 } , \pm \it { 2 } , \ldots .
$$

When the substitution is made, and the coefficients of the various powers of $\zeta ^ { 2 i \pm \mathrm { c } }$ equatcd to zero, we obtain a series of equations of condition for the dctermination of the unknowns εi, é', e, wlieh.are homogeneous and of the first degree with respeet to $\epsilon _ { i } , \epsilon _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ The determination of e is made by considering thc necessary relation which must exist between these equations ； it is actually found by a different method (see  28 (b)),and we suppose it known. One of the eei, ee'i, is arbitrary. The values of εi, ε'𝑖 may all be made definite by taking e as the arbitrary constant and putting

$$
\epsilon _ { 0 } - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \mathtt { I } .
$$

The coefficients of $\zeta ^ { \mathrm { c } } , \ \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { c } } \ \mathrm { i n } \ u \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ are $\mathrm { a e } \epsilon _ { 0 } , \mathrm { a e } \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ ,respectively.Since, by equations (9）,

$$
u \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \rho \cos { \left( V - n t - \epsilon \right) } + \upsilon \sin { \left( V - n t - \epsilon \right) } ,
$$

the coefficient ofa sin lin p sin (V-nt-e) will be \*

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { e } ( \epsilon _ { 0 } - \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) { = } \mathrm { e } , } \end{array}
$$

by the use of the assumed relation. The value of e thus defined is very nearly twice the constant of eccentricity used by DELAUNAY.

The general form of all the terms in $u \pmb { \zeta } ^ { - 1 }$ ,which involve $\zeta ^ { \tt c } , \zeta ^ { - \tt c }$ is $\left( \ S \textbf { \ i } 4 \right)$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { a e } \Big [ \sum _ { p ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } , r ^ { \prime } , s ^ { \prime } } \{ \big ( \epsilon ^ { 1 + p ^ { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime p ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { r ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } k ^ { q ^ { \prime } } k ^ { \prime q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } \big ) _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { \circ } } \\ & { \qquad + \big ( \epsilon ^ { p ^ { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime 1 + p ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { r ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } k ^ { q ^ { \prime } } k ^ { \prime \prime } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } \big ) _ { 0 } \zeta \cdot ^ { \circ } { \mathrm { e } } _ { ; } ^ { 0 } e ^ { 2 p ^ { \prime } } e ^ { \prime 2 r ^ { \prime } } { \mathrm { k } } ^ { 2 q ^ { \prime } } a ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } \Big ] . } \end{array}
$$

The dcfinition of e must be extended so as to cover all these terms.It has been found most convenient to define it to be such that the coefficients of ${ \pmb \zeta } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ and $\zeta ^ { - \infty }$ in the above expression are equal, except when $p ^ { \prime } { = } q ^ { \prime } { = } r ^ { \prime } { = } s ^ { \prime } { = } 0 ,$ when we have already defined it by making their difference unity. Denoting,for a moment,each of thesc equal coefficients by $\beta$ ，so that tle terms containing $\zeta ^ { \tt c } , \zeta ^ { - \tt c }$ in $u \pmb { \zeta } ^ { - 1 }$ are given by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { a e } ( \epsilon _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { \mathrm { c } } + \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { c } } ) + \mathrm { a e } \beta ( \zeta ^ { \mathrm { c } } + \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { c } } ) , } \end{array}
$$

it is evident that the coeffcient of a sin $\it l$ in $\rho$ sin $( \mathbf { \partial } ^ { \aa } \mathbf { l } ^ { \prime } - \varkappa t - \epsilon )$ will be $\mathrm { e } ( \epsilon _ { \mathrm { 0 } } - \epsilon _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ^ { \prime } )$ $= \mathrm { e }$

Hence the constant of eccentricity e is defined to be the coefficient of a sin $\it l$ in the final expression of $\rho$ sin $( V { - } n t { - } \epsilon )$ as a sum of periodic terms, where $V { - } n t { - } \epsilon$ is the difference of the true and mean longitudes and $\rho$ is the projection of the Moon's radius vector on the plane of reference.

and the definition of k must be extended so as to cover all these terms.

( \epsilon ^ { i \bar { \prime } } \epsilon ^ { \prime p ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime ^ { \prime } } \eta ^ { \prime _ { 1 } } { } ^ { \prime } k ^ { 1 + q ^ { \prime } } \bar { k } ^ { \prime q ^ { \prime } } \alpha ^ { 2 s ^ { \prime } } ) _ { 0 } = \circ ,

for all values of p', 4/,r',s',except for p'=q=)'=s=o, wlen we have already put $( ) _ { 0 }$ equal tounity.It is tobe remembered that if we interchange the accents and the sign of i, the coefficient merely changes sign ；hence the coefficient corresponding to that just written down is also zero.

Hence, the constant of latitude k is defined to be the coejficient of 2a sin F in the expression of $z$ as a sum of periodic terms.

Section (vii).-Solution of the Equations of Condition. Motions of the Perigee and Node.

It will lerc be necessary to divide up the subject according as we are treating tle equation (17) for u, tlie equation (18） for z, the hoinogeneous equations (2O), (21) for u, or the homogeneous equation (22) for z.

27. The Equation (17) for u. Thc generaltype of cquation for the terms of characteristic $\lambda$ in $u$ is

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathrm { { m } } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \lambda } + \mathcal { M } u _ { \lambda } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + \mathcal { N } s _ { \lambda } \zeta = \mathrm { { a } } \lambda \downarrow \quad \cdots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

where it is to be remembered that M, N are known even-power series in & witli numerical coefficients (equations (14)) and A contains the known terms with characteristic λ arising from the right-hand member. In only one casc do the latter terms contain an unknown quantity ；this case, which involves the determination of a part of the motion of the perigee, will be trcated in $\ S ~ 2 8 ~ ( l )$

ROYAL AsTRON. SOC., VOL. LIII.

Of the terms with charaeteristic $\lambda$ , suppose that we require to know those in $u \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ which involve $\zeta ^ { 2 i \pm \tau }$ where $\pmb { \tau }$ is one of the values of

the right-hand member containing such terms. Let, therefore, these terms in $\varLambda$ be denoted by

$$
A { = } \Sigma _ { i } ( A _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau } { + } A ^ { \prime } { _ i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - \tau } ) .
$$

We substitute

$$
u _ { \lambda } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { a } \lambda \Sigma _ { i } ( \lambda _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i \cdot \cdot r } + \lambda ^ { \prime } { _ i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - r } ) . ~ . . . \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

where $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are the unknown coeffieients to be found,and equate to zero the coeffieients of $\zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau } , \zeta ^ { - 2 i - \tau }$ The result is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( { 2 i } + \tau + \mathrm { { r + m } } ) ^ { 2 } \lambda _ { i } + \Sigma _ { j } M _ { j } \lambda _ { i - j } + \Sigma _ { j } N _ { j } \lambda _ { j - i } ^ { \prime } = A _ { i } } \\ & { ( { 2 i } + \tau - \mathrm { { r - m } } ) ^ { 2 } \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime } + \Sigma _ { j } M _ { j } \lambda _ { - i - j } ^ { \prime } + \Sigma _ { j } N _ { j } \lambda _ { j + i } { = } A _ { \mathrm { ~ - } i } ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \quad \dots \quad \quad \dots
$$

where $j { = } 0 ,$ ，土1， $\pm { } _ { 2 }$ ，..and $_ { 2 i }$ either $= 0$ ， $\pm { } _ { 2 }$ ，±4，·.. $\mathrm { o r } = \pm 1$ ， $\pm { } _ { 3 }$ ， ±5，．..Sinee $\pmb { \tau }$ multiplies $\lambda _ { i }$ and $\lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ it is evident that the eomplete values of c, $\mathbf { g }$ on the left may be replaced by their values ${ \bf c } _ { 0 } , \ { \bf g } _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ which are funetions of m only.

The $M _ { i } , N _ { i }$ have quiekly decreasing values for inereasing values of $i$ (see Chapter II.） and, in general, the same remark applies to $A _ { i } , A ^ { \prime } { } _ { - i } , \lambda _ { i } , \lambda ^ { \prime } { } _ { - i }$ The equations may thus be solved by eontinued approximation. The .unknown terms of lowest order in the equations (26） are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { c } { [ ( { \sf z } i + { \sf z } + { \sf z } + { \sf z } ) ^ { 2 } + M _ { 0 } ] \lambda _ { i } + N _ { 0 } \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime } , } \\ { N _ { 0 } \lambda _ { i } + [ ( { \sf z } i + { \sf z } - { \sf z } - { \sf z } - { \sf z } ) ^ { 2 } + M _ { 0 } ] \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime } , } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

respeetively. The equations (26） are therefore those of prineipal importanee in finding $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$

When wc solve them as two simultaneous cquations to find $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime } ,$ the common divisor is

$$
[ { \left( 2 i + \tau + \mathrm { { r + } } \mathrm { { r + } } \mathrm { { m } } \right) } ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { M } _ { 0 } ] [ ( 2 i + \tau - \mathrm { { r - } } \mathrm { { r - } } \mathrm { { m } } ) ^ { 2 } + \mathcal { M } _ { 0 } ] -  { \mathcal { N } _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } \ ;
$$

and,by the results contained in Part II.of the Investigations,it wil be seen that this is very nearly equal to

$$
( { \mathfrak { z } } { \mathfrak { i } } + { \mathfrak {tau } } ) ^ { * } [ ( { \mathfrak { z } } { \mathfrak { i } } + { \mathfrak {tau } } ) ^ { ? } - \mathfrak { c _ { 0 } } { \mathfrak { z } } ] \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

If we had eliminated all the other unknowns from the equations (26),this expression would have been a factor of the divisor,the other faetor being very nearly unity. In considering thc solution it is then only necessary to treat the cases where the expression (27） becomes small.

28.There are four special cases to consider-namely, the eases when either factor of $\left( 2 7 \right)$ is zero or small.We recall $( \ S \ 1 4 )$ that when inequalities involving odd powers of $^ { \alpha }$ are undcr consideration, $_ { 2 i }$ is an odd positive or negative integer. It is unnecessary to prove many of the statements made below ； their truth, if not evident, can easily be demonstrated.

$( a )$ The case $2 i + \tau = 0$ Here we must have $\scriptstyle \tau = \infty , i = \infty$ ，owing to the ineommensurability of c, g,in, 1. It is a question of determining coeficients of $\zeta ^ { 2 i }$ ； there arc no cocfficients $\lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and the two equiations coalesce into one which is of principal importance in determining $\lambda _ { 0 }$

$( b )$ The case $2 i + \tau = \pm \mathrm { c } _ { 0 }$ Motion of the Perigee. Here $i { = } 0$ $\tau { = } \mathrm { c } _ { 0 }$ ； $\tau { = } - \mathfrak { e } _ { 0 }$ is the same case as $\tau { = } \mathrm { c } _ { 0 }$ as we consider $\zeta ^ { \mathrm { { c } } } , \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { { c } } }$ together. In this ease $\varLambda$ eontains an unknown quantity-namely,the part of the motion of the perigce which has the characteristic $\lambda / \mathrm { e }$ say， $\mathrm { c } _ { \lambda / \mathrm { e } } ,$ and it will be found possible to put

$$
A _ { i } { = } B _ { i } { + } { \mathrm { c } } _ { { \lambda } { ; \mathrm { e } } } b _ { i } , ~ A ^ { \prime } { _ i } { = } B ^ { \prime } { _ i } { + } { \mathrm { c } } _ { { \lambda } { ; \mathrm { e } } } b ^ { \prime } { _ i }
$$

where $\mathcal { B } _ { i } , b _ { i } , \mathcal { B } _ { i } ^ { \prime } , b _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are entirely known. It will be found also that $b _ { i } , \ b _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are always the same whatever $\lambda$ may be.

Substitute these values for Ai, A'_i in (26). Multiply the frst equatio y $\epsilon _ { i \cdot }$ ,the second by $\epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ and take the sum for all values of $\mathbf { \chi } _ { i } ^ { \bullet }$ Sinee $\boldsymbol { \tau } { = } \mathbf { c } _ { 0 } ,$ W ind

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \Sigma _ { i } [ ( 2 i + { \bf c } _ { 0 } + { \bf r } + { \bf m } ) ^ { 2 } \lambda _ { i } \epsilon _ { i } + ( 2 i + { \bf c } _ { 0 } - { \bf r } - { \bf m } ) ^ { 2 } \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime } \epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime } ] } & { } \\ { + \Sigma _ { i } \Sigma _ { j } { M _ { j } } ( \lambda _ { i - j } \epsilon _ { i } + \lambda ^ { \prime } _ { - i - j } \epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime } ) + \Sigma _ { i } \Sigma _ { j } { M _ { j } } ( \lambda _ { j - i } ^ { \prime } \epsilon _ { i } + \lambda _ { j + i } \epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime } ) } & { } \\ { = \Sigma _ { i } [ B _ { i } \epsilon _ { i } + { B ^ { \prime } } _ { - i } \epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime } + { \bf c } _ { 3 i \mathrm { f } } ( B _ { i } \epsilon _ { i } + { B ^ { \prime } } _ { - i } \epsilon _ { - i } ^ { \prime } ) ] \quad , . . . \quad } & { . . . \quad \quad . . . \quad . . . \quad . . . } \end{array}
$$

But for the terms with characteristie e, we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( { 2 } i + \mathfrak { c } _ { 0 } + \mathfrak { r } + \mathfrak { m } ) ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { \epsilon } _ { i } + \Sigma _ { j } \mathcal { M } _ { j } \mathfrak { \epsilon } _ { i - j } + \Sigma _ { j } \mathcal { N } _ { j } \mathfrak { \epsilon } _ { j - i } ^ { \prime } = \circ , } \\ & { ( { 2 } i + \mathfrak { c } _ { 0 } - \mathfrak { r } - \mathfrak { m } ) \mathfrak { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } _ { - i } + \Sigma _ { j } \mathcal { M } _ { j } \mathfrak { \epsilon ^ { \prime } } _ { - i - j } + \Sigma _ { j } \mathcal { N } _ { j } \mathfrak { \epsilon } _ { j + i } = \circ . } \end{array}
$$

Substituting these in the previous equation, it is easily seen \* that the lefthand member vanishes and therefore the right-hand member of (28) equated to zero deterinines $\mathrm { e } _ { \lambda , \mathrm { e } }$ (See Investigations, p. 336).

When the value of this quantity has been fouind, the equations (26) may be solved by continued approximation,all the $\lambda _ { i }$ ， $\lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ being expressed in terms of λo,Xo. One of the two equations for X,Xocan then be deduced from the other. An arbitrary relation connects the $\lambda _ { i } , ~ \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ We have already (S 25） settled this relation to be snch that λ0=X. The determination of a new part of the motion of the perigee thus goes with a more accurate definition of the constant of eccentricity.

The manner in which ${ \mathfrak { C } } _ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ may be best obtained is fully discussed by Dr. HILL in his paper“ On the Part of the Motion of the Lunar Perigee, &c.” (Acta Math. vol. vii.). Its value is there found to fteen places of decimals. The parts of c which have the characteristics e², e², k², α² properly belong to Chapter V.(“ Inequalities of the Third Order") of this memoir. The calculations,however,have been advanced in this particular direction so that $\mathbf { C } _ { \mathrm { { e } ^ { 2 } } }$ ， $\mathbf { C } _ { \rho ^ { \prime } { 4 } }$ ， Ck: might be obtained ； they will be found in the appendix to Chapter IV.

(c) The case 2i+T small compared uith unity. The inequalities uhich are of long period comparedl with the lunar month. A troublesome defect of the method arises here. The divisor (27) contains the square of 2i+τ, while the corresponding coeffcients X, X_ are in general of the same order of magnitude as Ai/(2i+T). The reason of this is easily secn on solving the equations : one of them,in fact, generally differs from being deducible from the other by a quantity which is of the order of magnitude 2i+r. This is illustrated in a striking manner by the long-period inequality whose argument is 2F-2l (which is one of thc most troublesome in any method). Here $2 i + \tau = 2 \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { 0 } } - 2 \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ $= + \cdot 0 2 7 2$ , nearly,and therefore $( 2 i + \tau ) ^ { 2 } { = } + \cdot \infty 0 0 7 4$ ，while the corresponding numerical coefficients are of the order of magnitude unity.

The difficulty in all these cases is best avoided by coinputing the homogeneous equation (21) for the particular value of 2i+T,and combining it with one of the equations (26). In (21) the terms divided by (2i+τ)² have thc factor m²,and therefore with the same degree of accuracy in the known parts of the equation we are able to obtain λi,X'_;more accurately than if wc simply used the two equations (26).

(d) The case 2i+r±c, small compared uith unity. The numerous shortperiod inequalities the mean motions of vhose arguments approximate to that of the principal eliptic term ； e.g. the Evection and the Parallactic Inequality. The method is not in defect here, since the divisor is of the same numerical magnitude as that arising in any other method. It will be notieed that the divisors are smaller aceording as the periods approach more nearly to that of the principal elliptic term and not to the lunar mean sidereal or synodie periods.

29. When the pair of equations for $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ possess a small divisor, the approximations proeeed slowly. In some of these cases it is advisable to save labour by finding $\lambda _ { i \pm 1 } , \lambda _ { i \pm 1 } ^ { \prime }$ , in terms of $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ and the known qnantities, from the equations with suffi ${ \bf x } \mathrm { e s } \pm i \pm { \bf I }$ ,and to substitute the results in the cquations with suffix $i$ bcfore solving the latter. In all cases where tle diffieulty oeeurred, it has been avoided by this device.

The equations (6),(7） must now be put into the forms which wil give results best adapted for numerical calculation when we substitute the expressions（37） in them.

where $L$ contains the known terms arising from $z , \ 6 3 \ _ { 1 9 }$ in (6）and $D . 1$ the known terms arising from $\ Q _ { \ 1 }$ in (7).No arbitrary constant is needed in $\Lambda$ ， for the coefficient of $\zeta ^ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ always vanishes in （7).
