an equation which replaces (38).

33. Substituting the results (37） in (4O),(39)，and equating the coefficients of $\zeta ^ { 2 j + \tau }$ to zero,we find

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma , \sigma , \downarrow } \Bigg [ \Big ( ( 2 j + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - ( 2 i + \sigma ) \big ( 2 j - 2 i + \tau - \sigma ) - \mathbf { r } - 2 \mathbf { m } + \frac { \mathbf { r } } { 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } \Big ) \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , i - j } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \left( \frac { 9 } { 4 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } \frac { \mathbf { { 2 m } } + 1 } { 2 j + \tau } \right) \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , j - i - 1 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \left( \frac { 9 } { 4 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } \frac { \mathbf { { 2 m } } + 1 } { 2 j + \tau } \right) \mu _ { - \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , i - j - i - 1 } \Bigg ] = L _ { j } + ( 2 \mathbf { m } + \mathbf { r } ) \Lambda _ { j } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma , i } \Bigg [ \Big ( 2 j - 4 j + \tau - 2 \sigma - 2 \mathbf { m } - 2 ) \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , i - j } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } } { 2 j + \tau } \left( \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \sigma , j - i - 1 } - \mu _ { - \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , j - i - 1 } \right) \Bigg ] = \Lambda _ { j } \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots } } \end{array}
$$

The unknown quantities which are found by means of these equations are given by the values μ=λ, σ=T,and μ=1, o=o. Since the ai are known numerical quantities, tle equations are linear with respect to tle unknowns N±,; and they can be solved by the ordinary methods of continued approximation.

The equations written down are those of principal importance in finding X,, >-,-j.The principal terms involving these two quantities are obtained by putting

The corresponding terms in the rcspective equations are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \left\{ ( { \bf \kappa } _ { 2 } j + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { { r - } } 2 \mathrm { m } + \frac { \mathrm { I } } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \right\} ( \lambda _ { \mathrm { r } , j } + \lambda _ { \mathrm { - r } , \mathrm { - } j } ) , } } \\ { { - ( { \bf \kappa } _ { 2 } j + \tau ) ( \lambda _ { \mathrm { r } , j } - \lambda _ { \mathrm { - r } , \mathrm { - } j } ) - ( { \bf \kappa } _ { 2 } + 2 \mathrm { m } ) ( \lambda _ { \mathrm { ~ } , j } + \lambda _ { \mathrm { - r } , \mathrm { - } j } ) , } } \end{array} \nonumber \dots \quad \dots \quad \dots \quad . . .
$$

The method of solution of the simultaneous equations is therefore evident. We find the sum of the two unknowns from the first equation, and thence, substituting, their difference from the second equation.

The greater part of the labour of calculation consists in the computation of the first tcrm of each equation,owing to the fact tlat the coefficients of $\mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , i - j }$ are different for different values of $i , j$ The cocfficient in question in equation $\left( 4 1 \right)$ is best written

$$
\left( \tau ^ { 2 } - \sigma \tau + \sigma ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { { I } } - 2 \mathrm { { m } } + \textstyle { \frac { \mathrm { I } } { 2 } } \mathrm { { m } } ^ { 2 } \right) + \langle 4 j ^ { 2 } - 4 i j + 4 i ^ { 2 } \rangle + ( 4 j - 2 i ) \tau + ( 4 i - 2 j ) \sigma .
$$

The first term of this remains tlc same wlilc $\sigma$ remains the same and when $\tau - \sigma$ is put for $\sigma$ ； the second tcrm is always integral ；the third and fourth terms require only multiplication by integers. Hence, after thc first term has been obtained,no logarithmic multiplications are necessary to find the whole set of cocfficients corrcsponding to the different values of $i , j$ The last remark applies also to the corresponding coefficient in (42). Thc rest of the numerical coefficients in both equations do not involve $\dot { \iota }$

34.We can dcduce from (41),(42） forms in which the coefficicnt of $a _ { 0 } \lambda _ { \tau , j }$ is-1 and that of $a _ { 0 } \lambda _ { - \tau , - j }$ is O, so that there will be no need to solve two simultaneous equations as thc final step in each approximation to a pair of thc unknowns. The details of the algebraical steps are sufficiently simple, and I merely give an ontline.\*

The expressions (43) show that if we multiply(41), (42) by

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { 2 \mathrm { m } + \imath - 2 \dot { j } - \tau , } & { { } } & { ( \imath ^ { } \dot { } j + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { I } - \imath \mathrm { m } + \frac { \mathrm { I } } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

respectively, add and divide the resulting cquation by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 2 \left( 2 j + \tau \right) \stackrel { \textstyle \left( \right\} { \left( 2 j + \tau \right) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { I } - 2 \mathrm { m } + \frac { \mathrm { I } } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } } , } \end{array}
$$

\* Further details will befound in Chap.XI.(ii) of my Treatise on the Lunar Theory, where the spirit of this method is applied to a particular case.

the coefficient of $\lambda _ { - \tau , - j }$ will be zero,and that of $\lambda _ { \tau , j }$ will be $- \textbf { I }$ .The result is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \Sigma _ { \sigma , i } [ [ 2 j + \tau , 2 i + \sigma ] \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , i - j } + [ 2 j + \tau , ] \mu _ { \sigma , i } \nu _ { \tau - \sigma , \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ - ~ } i - 1 } }  } \\ & { } & {  + ( \imath j + \tau , ) \mu _ { - \sigma , i } \nu _ { \sigma - \tau , - j - i - 1 } ] = U _ { \tau , j } \quad \mathrm { ~ . . . ~ } \quad \mathrm { ~ . . . ~ } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array}{c} { \scriptstyle { [ { 2 \dot { j } + \tau , \ : \tau + \sigma \middle ] = - \frac { 2 \dot { \sigma } + \sigma } { 2 \dot { j } + \tau } } \ { \scriptstyle { \frac { 2 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 3 } - 2 - 4 \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \dot { j } + \sigma - 2 \dot { j } - \tau ) ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau - 2 - 2 \mathrm { m } ) } { 2 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 3 } - 2 - 4 \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } } } ) } } \\ { { \scriptstyle { [ { 2 \dot { j } + \tau , \tau } ] = - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \displaystyle { \frac { ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - 4 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) - 2 - 2 \dot { \sigma } - 2 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau + 4 ) \mathrm { m } - 9 \mathrm { m } ^ { 4 } } { ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 3 } \{ 2 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - 2 - 4 \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { m } ^ { 3 } \} } } } } \\ { { \scriptstyle { ( { 2 \dot { j } + \tau , \tau ) = - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 4 } \displaystyle { \Sigma ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - 8 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) + 2 - 2 ( \mathrm { r o } \dot { j } + \ 5 \dot { \tau } - 4 ) \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { \mathrm { m } } ^ { 2 } } } } \normalsize \ } \ \dots \ { \scriptstyle { ( 4 5 ) } } } \end{array} }
$$

$$
H _ { \tau , j } = \frac { ( 2 \mathrm { m } + 2 - 2 \dot { j } - \tau ) L _ { j } + \left\{ ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { { r } } - 2 \mathrm { m } + \frac { \mathrm { { r } } } { \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } + \left( \mathrm { { 2 m } + \mathrm { { r } } } \right) \left( 2 \mathrm { m } + 2 - 2 \dot { j } - \tau \right) \right\} \Lambda _ { j } } { ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) \left\{ 2 ( 2 \dot { j } + \tau ) ^ { 2 } - 2 - 4 \mathrm { m } + \mathrm { { m } ^ { 2 } } \right\} } \mathrm { ~ , ~ }
$$

The equation tor $\lambda _ { - \tau , - j }$ is obtained trom (44）by changing the signs of $\tau , j$ 35. The case $( \cdot )$ of $\$ 28$ deserves mention in connection with equation (44). It will be noticed that whenever the square of $2 i + \tau$ appears as a divisor,namely, in the second and third terns of (44） and in $\Lambda _ { j } / ( 2 j + \pmb { \tau } )$ (sinice $\Lambda _ { j }$ already contains it as a divisor), the terms have the factor $\mathrm { { m } ^ { 2 } }$ The usc of equation (7)or $( 4 2 )$ for the calculation of the coefficients of longperiod inequalities has been noticed in $\ S \ O \ O \left( \begin{array} { l } { \delta } \end{array} \right)$ Thus (41), (42） and (44) are free from the objection remarked in that paragraph.

It may be also noticed that the expressions (43)show that when $2 j + \tau$ is small, the corresponding loss of accuracy in the results arises in the difference of $\lambda _ { \tau , j ; \lambda } \lambda _ { - \tau , - j }$ and not in their sum.This, translated to polar coordinates, is equivalent to saying that the loss of accuracy in the coeffcients of longperiod inequalitiesis chiefly felt in the longitude and but little in theparallax. In the caseofshort-period inequalities withsmall divisors the loss of accuracy falls on both coordinates.See also $\ S \ 3 \mathtt { I }$

With regard to the relative advantages and disadvantages of the equations (3),(4) and the homogeneous equations,from the point of view of actual calculation,it seems,from the experience gained in using both forms, that on the whole the advantage lies with the former. This is certainly true for inequalities of the second order and very probably for those of the third order also. For inequalities of higher orders， the operations in the former are numerous but simple, and capable of eontinual verification ；those in the latter are rather less numerous but more complicated, more productive of eror,less casily verified at the various stages,and are much less easily arranged for a computer. The equations (3),(4) are now being used for the inequalities of the third order.

If the homogeneous cquations be used, thc expansions (41), (42） are preferable in actual calenlation to (44). Tlis remark does not, of course,apply to the inequalities of zero order, computed by Dr. HILL in the Rescarchcs.

36. The Homogeneous Equation (8) or $( 8 ^ { \prime } )$ for $z$ may be treated in a similar manner,and a formula similar to (41） or(42） obtained for it. The calculations and rcsults are much simpler, first on account of the less complicated form of(8)；and, secondly,on account of the faet that there is only one equation of principal importanee for the detcrmination of eaeh unknown, instead of two equations for eaeh pair of unknowns. As the equation (4) will probably be used for all inequalities,I shall not develope thc equation (8).

The motions of the perigee and node are found by continued approximation along with the unknown coefficients. It is possible to eliminate all the latter and to get single equations for the parts of the motions of the perigee or node corresponding to those given in $\ S \ S \ S$ ，31 above ；but this will be no saving of time,as the equations are somewhat complicated. The formula necessary will therefore not be given here.

# Section (vii). Calculation and Verifcation.

37. It has already been stated that one of the chief objects aimed at in the developments given above was the rcduction of the calculations to forms which made them as far as possible merely mechanical. This is the case with the right-hand members of(17),(18) or(17'),(18). In the case of the former, $\frac { \kappa \mathit { v } _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } { \mathsf { a } \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } , \frac { \kappa } { \mathsf { a } \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } }$ ap2 are calculated， once for all, from the values of $u _ { 0 } , \cdot s _ { 0 }$ At each stage of the approximations $u _ { \mu } / u _ { 0 }$ is obtained from the value of $u _ { \mu }$ by multiplication of $\mathcal { U } _ { \mu } \big > ^ { - 1 }$ by $\Im / \mathcal { U } _ { \ u { 0 } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathcal { S } _ { \ u { 0 } } \zeta / \rho _ { \ u { 0 } } ^ { \ 2 }$ ,the last quantity being found by “ special values.” Thc remainder of the caleulations of the right-hand members are then simple multiplications of $\zeta$ series. In the case of the equations $( { \bf 1 7 ^ { \prime } } ) , ~ ( { \bf 1 8 ^ { \prime } } )$ (whieh were used for the rcsults of Chap.IV.and for some of those which will be published in Chap. V.) $P .$ $\bar { P }$ $\mathcal { Q }$ ，．．．were calculated by the method of speeial values from those of $u _ { \mathrm { ( ) } } \ \mathcal { S } _ { 0 }$ ； the remainder of the process is then as before.

$3 8$ .The plaadoptedfor themultiplicationof any two $\zeta$ -series, say

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \big ( \Sigma _ { i } \alpha _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \alpha } \big ) \big ( \Sigma _ { i } / 3 _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \beta } \big ) { = } \Sigma _ { i , j } \alpha _ { j - i } \beta _ { \iota } \zeta ^ { 2 j + \mathrm { ~ } } } & { { } \beta } \end{array}
$$

consists in taking out the logarithms of the $\pmb { a } _ { i }$ and arranging them along a slip of paper in the order.．. $a _ { 2 } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 0 } , a _ { - 1 } , a _ { - 2 } ,$ ...；the logarithms of the $\beta _ { i }$ are arranged along another slip in the order . $\cdot \cdot \beta _ { - 2 } , \beta _ { - 1 } , \beta _ { \circ } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } ,$ ... The two slips being placed over one another, thc sums of all the logarithms for a given value of $j$ are taken witlout moving the slips,and they are written down in a eolumn. The number corresponding to cach logarithm is then taken from the tables and the results added for cach value of $j .$ Thus to find the coefficient of $\zeta ^ { 4 + \alpha + \beta }$ in the product, that is $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } \alpha _ { 2 - i } \beta _ { i } . } \end{array}$ the slips are placed so that $\pmb { a } _ { \mathrm { 2 } }$ falls under $\beta _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ then ${ \pmb { a } } _ { 1 }$ falls under $\beta _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ，&c.The arrangement of this part of the shect is then-

<table><tr><td colspan="2">Values of</td><td colspan="2">ajB</td></tr><tr><td>j i</td><td></td><td>log</td><td>number</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">2</td><td>：</td><td>：</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>12</td><td>：</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>一I</td><td>：</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>：</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>：</td><td>*</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Sum,j=2</td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The process can be thus arranged for a computer, and the mere copying of figures from one shet to another is very rarely neeessary.

The result of each inultiplication of serics is verified by adding the sums for all values of $j$ ，The suin should be equal to $\big ( \Sigma _ { i } a _ { i } \big ) \big ( \Sigma _ { i } \beta _ { i } \big )$

39. The values of the At, $A _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ in cquations(26), (32) are in general carried to the same number of places of deimals for each value of i. In the solution of thc equations of condition, large divisors frequently oecur for large values of i and the λ, X are obtained to one or two more plaees of dceimals for large values of $i$ Thus it is in general possible to find $D ^ { 2 } u _ { \lambda }$ to the same degree of accuraey as the $A _ { i } , A ^ { \prime } { } _ { i }$ .Exception. only oceurs in the cases of some long and short period inequalities ； when it oceurs, the corresponding valucs of

$A _ { i } , A ^ { \prime } { } _ { i }$ are taken to one or two more places of decimals or the homogeneous equation(7） is used,as explained il $\ S \ 2 8 \ ( c )$ ·

The object of taking the values of $\lambda _ { i } , ~ \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ to more decimals for large values ofiis to render the equation of verification more searching. For verification I use one of the homogeneous equations with ${ \pmb \zeta } = { \bf I }$ ；the calculation ofit is never very long,and it appears to furnish a good test. See Investigations, p. 343.

40.In the following chapters I give in general two sets of numerical results : First, the values of the right-hand coefficients $A _ { i } , A ^ { \prime } { } _ { i }$ ； and, secondly, the values of $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ .They are taken exactly as they stood in my manuscripts. Although many of them will not be more than two units wrong in the last place given, the number of the calculations prevents this being said of all. They are intended to be trustworthy as far as the last figure but one in each case. The sums of the numbers in cach colunin are always given, so that any error of transcription or typography may be detected should it occur.

All calculations are made at least twice,separated by an interval of time. The general plan,when several hours a day were available,was to have two or three separate sets of calculations proceeding together. Each of these would be carried to a certain stage and,after the lapse of a day or two, they would be gone over again，the errors,if any,corrected, the results verified(whenever this was possible) and they would then be taken a stage further. In this way an error running through several pages of calculations was avoided.

Section (i).-Characteristic e. Value of $\mathbf { c } _ { 0 }$

48. The value of co, which is the part of c depending only on m, has been found by Dr. HIL \* to 15 places of decimals. It is,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r l } { \mathsf { c } _ { 0 } = + \mathsf { r } ^ { \bullet } \mathsf { o } 7 \mathsf { \bf { I } } \mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathrm { { 8 } } } } & { { } 3 ^ { 2 } 7 7 4 \quad \mathrm { ~ I } 6 \mathsf { o } \mathrm { { \bf { I } } } 2 . } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

The equation satisfied by $\mathbf { c } _ { 0 }$ and the terms with characteristic e is

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathbf { m } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \mathsf { e } } + \mathcal { M } u _ { \mathsf { e } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + \mathit { N s } _ { \mathsf { e } } \zeta = \mathsf { o } .
$$

Thesolution is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathcal { U } _ { \Theta } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { a e } \Sigma _ { i } ( \epsilon _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \mathfrak { c } } + \epsilon ^ { \prime } _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - \mathfrak { c } } ) . } \end{array}
$$

The equations of condition for the unknowns having been obtained by substituting the assumed solution in the differential equation, and equating the coefficients of the various powers of $\zeta$ to zero, we may solve them with the above value of c, so as to give $\epsilon _ { i }$ ， $\boldsymbol { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ in terms of $\epsilon _ { 0 } , \epsilon _ { \mathrm { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } } ^ { \prime }$

Let

$$
\epsilon _ { i } \mathrm { = b } _ { i } \epsilon _ { 0 } + \beta _ { i } \epsilon { ' } _ { 0 } , \qquad \epsilon { ' } _ { i } \mathrm { = b } { ' } _ { i } \epsilon _ { 0 } + \beta { ' } _ { i } \epsilon { ' } _ { 0 } .
$$

I obtained these results by the use of the homogeneous equations.\* A different set of values for $b _ { i } , \ b _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \beta _ { i } , \beta _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ will naturally arise if we use the equation at the beginning of this section. One slight error which occurred in the reduction of $\log _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } \epsilon _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } \epsilon _ { 0 }$ to the final value of $\epsilon _ { \mathrm { ~ 4 ~ } } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { \ w a s }$ discovered and corrected.

The short-period inequality with a small divisor is the“Evection”; the corresponding coefficients are $\epsilon _ { - 1 } , \epsilon _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$

# 49. The equation is

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathrm { m } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { e ^ { \prime } } + M u _ { e ^ { \prime } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + N s _ { e ^ { \prime } } \zeta = - \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 1 } } { \partial s } \zeta ^ { - 1 } .
$$

In the right-hand member we put $\ L _ { 1 } = \omega _ { 2 } , \ z = \ L _ { 0 } , \ u = u _ { 0 } , \ s = s _ { 0 } ,$ and neglect powers of $e ^ { \prime }$ above the first (Chap. I., $\ S \_ { 1 9 }$ ）.

Hence, by Chap.I., Sect. (ii),

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial \bigotimes _ { 1 } } { \partial s } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \Big [ \frac { 3 } { 2 } \vec { \mathfrak { a } } _ { 2 } s _ { 0 } + \frac { \mathtt { I } } { 2 } \mathfrak { b } _ { 2 } u _ { 0 } \Big ] \zeta ^ { - 1 } } \\ & { \qquad = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } e ^ { \prime } \big [ ( u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + 7 s _ { 0 } \zeta \cdot \zeta ^ { - 2 } ) \zeta ^ { \mathrm { m } } + ( u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } - s _ { 0 } \zeta \cdot \zeta ^ { - 2 } ) \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { m } } \big ] . } \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { u _ { e ^ { \prime } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathsf { a } e ^ { \prime } \Sigma _ { i } ( \eta _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \mathrm { m } } + \eta ^ { \prime } _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - \mathrm { m } } ) . } \end{array}
$$

The equations of condition are formed and then solved by continied approximation.

The long period inequality with a small divisor is thc“ Annual Equation,” having the coefficients $\eta _ { \mathrm { 0 } } , \eta _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ^ { \prime }$ .The method of $\ S$ 29 was used in the approximations to these two coefficients.

The values of the corresponding terms in the true longitude have been given in a note in the Monthly Notices, vol. liv. p. 471.

$5 0$ The equation is

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathrm { m } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \alpha } + M u _ { \alpha } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + N s _ { \alpha } \zeta = - \frac { \partial \otimes _ { 1 } } { \partial s } \zeta ^ { - 1 } .
$$

In the right-hand member we put $\begin{array} { r } { 8 _ { 1 } = \omega _ { 3 } , z = 0 , \ell = 0 , u = u _ { 0 } , s = s _ { 0 } . } \end{array}$ Hence, by Chap. I., Sect. (ii),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \otimes _ { 1 } } { \partial s } \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathbf { a } a \mathrm { ~ . ~ } \frac { \ d ^ { 3 } } { 4 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ . ~ } \frac { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } { \mathbf { a } ^ { 2 } } \Big [ \frac { 5 } { 2 } ( s _ { 0 } \zeta ) ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { - 3 } + \frac { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } { 2 } ( u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } \zeta + ( u _ { 0 } s _ { 0 } ) \zeta ^ { - 1 } \Big ] . } \end{array}
$$

Tle solution is

$$
\varkappa _ { a } \xi ^ { - 1 } = \mathtt { a } a \mathfrak { x } _ { i } ( a ) _ { i } \xi ^ { 2 i } ,
$$

where

$$
\iota 2 i = \pm 1 , \pm _ { 3 } , \pm _ { 5 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \ .
$$

These coefficients I found to seven places of decimals in a paper “ On the Parallactic Inequalities in the Lunar Theory”\* by the use of the homogeneous equations. They have been recalculated and extended to ten places by the above method ；errors of one unit only in the sixth places of decimals in the values of $a _ { \scriptscriptstyle - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } , a _ { \scriptscriptstyle \frac { 3 } { 2 } }$ were detected. Dr.HILL in his paper ${ \mathfrak { s o } } _ { \mathfrak { n } }$ the Periodic Solution,&c.,"+ using my former values as a first approximation has also recalculated these terms to a high degree of accuracy by a totally different method.

The short-period inequality with a small divisor is the“ Parallactic Inequality,”having thecoeffcients $a _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } , \ a _ { \textrm { - } \frac { 1 } { 2 } } .$

Section (iv).Characteristic k.Value of ${ \bf g } _ { 0 }$

51. The part of the value of $\mathbf { g }$ which depends on m only, namely $\mathbf { g } _ { 0 } ,$ has been obtained by Professor $\mathbf { J }$ .C. ADAMs and Mr. P.H. CowELL (see the references in $\ S \ O _ { 3 ^ { \ I } }$ ).The latter finds

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { g } _ { 0 } \mathbf { = } \mathbf { r } { \cdot } \circ 8 5 \mathbf { \pi } \mathbf { \intercal } \mathbf { 7 } \quad \mathbf { \textit { 1 4 2 6 5 } } \quad \mathbf { 5 } ^ { 8 } . } \end{array}
$$

The slightly different result obtained by ADAMs is due to the use of a different value for m.

The equation giving ${ \bf g } _ { 0 }$ and the terms with characteristic k is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { D ^ { 2 } z _ { \mathrm { k } } - 2 \mathcal { M } z _ { \mathrm { k } } = \circ . } \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
z _ { \mathbf { k } } \ \tau { = } { \mathrm { a k } } \Sigma _ { i } \ k _ { i } \ ( \zeta ^ { 2 i + g } { - } \zeta ^ { - 2 i - g } ) .
$$

The constant k is defined (Chap. I., $\ S \ 2 6 )$ to be such that

$$
k _ { 0 } = \mathtt { r } .
$$

\* Amer.Jour. Math.vol. xiv.p. I57. A different notation is there used.

$^ { \dagger }$ Astron.Jour.vol. xv. pp. 137-I43. Dr. HiL informs me that the large correction which he obtained to my value of the coefficient of the Parallactic Inequality n longitude, amounting to $\mathfrak { s }$ units in the ffth place of decimals in the value of $\pmb { a } _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } - \pmb { a } _ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ,$ was due to a slight error in reducing them to his form，As the resulting value was only used as a first approximation,his final results are,of course, correct.

ROYAL ASTRON. SOC., VOL. LIII.

# CHAPTER IV

TERMS OF THE SECOND ORDER.

52. The general type of the equation for u for terms with characteristic 入, and arguments $z i \pm \tau$ ,is, by Chap. I., $\ S \ O 2 0$ ，

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } ( D + \mathrm { { m } } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \lambda } + M u _ { \lambda } \ \zeta ^ { - 1 } + N s _ { \lambda } \zeta { = } \mathrm { { a } } \lambda A
$$

where

$$
A = \Sigma _ { i } ( A _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau } + A ^ { \prime } { } _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - \tau } ) \mathrm { w h e n } \tau \ne \circ ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A = \Sigma _ { i } \ A _ { i } \ \zeta ^ { 2 i } \ \mathrm { w h e n } \ \tau = \circ . } \end{array}
$$

Here, by Chap.I., equation $\left( 1 7 ^ { \prime } \right)$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { a } \lambda A = \mathrm { P a r t , \ c h a r e } \mathrm { , ~ } \lambda \mathrm { , ~ i n ~ \frac { 1 } { a } ~ } \biggl [ - \mathrm { a } \frac { \hat { \sigma } \otimes _ { 1 } } { \hat { \sigma } s } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 3 } { 8 } \stackrel { \bar { P } } { P } ( \Sigma u _ { \mu } \zeta ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { \Gamma \bar { \varsigma } } { 8 } Q \ ( \Sigma s _ { \mu } \zeta ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } P \ ( \Sigma u _ { \mu } ) \ ( \Sigma s _ { \mu } ) - \frac { 3 } { 2 } P \ ( \Sigma z _ { \mu } ) ^ { 2 } \biggr ] \qquad \ldots } \\ & { \qquad \quad \Sigma u _ { \mu } = u _ { \mathrm { e } } + u _ { e } + u _ { \alpha } , \qquad \Sigma z _ { e } = z _ { \mathrm { k } } . } \end{array}
$$

In all cases $s$ is derived from $\boldsymbol { u }$ by putting $1 / \zeta$ for $\boldsymbol { \zeta } .$ The first term of $\left( { { \bf { I } } 7 ^ { \prime } } \right)$ contributes nothing to the terms of the second order.

Also,by Chap. I.， Sect. (i)，substituting for $u , s , z$ and neglecting powers and products of $u _ { \mu } , \ s _ { \mu } , \ z _ { \mu } ,$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \begin{array} { r l r } { \frac { \partial \bigotimes _ { 1 } } { \partial s } = \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \frac { 3 } { 2 } ( s _ { 0 } + \Sigma s _ { \mu } ) \overline { { \mathfrak { a } } } _ { 2 } + \frac { \Pi } { 2 } ( u _ { 0 } + \Sigma u _ { \mu } ) \mathfrak { b } _ { 2 } \biggr ] } \end{array} } \\ & { \qquad + \begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { ~ + ~ } \frac { \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } { \alpha ^ { \prime } } \biggl [ \frac { \mathrm { T } \mathrm { S } } { 8 } ( s _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + 2 s _ { 0 } \sum s _ { \mu } ) \overline { { \mathfrak { a } } } _ { 3 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } ( u _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \ u _ { 0 } \Xi u _ { \mu } ) \mathfrak { c } _ { 3 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } ( u _ { 0 } \ : s _ { 0 } + u _ { 0 } \ : \Sigma s _ { \mu } + \delta _ { 0 } \ : \Sigma u _ { \mu } ) \overline { { \mathfrak { c } } } _ { 3 } \biggr ] } \end{array} } \\ & { \qquad + \begin{array} { r l r } { \frac { \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } { \alpha ^ { \prime } } \biggl [ 3 \sum _ { 1 } \delta _ { \begin{array} { c c c c c } { 3 } & { + } & { 5 } & { u _ { 0 } \mathrm { ~ } ^ { 3 } + \frac { \mathrm { T } \cdot 5 } { 1 6 } } u _ { 0 } \ : s _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 9 } { 1 6 } u _ { 0 } \ : ^ { 2 } \delta _ { 0 } ] } & { \cdots } & { \cdots } & { \cdots } & { \cdots } & { \cdots } \end{array} \quad \cdots \quad \cdots \quad \cdots \quad \cdots \quad \cdots } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

where

The solution is of the form

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { u , \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { a } \lambda \Sigma _ { i } ( \lambda _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau } + \lambda ^ { \prime } _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i - \tau } ) , \mathrm { ~ w h e n ~ } \tau \neq \mathrm { 0 } , } } & { { \quad \dots \quad \dots \quad \dots \quad \dots \quad ( 4 ) } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { u , \zeta ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { a } \lambda \Sigma _ { i } \lambda _ { i } \zeta ^ { 2 i } , \mathrm { ~ w h e n ~ } \tau = \mathrm { 0 } \quad \quad \dots \quad \quad \dots \quad \dots \quad \langle 4 ^ { \prime } \rangle } } \end{array}
$$

The process consists in first finding the series for $\lrcorner 1$ in each case,and then,after the substitution of the solution in the differential equation, to form the equations of condition for the unknown coefficients. These are solved by continued approximation， there being one pair of equations of principal importance in finding cach pair of coefficients $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ The known values ${ \bf c } _ { 0 } , \ { \bf g } _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ of c,g,and the definitions of e,k given in Chap.III. are sufficient. The further definition of the linear constant $( \ S \ 2 4 )$ occurs in sections (ii),(iv)，(v)，(vii) below.

53. The general type of the equation for $z$ ,for terins with characteristic $\gimel$ and arguinents $2 i + \tau$ is,by Chap.L, $\ S$ 20,

$$
\scriptstyle D ^ { 2 } z _ { \lambda } - \lambda M z _ { \lambda } = \mathrm { a } \lambda M \iota \quad \ldots \quad \ldots \quad \ldots \quad \ldots \quad \ldots \quad \ldots \quad \ldots
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A { = } \Sigma _ { i } A _ { i } ( \zeta ^ { { 2 i } + { r } } { - } \zeta ^ { { - } { 2 i } - { r } } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Here,by Chap. I., equation $\left( 1 8 ^ { \prime } \right)$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathrm { a } \lambda A \iota = \mathrm { P a r t , ~ c h a r ^ { \circ } . } \lambda , \mathrm { i n } \biggl [ - \frac { \tau } { 2 } \frac { \partial \mathsf { \otimes } _ { 1 } } { \partial z } } & { } \\ { - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \mathsf { S } \mathsf { z } _ { \mu } } { \mathsf { a } } ( \vec { P } \Sigma u _ { \mu } \zeta ^ { - 1 } + P \Sigma s , \zeta ) \biggr ] \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots } & { } \\ { \Sigma u _ { \mu } = u _ { \mathrm { e } } + u _ { e } , + u _ { \alpha } , \qquad \Sigma _ { \widetilde { \alpha } _ { \mu } = \widetilde { s } _ { \mathrm { k } } } . } & { } \end{array}
$$

The first term of equation $\left( { \tt I } 8 ^ { \prime } \right)$ contributes nothing to tlie terms of the second order.

Also,by Chap. I., Sect. (i),substituting for $u , s , z$ the values just given and neglecting powers and products of $u _ { \mu } , \ s _ { \mu } , \ z _ { \mu } ,$

$$
\begin{array}{c} - \frac { \texttt { I } } { 2 } \frac { \partial \texttt { B } _ { 1 } } { \partial z } = \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } z _ { \mathrm { k } } \dot { \mathfrak { h } } _ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } \frac { \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } } { \alpha ^ { \prime } } \tilde { z } _ { \mathrm { k } } ( u _ { 0 } + s _ { 0 } ) \quad \ldots \quad  & { \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad ( 7 ) } \end{array}
$$

wwhere ${ \mathfrak { k } } _ { 2 }$ takes its value as far as $e ^ { \prime }$

The solution is

$$
z _ { \lambda } \iota = \mathrm { a } \lambda \Sigma _ { i } \lambda _ { i } ( \zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau } - \zeta ^ { - 2 i - \tau } ) \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ .
$$

The process is the same as before. There is only one equation of principal importance in finding any coefficient $\lambda _ { i }$ The known values ${ \bf c } _ { 0 } , \ { \bf g } _ { 0 }$ of c,g and the definitions of the arbitrary constants given in Chap. II. are sufficient.

The results selected for publication are the numerical values of A, A' and those of the unknowns $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$

The degree of accuracy to which the various results have been carried depend, first, on the gcneral numerical magnitude of the characteristic ； secondly, for Ai, A', on the cases where 2i±T, 2i±τ±c,or 2i±τ±g become small ； thirdly,for λi, X,on the accuracy required for certain terms of higher orders. The symbols $( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) , ( \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } )$ are there denoted by $f / Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } , f ^ { \prime } / Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ respectively ； the symbol $( \epsilon \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) _ { i }$ used here is not the exact equivalent of $\delta a _ { i } / Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }$ in that paper， owing to the meaning there assigned to $a _ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ being different from that of a. To compare them we must put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( \epsilon \epsilon ^ { \prime } ) _ { i } = ( a _ { 0 } \delta \alpha _ { i } + a _ { i } \delta a _ { 0 } ) \div Y _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

the terms in the right-hand member being the quantities contained in the paper referred to.

An error of one unit in the sixth place of the value of $( \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) _ { - 2 } ,$ or $f _ { - 2 }$ in the paper, was discovered,inducing smaller errors in the other coefficients, All the coefficients have been re-calculated by the method of this memoir and the results,as seen above, extended to nine places of decimals.

The long-period inequality with a small divisor is that having the coefficients $\left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } \right) _ { - 1 }$ ， $( \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } ) _ { 1 }$ .This was separately calculated by the homogeneous equations as in the“ Elliptic Inequalities”and the results were verified by the equations of condition which the above method furnishes.

The short period inequalities having small divisors are those with coefficients $( \epsilon \eta ) _ { 0 } , ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } \eta ^ { \prime } ) _ { 0 } ; ( \epsilon \eta ) _ { - 1 } , ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } \eta ^ { \prime } ) _ { 1 } ; ( \epsilon \eta ^ { \prime } ) _ { 0 } , ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } \eta ) _ { 0 } ; ( \epsilon \eta ^ { \prime } ) _ { - 1 } , ( \epsilon ^ { \prime } \eta ) _ { 1 } .$ For the purpose of obtaining these with the required accuracy, the corresponding coefficients in A are carried one place further than the rest. The values of A have been computed by both(17）,(17'） of§2o.The slow approximations to the values of the coefficients with suffix zero were avoided by the method of $\ S$ 29.\*

The values of the coefficients of the corresponding terms in the true longitude have been published in a note in the Monthly Notices, Vol. LV.p. 4.

$$

These coefficients were given to seven places of decimals by Mr. P. H. Cowell on pp. 119, 117 of his paper referred to in $\ S \ O 3 1$ above,being obtained by means of the homogeneous equations. With his values as a first approximation,Irecalculated and extended them to nine places by the above method.Small errors in $( k ^ { 2 } ) _ { - 1 } , ( k ^ { \prime 2 } ) _ { 1 }$ were found. These two coefficients are those of a long-period term with a small divisor. The former was obtained by means of equation （44） of Chap.I.， the latter being tlen found from one of the two equations of condition furnished by the general method used for the rest of the coefficients. The coefficients $( k k ^ { \prime } ) _ { i }$ in Mr. Cowell's paper are such that $( k k ^ { \prime } ) _ { 0 } { = } 0$ ; to compare them with those given here,a transformation like that noted at the end of Sect. (ii) of this chapter must be made. The value of $\delta { a } _ { 0 }$ in that formula is the $( k { k ^ { \prime } } ) _ { 0 }$ of the table in this section.

62.Having finished the terms of the second order in $u$ ，we now come to those in $z$ which are distinguished by having the first power of k in all their cliaracteristics. For these terms the equations(5)-(8） of Sect.(i) of this chapter are uscd. The calculations cause very little trouble and are not long. In the short-period inequalities with small divisors, the progress of the approximations is not very slow. Long-period inequalities do not produce small divisors.

dppendix-The terms of the third order (Chap. V.)are in process of calculation. Tle following results, properly belonging to Chap.V., have already been obtained and may be recorded here. They have been used as the basis of a paper in the Monthly Notices," On the Mean Motions of the Lunar Perigee and Node"(1897 March).

Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { c } = \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { 0 } } + \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 2 } } + \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \mathrm { g } = \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { 0 } } + \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 3 } } + \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } + \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { E } ^ { 3 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { \mathfrak { e } } _ { \mathfrak { e } ^ { 2 } } = + \ { \cdot } { \ 0 0 2 6 8 } } & { { 5 7 5 } , } & { \quad { \mathfrak { E } } _ { \mathfrak { e } ^ { 2 } } = + { \cdot } { \ 0 0 3 1 8 } } & { { 5 7 9 } , } \\ { { \mathfrak { e } } _ { e ^ { \prime } } = - { \cdot } { \circ 3 4 } ^ { 6 } { 5 } } & { { 5 3 } , } & { \quad { \mathfrak { E } } _ { e ^ { \prime } } = \div { \cdot } { \ 0 0 5 6 } 4 } & { 6 5 , } \\ { { \mathfrak { e } } _ { \mathtt { k } ^ { 2 } } = + { \cdot } { \circ 5 3 8 } 4 } & { { 9 \mathrm { I } } , } & { \quad { \mathfrak { E } } _ { \mathtt { k } ^ { 2 } } = - { \cdot } { \ 0 8 0 6 } } & { 6 3 3 . } \end{array}
$$

The value of gr: is quoted from Mr. Cowel's paper referred to in \$ 31 above,

Haxerford College,Pa.U.S.A.: 1897 May I2.

Theory of the Motion of the Moon； containing a Neu Calculation of the Expres-sions for the Coordinates of the Moon in Terms of the Time. By ERNEST W. BROWN, M.A., Sc.D., F.R.S.

[Received 1899 February 3.] PART II. CHAPTER V.

IN the following pages I continue the Memoir the first part of which was published under the same title in the Memoirs of the Royal Astronomical Society in 1897. The general theory was given in Chap. I. as completely as I could then foresee would be necessary for the whole work. In Chaps.II.， II., IV. the numerical results up to and inclusive of thc terms of the second order were given.

As the work progressed modifications tending to simplify or abbreviate the calculations naturally occurred. These, however, were fewer than might have been expected. Thc most important of them is given in Section (ii) below,consisting of a new method for finding the values of the final coefficients, after those of the quantities denoted in Chap. I. by A have been obtained. Previously this process consisted in solving,by continued approximation, for each characteristic and argument, a set of linear equations which were gcnerally about 2o in number, with 2o unknowns. This proccss I had not succeded in arranging conveniently for the computer,and as, in the terms of the third order,it involved about one-third of the whole work, some change was desirable. The investigation which led up to the ncw method for this purpose was made from a differcnt point of view some three years ago ； its usefulness became apparent directly the arrangement of the work for thc computer was under consideration. Moreovcr, the numerical errors made in solving the lincar equations were quite numerous ；under the method of Section (ii） they have been no more frequent than in other parts of the work.

The numerical results given below are the values for all thc terms of the third order, with ccrtain subsidiary results which the above-mentioned modifications require. The degree of accuracy which the theory up to this point attains may be best apprcciated by a statement of the maximum number of coefficients of the fourth and higher orders, which may be as great as $1 ^ { \prime \prime }$ of arc in longitude. Of the fourth order, one of $4 ^ { \prime \prime }$ and three of ${ } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，which contain only e, k in their characteristics,and twenty of $\bar { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ； of the fifth order thcre are two of ${ \bf 1 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,which involve both e,k. Moreover, thc principal parts of nearly all of these are thc purely elliptic terms.

I have rcceived very great help in performing the calculations from Mr. Ira I. Sterner, A.B., of Haverford College, who has, since $\mathrm { \Delta I } 8 9 7$ September,been my only assistant. That so much has bcen achieved in the time we were ablc to give is largely due to his accuracy and capability. Much of the work done by him would scarcely have been attempted by an ordinary computer without very extended instructions,while his knowledge of arithmetical processes has not only been a great saving of time and labour, but has made the chief part of my task-that of testing and correcting his work-a comparatively light one.\*

The following is the table of contents of Chap. V. :-

Seetion (i). A brief outline of the application of the general method to thc terms of the third order in thc calculation of the series $\varLambda$

Section (ii). New method for solving the linear equations when the series $\boldsymbol { A }$ have been obtained. Numerical valucs of certain quantities required in this method.

Section (ii). Modification of this mcthod, in order to avoid,as far as possible, the loss of accuracy arising with long-period terms.

Section (iv). The method of calculating the new parts of the motions of

\* A portion of the expense of making the computations necessary to obtain the results given below has been met by a grant from the Government Grant Fund of the Royal Socicty.

the perigce and node, and the coefcients arising therewith. Numerical values of certain quantities required.

Section (v). The final numerical results for the series A,and for the coeffcients ofall terms of te third order in u, \*

Seetion (i). Formula and Tests.

$$

and choosing out the parts of the third order. Thc products in the fourth and fth lines of (17)and thc third line of (18)are obtained with

$$

$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { u _ { i } = u _ { \mathrm { e } } + u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { t } } + u _ { \mathrm { f } } \approx z _ { \mathrm { i } } = z _ { \mathrm { i } } , } \\ & { u _ { \mathrm { i } } = u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { s } } } + u _ { \mathrm { e } e ^ { \prime } } + u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \prime \mathrm { s } } } + u _ { \mathrm { i } s ^ { \prime } } + u _ { \mathrm { e } \ast } + u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \prime } \ast } + u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \prime } } , } \\ & { z _ { \mathrm { i } } = z _ { \mathrm { k e } } + z _ { \mathrm { k } e ^ { \prime } } + z _ { \mathrm { i } s } , } \end{array}

thc expression for $s _ { 2 }$ being similar to that for $\mathcal { U } _ { \mathfrak { Y } }$

The parts arising from &1, namely, $\frac { 0 . 8 3 } { 0 . 5 } ^ { 1 } \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ ， - 081 are treatcd in like manner.

The parts arising from the first terms in tle right-hand members of the equaticns，are treated in Section (iv) below ； they only appear when the terms of arguments $_ { 2 i \pm \mathrm { c } }$ $2 i { \stackrel { \pm } { - } } \mathrm { g }$ are under consideration.

The general method of procedure has been as follows :--Tle computer having performed the caleulations allotted to him, I go over them all and test them by all the means available. Each multiplication of series is tested by sums as explained in Section (vii), Chap. I. “The final values of $\varLambda$ are tested in the same manner,but in larger groups, so as to make certain that no series of terms has been omited ; for this, the values of $A$ for each characteristic，with $\zeta = 1$ ，are all added together and thc sums compared with the corresponding sums obtained as directly as possible from the algebraical formule,which become quite simple when $\zeta = \tt { I }$ .It is true that this method will not test for all kindsof errors,e.g.the accidental interchange of $u$ and $s ,$ but reliance las been placed less on test cquations than on the care which has been taken to avoid errors. The calculations madc after the method of Section (ii) were treated in the same way as far as possible ；a further and vcry searching test was obtained by forming $D ^ { 2 } u _ { \lambda }$ ${ D u } _ { \mathrm { \lambda \mathrm { \Gamma } } }$ with $\zeta = \tau$ ,for each value of $\pmb { \tau }$ and substituting the results in equation (24) of Chap. I. In the cases where calculations wcre not turncd over to the computer, they were gone over again after an intcrval of time and, when possible, tested. Increased accuracy in Inaking the calculations was always found whenever blocks of the same nature were performed together.

Section (ii). Method of solving the Linear Diferential Equations.

67. The cquation (24) of Chap.I. to be solved is

$$
( D + \mathrm { m } ) ^ { 2 } u _ { \lambda } + \mathcal { M } u _ { \lambda } + \bar { N } s _ { \lambda } \zeta ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { a } \lambda A \zeta = A ^ { \prime } ,
$$

Finally,it is shown that the solution of (i） when A is not zero can be put into the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { u _ { \lambda } = \frac { \Upsilon } { \mathrm { C } _ { 1 2 } } \left[ \mathfrak { u } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { s } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } + \mathfrak { u } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \overbar { \mathcal { A } } ^ { \prime } ) - \mathfrak { u } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { s } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } + \mathfrak { u } _ { 1 } \overbar { \mathcal { A } } ^ { \prime } ) \right] + \mathfrak { u } _ { 4 } \zeta \mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { s } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } + \mathfrak { u } _ { 3 } \overbar { \mathcal { A } } ^ { \prime } ) } \\ { - \mathfrak { u } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } \left\{ ( \mathrm { s } _ { 4 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } + \mathfrak { u } _ { 4 } \zeta \overbar { \mathcal { A } } ^ { \prime } ) - \mathbb { q } \mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { s } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } + \mathfrak { u } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \mathcal { A } ^ { \prime } ) \right\} \ \quad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots } \end{array}
$$

in which the bar over $A$ has the same meaning as before.

Tlis is the form required ； it will be noticed at once that most of th operations will consist of multiplications of series,and will therefore be i1 line with the work which was necessary for the calculation of $A$

68. It is advisable to make a few changes in order that the formula may be more compact. We first observe that for all purposes except the parts due to $\otimes$ , we rcquirc, not $u _ { \lambda }$ but $u _ { \lambda } / u _ { 0 }$ ; the parts due to $\otimes$ have the factor m²,and are therefore small,and tle multiplicatiol of u/uo by u is easicr than that of $u _ { \lambda }$ by $\mathfrak { 1 } / u _ { 0 }$ owing to the fact that $u _ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ has one coefficient unity. As there is no increase of troublc involved in finding ${ \boldsymbol { \mathit { l } } } { \boldsymbol { \mathit { l } } } _ { \lambda } / { \boldsymbol { \mathit { l } } } _ { 0 }$ we shall do so.

Write therefore-

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \mathrm { u } _ { 1 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { u } _ { 1 } / \zeta ^ { - 1 - \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } , ~ \mathrm { u } _ { 2 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { u } _ { 2 } / \zeta ^ { - 1 + \mathrm { \ c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } , ~ \mathrm { s } _ { 1 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { s } _ { 1 } / \zeta ^ { 1 - \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } , ~ \mathrm { s } _ { 2 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { s } _ { 2 } / \zeta ^ { 1 + \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } \cdots } \\ & { } & { \mathrm { u } _ { 3 } { = } \mathrm { u } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \zeta ^ { - 1 } , ~ \mathrm { s } _ { 3 } { = } \mathrm { s } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \zeta ~ \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots } \\ & { } & { \mathrm { U } _ { 1 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { a } \varepsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { u } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \zeta ^ { - \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } } { \mathrm { C } _ { 1 2 } u _ { 0 } } , ~ \mathrm { U } _ { 2 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { a } \epsilon _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { u } _ { 2 } / \zeta \mathrm { c } _ { \mathrm { o } } } { \mathrm { C } _ { 1 2 } u _ { 0 } } , ~ \mathrm { U } _ { 3 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { a u } _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } } { u _ { 0 } } , ~ \mathrm { U } _ { 4 } { = } \frac { \mathrm { a u } _ { 4 } \stackrel { \zeta } } { u _ { 0 } } . . . } \end{array}
$$

The calceulations are arranged as follows :-Tle series ${ \tt U } _ { 2 }$ ${ \bf \chi } _ { 3 } , \ { \bf S } _ { 2 } , \ { \bf S } _ { 3 } ,$ $\mathrm { S } _ { \phi }$ $\mathrm { U _ { 1 } , \ U _ { 2 } , \ U _ { 3 } , \ U _ { 4 } }$ are obtained once for all by multiplication or “ special values,” and the logaritlms of the coefficients written out on slips,as explained in Chap. I., Section (viii). The slips containing $A$ having been made out for each value of $\gimel$ ,the multiplications

$$
\mathrm { s } _ { 2 } A , \mathrm { u } _ { 2 } \overline { { A } } , \mathrm { s } _ { 3 } A , \mathrm { s } _ { 4 } A \quad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

are performed by tle computer, and thence the values of $\mathrm { Q } _ { \lambda } , \mathrm { Y } _ { \lambda } , \mathrm { T } _ { \lambda }$ are, by a few easy processes, obtaincd. The slips containing these latter are then made out, those for $\mathrm { Q } _ { \lambda }$ serving for $\left( \right) _ { \lambda }$ Finaliy thc multiplications

$$
\mathrm { U _ { 1 } Q _ { 3 } , U _ { 2 } Q _ { 3 } , U _ { 3 } T _ { 3 } , U _ { 4 } V _ { \lambda } } \quad , . . . \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots \qquad \ldots
$$

are performed by the compnter,and thence, by addition, tle value of $u _ { \lambda } / u _ { 0 }$ is obtained.

The series $A$ are of the form $\pmb { \Sigma } _ { i } p _ { i } \pmb { \zeta } ^ { \circ _ { i + \tau } } + \pmb { \Sigma } _ { i } p _ { i } \pmb { \zeta } ^ { \circ _ { i - \tau } }$ When $\boldsymbol { \mathfrak { T } }$ is not zero, the proc ss for each such double set involves sixteen multiplications of series of tl；form $( \Sigma _ { \mathcal { P } _ { 1 } } \Sigma ^ { : : } ) ( \Sigma _ { \mathcal { I } _ { i } } \Sigma ^ { : : } )$ .When $\tau = 0$ ,half the number suffices. When $\tau = \mathrm { c } ,$ a new part of the motion of the perigee is under consideration,and though the same proccss may be used, it is not convenient (see Section (iv) below).

When $\tau = 0$ and $_ { 2 i }$ is cven, it would appear that $\mathrm { { T } } _ { \lambda }$ gives rise to terms with the time as a factor. It is shown in the paper referred to at the beginning of tliis section tlat such terms can always be made to disappear. However, none sucl occur amongst the terms of the third order. In any case the method of this section will not be used for them,as the approximations in the ordinary method are rapid, owing to the absenee of any small divisors.

The same method may be applicd, using well-known formula, to the calculation of $z _ { \lambda }$ ,but it appeared casier,at any rate in the terms of the third order, to usc the method given in Chap. I.

The chief objection to.the method consists in the fact that the small coefficients which accompany large values of $i$ appear as differences between comparatively large numbers.Tlis faet does not impair the required accuracy of the results,but the multiplications arc much longer than they otherwisewouldbe.Thusthecocffcentof $\sum \limits _ { i = 1 } ^ { \infty \ldots + 6 - \infty } i ^ { 2 }$ in $\mathrm { u } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 3 } } / \mathrm { e } ^ { 3 } u _ { 0 }$ appears as follows :-

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l l l } & { { \mathrm { F r o n ~ U _ { 1 } Q _ { \cdot } } } } & { - \cdot \circ \circ \circ \circ 6 } & { 4 \operatorname { I } ^ { 8 } } \\ & { , } & { { \mathrm { U _ { 2 } } } { \overline { { \mathbb { Q } } } } , } & { + \cdot \circ \circ \circ \circ 6 } & { 4 9 6 } \\ & { , } & { { \mathrm { U _ { 3 } T _ { \cdot } } } } & { + \cdot \circ \circ \circ \circ \circ } & { 3 \operatorname { I } ^ { 6 } } \\ & { , } & { { \mathrm { U _ { 4 } V } } , } & { { \frac { - \cdot \circ \circ \circ \circ \circ \circ } { 3 \cdot \circ \circ \mathrm { 2 } } } } \\ & { { \mathrm { S u m } } } & & { + \cdot \circ \circ \circ \circ \circ \circ } & { \circ \circ 2 } \end{array} }
$$

On the other hand,as the prcsence of this diffculty can be shown to be peculiar to the method,it furnishes a means of detecting a certain class of error.

# Section (i). The Terms of Long Period.

72. Small divisors arise when the period of any term approximates to that of the principal elliptic term or when it is long. In the former case, the small divisor arises in the first and second terms of (8), being due to the operators $( D \pm \mathbf { c } _ { 0 } ) ^ { - 1 }$ . In the latter case, as was pointed out (Chap. I., $\ S \ : 2 8 \ : ( c ) )$ ， the square of the divisor occurs, but this diffculty may be avoided by the use of the homogeneous equation (21). I shall now show how the latter equation may be adapted when the methods of the last section are used.

The long-period small divisor arises only in $\mathrm { ~ V ~ } , ~ \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ， owing to the operator $D ^ { - 1 }$ But (omitting the suffix $\lambda$ for brevity)

$$
\mathrm { { T } = \it { D } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { { q } \mathrm { { V } - \mathrm { { W } ) , \mathrm { { V } = \it { D } ^ { - 1 } ( A \mathrm { { s } _ { 3 } + \mathrm { { \overline { { A } } \mathrm { { u } _ { 3 } ) , } } } } } } } } }
$$

$$

$$

of T. This is therefore the term to be considered.

The portion of （14） under the operator $\mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 }$ contains the factor ${ \mathfrak { m } } ^ { 2 }$ at least；hence the effect of the simall divisor (which is never of an order higher than $\mathrm { { m ^ { 2 } } }$ )is neutralised in the first term of $ { \boldsymbol { \Gamma } }$ .The same thing occurs with the remainder of the expression for $\mathrm { V }$ , owing to the fact that $\mathit { D u } _ { 0 } - \dot { u } _ { 0 }$ and $D s _ { 0 } + s _ { 0 }$ contain the factor ${ \mathfrak { M } } ^ { 2 }$ at least.Hence V can be found to the same degree of accuracy as $\mathcal { A }$ , and the loss of accuracy in ${ \mathcal { U } } _ { \lambda }$ is limited to that due to the first power of the small divisor.

In using this we first find $\lrcorner 1$ as usual,and with it calculate the coefficient of the particular power of $\boldsymbol { \check { \varsigma } }$ in thc sccond part of(13). The terms containing this power of $\boldsymbol { \varsigma }$ for the given value of $\gimel$ are then chosen out of the expression (14),and thence $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { V } }$ is obtained to the required accuraey for this power of $\boldsymbol { \check { \varsigma } }$ The method of Section (ii) serves for all the other powers of $\boldsymbol { \varsigma }$

Scction (iv). The Neu Purts of the Motions of the Perigee and Node.

73.The method of calculating $\mathrm { c } _ { \lambda , \mathfrak { e } }$ is explained in Chap. I., $\ S \ 2 8 \ ( b )$ ； when this quantity has been obtained $\lrcorner 1$ is completely known. Puting $\gimel _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ $= 7 . 0 ^ { 7 } ( 8 2 5 )$ and omitting one of the cquations for $i { = } 0$ ,we can solve the linear equations by continued approximation. The omitted equation serves as a test. Eitlier this or the method of Section (ii) somewhat modified, can be used, but it is more convenient to procced in the following manner.

In $\ S \ 2 8 \ ( b )$ we have put $\varLambda = \mathcal { B } +  { \mathbf { c } } _ { \mathrm { \lambda / e } } \textit { l }$ .Hence write the solution in tlc form

$$
\begin{array}{c} \begin{array} { r l } & { \lambda _ { i } = \phantom { \rho } _ { 1 } \lambda _ { i } + \lambda _ { 0 } ( f ) _ { i } + \mathfrak { c } _ { \lambda , \mathfrak { e } } ( c ) _ { i } , } \\ & { \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \phantom { \rho } _ { 1 } \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime } + \lambda _ { 0 } ( f ^ { \prime } ) _ { i } + \mathfrak { c } _ { \lambda ^ { \prime } \mathfrak { e } } ( c ^ { \prime } ) _ { i } , } \end{array} \bigg ] \quad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdots \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot  \end{array}
$$

except for $i { = } 0$

Herc $\hat { \mathbf { \varepsilon } } _ { 1 } \lambda _ { i j } \mathbf { \varepsilon } _ { 1 } \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ,are obtained by solving equation (26) of Chap. I. with $\mathcal { A } _ { i } { = } \mathcal { B } _ { i }$ $A _ { i } ^ { \prime } { = } B _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\lambda _ { 0 } = 0 = \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$

The terms $( f ) _ { i } \lambda _ { 0 } , ( f ^ { \prime } ) _ { i } \lambda _ { 0 }$ are obtained by solving thc same equation with $A _ { i } { = } { \circ } { = } A _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\lambda _ { 0 } { = } \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ( \ S 2 5 ) .$ ,in terms of $\gimel _ { 0 }$ (If, instead of $\lambda _ { 0 } = { \lambda _ { 0 } } ^ { \prime }$ ,we had put $\mathfrak { I } _ { 0 } - \mathfrak { I } _ { \mathfrak { v } _ { 0 } } / = \mathfrak { I }$ ， we should have found $\varepsilon _ { i } , \ \Xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } .$ ）

The terms $\begin{array} { r l } { ( \mathrm { \it ~ c ~ } ) _ { i 9 } } & { { } \left( \mathrm { \it ~ c ~ } ^ { \prime } \right) _ { i } } \end{array}$ are obtained by solving with $A _ { i } { = } b _ { i } , \quad A _ { i } ^ { \prime } { = } b _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ $\lambda _ { 0 } = 0 = \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$

In all three cascs thc cquations for $i { = } 0$ are omitted.

We now substitute the values of $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime } .$ thus obtained, in the two equations for $i = 0$ and obtain two equations of the form

$$
\begin{array}{c} \begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \lambda ^ { 1 } \mathrm { _ 0 + } ~ } }  { \lambda } _ { 0 } ( f _ { 0 } ) +  { \mathrm { c } } _ { \mathrm { _ \mathrm { + } , e } } ( c _ { 0 } ) = \infty } \\ { \mathrm { _ 1 }  { \lambda ^ { \prime } \mathrm { _ 0 + } }  { \lambda } _ { 0 } ( f _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) +  { \mathrm { c } } _ { \mathrm { _ \lambda ^ { \prime } e } } ( c _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 } \end{array} \biggr \} \begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ 0 } ~ } } \\ { \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda { 0 } } ~ } } } \end{array} \cdots \qquad \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda { 0 } ~ } } } \quad \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda \mathrm { _ \lambda { + } } } }  \end{array}
$$

in which cverything is known except $\gamma _ { \bullet _ { 0 } }$ If the work be eorrect these should give the same value of $\lambda _ { \mathfrak { d } }$ and thence, substituting in $\left( \mathfrak { I } \mathfrak { J } \right)$ ,the values of $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { i } ^ { \prime }$

The coefficients $( \dot { f } ) _ { i \bullet } ( \dot { f } ^ { \prime } ) _ { i \bullet } ( c ) _ { i \bullet } ( c ^ { \prime } ) _ { i }$ being independent of $\gimel$ ,are found once for all ；they are given below. The advantage of this procedure arises from the omission of the equations for $\scriptstyle { \dot { \imath } } = 0$ in the solution of the linear equations : the approximations are rapid.

The same process is used for the nodal motion with the $\tilde { \cdot }$ equation. But as we here put $\lambda _ { 0 } { = } 0 { = } \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \ ( \ S \ 2 6 ) ,$ , the equations (15),(16) reduce to

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \lambda _ { i } = \mathrm { _ { 1 } } \lambda _ { i } + \mathrm { g } _ { \lambda \mathrm { ~ k } } ( g ) _ { i } , ( \mathrm { e x c e p t ~ } i = \circ ) \qquad \quad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad ( \mathrm { _ { 1 5 } } ) ^ { \prime } } \\ { \mathrm { _ { 1 } } \lambda _ { 0 } + \mathrm { g } _ { \lambda / \mathrm { k } } ( g _ { 0 } ) = \circ \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad \dots \qquad ( \mathrm { _ { 1 6 } } ) ^ { \prime } } \end{array}
$$

The equation (16) serves then as a test. In all coefficients arising in the $\tilde { \downarrow }$ -equation we have $q _ { i } = - q ^ { \prime } _ { - i }$

Theory of the Motion of the Moon； containing a New Calculation of the Eapressions for the Coordinates of the Moon in Terms of the Time. By ERNEsT W. BROWN, Sc.D., F.R.S.

[Received 19oo May 25 ; read 190o June 8.]

PART III. CHAPTER VI.

Is the first two parts of this Memoir, publishcd under the same title in the Memoir's of the Royal Astronomical Society in 1897, 1899, the general theory and the numerical results,up to and inclusive of the tlird-order terms, lave been given. This part contains tlc numerical results for the terms of the fourth order.

The methods adopted are in general the same as those used for the third-order terms. Instead of finding the values of ${ \it \Omega } \cdot { \it \Omega } u _ { \lambda } / { \it \Omega } u _ { 0 }$ directly and then deducing thosc of $u _ { \lambda }$ as in the third-order terms,I have found the valucs of $u _ { \lambda }$ directly. This clange was found advisable when preparations were being made for the calculation of the fiftlordcr tcrms. It was sccn that the non-homogeneous cquations (17), (18) of Chap. I. would involve much more calculation than the homogeneous equations (6), (7), (8) of the same chapter,and the latter require the results for $\mathcal { U } _ { \lambda }$ and not those for $u _ { \lambda } / u _ { 0 }$ In the former case ${ \mathit { t } } _ { } / { \mathit { I } } ^ { 3 }$ has to be expanded to the ffth ordcr,an enormous piece of work ；in thc latter case wc only requirc the calculation of such exprcssions as $u ^ { 2 }$ ， uDs,&c., to the ffth order,and this has beer so arranged as to require much less computation. The only other change from the methods of Chap. V. is that mentioned in $\ S 7 ^ { \mathrm { I } }$

As in the earlier work all the assistancc I have had in performing the computations has bcen rendered by Mr. IRA I. STERNER, A.M., and I take this opportunity of again expressing my obligations to him for the ability and accuracy with which he has conducted the work allotted to him.\* I have also done a considerable amount of caleulation myself, especially in the later portions of the work.

The following is the table of contents of Chap. V.:-

Section (i). A brief outline of the steps follwed in the application of the general method to the terms of the fourth order.

Section (ii). The final numerical results for the serics &, A,and tor tne coeffcients of all terms of the fourth order in u, z.

# Scction (i). Formule and Method of Procedure.

96. The method cmployed is in general the same as that outlined in }66. 1ne products in the third line of equation (17)and in the second line of (18)of Chap.I. are formed by putting

$$
u _ { \mu } = u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + u _ { 3 } , \quad s _ { \mu } = s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } + s _ { 3 } , \quad \tilde { { \bf \alpha } } _ { \mu } = \tilde { { \bf \alpha } } _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } + z _ { 3 } ,
$$

and choosing the parts of the fourth ordcr. The products in the fourth and fifth lines of(17）and in the third line of （18） are similarly obtained with

$$
u _ { \mu } = u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } , ~ s _ { \mu } = s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } , ~ z _ { \mu } = z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } .
$$

The additional parts of(17), (18） in which we put

$$
u _ { \mu } = u _ { \ ; } , ~ s _ { , } = s _ { 1 } , ~ \approx _ { \mu } = \stackrel { \pi } { s } _ { 1 } ,
$$

are respectively

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad \cdot \left\{ \frac { 3 5 } { 1 2 8 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { u _ { 0 } } } } \right) ^ { 4 } + \frac { 3 1 5 } { 1 2 8 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { s _ { 0 } } } } \right) ^ { 4 } + \frac { 1 5 } { 3 2 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { u _ { 0 } } } } \right) ^ { 3 } \frac { \sum \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } { \vartheta _ { 0 } } + 3 5 \left( \frac { \sum \vec { s } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } { s _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 3 } \frac { \sum \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } { u _ { 0 } } + \frac { 4 5 } { 6 4 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { u _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { s _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } } _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \frac { \sum \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } { \vartheta _ { 0 } } \displaystyle \sum \left[ \frac { \sum \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } { u _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 5 } { 1 6 } \left( \overline { { \stackrel { \sum } { s _ { \vec { \kappa } _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 4 5 } { 8 } \frac { \sum { u _ { \vec { \kappa } } } } { u _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { \sum s _ { \vec { \kappa } } } { s _ { 0 } } \right] + \frac { 1 5 } { 8 } \left( \frac { \sum s _ { \vec { \kappa } } } { \sigma _ { 0 } } \right) ^ { 4 } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\small \frac { \kappa } { \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \Bigg [ - \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } ( \beta ) \tilde { \varsigma } } { \rho _ { 0 } } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \alpha _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { 1 6 \big ( \kappa _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \big ) } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 3 5 } { 1 6 } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } { s _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } + \frac { 4 5 } { 1 6 } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { u _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { s _ { 0 } } + \frac { 4 5 } { 1 6 } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } { s _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { u _ { 0 } } \Bigg ) + \frac { 1 5 } { 4 } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } { \rho _ { 0 } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } \Big ( \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { u _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sum _ { \tilde { \alpha } _ { \beta _ { \varepsilon } } } } { s _ { 0 } } \Big ) \Bigg ] .

Tho mcanings of the various symbols have been explained in Chap. I.

C $\frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { s } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } , - \frac { \imath } { \imath } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { z } } .$ are treated in like manncr. The parts arising from $\Omega$ ,namely,

In tle parts of equations (17),(18),

$$
\zeta ^ { - 1 } \left( D ^ { 2 } + \mathsf { z m } D \right) \Sigma _ { \mathsf { \ell m } } , \qquad - D ^ { 2 } \Sigma _ { \mathsf { \ell m } }
$$

we substitute

$$
u _ { \mu } { = } u _ { 2 } , \qquad z _ { \mu } { = } { \tilde { \sigma } } _ { 2 } ,
$$

and proceed as follows : in those parts of the arguments of uz,  which contain c, g wc must use thc values of c,g to.the second order (that is, we must retain the parts of these two quantities which depend on e², e²,k²,α²) ；when the operations D²,D have been performed the portions of the fourth order must be retained. In all other operations the parts co, go of c, g will be sufficient.

The general procednre in performing the calculations and the methods o1 tesung the results are in other respects tlie same as those explained in § 66.

97. The serics finally obtained before proceeding to tle solution of the linear differential cquations are not, in the case of equation (17)，the actual right-hand members,but series $\mathfrak { Y }$ ,where

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A { = } \frac { 3 } { 4 } \frac { \kappa u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } \mathfrak { X } . } \end{array}
$$

The reasons for this have been stated in $\ S 7 ^ { 1 }$ (the symbol $\mathfrak { X }$ is there denoted by $\boldsymbol { A } _ { 1 } ^ { \phantom { + } }$ ).

In consequence of this change the series s, uz, \$, \$4 (§§ 68-7O) must be replaced bv series ,u, ,, where

$$
{ \mathfrak { u } } _ { 2 } { = } \frac { { 3 } } { 4 } \frac { \kappa \mathcal { U } _ { 0 } { \zeta } ^ { - 1 } } { \rho _ { 0 } { ^ 3 } } { u } _ { 2 } , \hat { \mathfrak { s } } _ { \rho } { = } \frac { { 3 } } { 4 } \frac { \kappa \mathcal { U } _ { 0 } { \zeta } ^ { - 1 } } { \rho _ { 0 } { ^ 3 } } { \overset { \le } { _ 3 } } { = } { } \mathrm { s } _ { p }
$$

$$
( p = 2 , 3 , 4 ) .
$$

Further,as we shall find ux&-1/a directly,instead of finding ux/uo frst, we use series U1, U, u, u4 instead of the series U,U, U, U, where

$$
\mathfrak { U } _ { p } = \frac { u _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } { \mathfrak { a } } \mathbf { U } _ { p }
$$

The coefficicnts of $\iota _ { \tilde { \mathbf { \Gamma } } ^ { \prime } \tilde { \mathbf { \Gamma } } ^ { \prime } } ^ { * } \big / \mathfrak { a } \lambda$ change their signs when the corresponding arguments cliange their signs.

The following long-period terms have been obtained with tlie required accnracy by the mcthod of Section (iii), Chap. V. :-

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { \pm ( - 2 + 2 \mathrm { c } ) , \qquad } } & { { \left( \cdot ^ { 3 } \mathrm { c } ^ { \prime } \right) _ { - 1 9 } \left( \varepsilon \varepsilon ^ { \prime 3 } \right) _ { \mathrm { r } } } } \\ { { \pm ( - 2 + 2 \mathrm { c } - 2 \mathrm { m } ) , } } & { { \left( \varepsilon ^ { \prime } \eta ^ { \prime \prime } \right) _ { - 1 9 } \left( \varepsilon ^ { \prime \prime } { h } ^ { 2 } \right) _ { \mathrm { r } } } } \\ { { \pm ( \mathrm { z c } - 2 \mathrm { g } ) , } } & { { \left( \varepsilon ^ { \prime } k ^ { \prime \prime } \right) _ { 0 } \left( \varepsilon ^ { \prime \prime } k ^ { \prime } \right) _ { 0 } } } \\ { { \pm ( \mathrm { z } + 2 \mathrm { m } - 2 \mathrm { g } ) , } } & { { \left( \eta ^ { \prime } k ^ { \prime \prime } \right) _ { \mathrm { s } } \left( \eta ^ { \prime \prime } k ^ { \prime } \right) _ { - \mathrm { s } } } } \end{array}
$$

The values of $\mathrm { V } _ { \lambda }$ for these tcrms, obtained by the method of Section (ii), Chap. V., agreed,as far as they went, with the values obtained by Scction (ii); this agreemcnt furnished a valuable test.

In tle solution of the linear differential equations tlose sets of terms with arguments 2i have no small divisors ; the continued approximation method was, therefore, employed for sucl terms instead of tle method. of Section (i), Chap. V.\* The continued approximation method was also employed in the solution of the differential equations for the terms with characteristics c"α, e²α², e'a²， k²α², and for all the terms in $\tilde { \mathcal { T } }$

The numerical results now follow. The values of Qx for the terms arising in u are given in two parts--those arising from tle expansion of xuγ-1/r3, denoted by S, and those arising from $- \int _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } ( D ^ { 2 } + 2 \mathrm { u } D ) \Sigma \mathcal { U } _ { \mu } + \frac { \hat { \sigma } \Omega _ { 1 } | } { \hat { \sigma } \textit { s } } \int$ ,denoted by ${ \mathfrak { L } } _ { \lambda }$ Then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathfrak { U } _ { \lambda } = \mathfrak { K } _ { \lambda } + \mathfrak { L } _ { \lambda } = \frac { { 4 \rho _ { 0 } } ^ { 3 } } { 3 \nu \mathfrak { u } _ { 0 } } \zeta ^ { - 1 } \mathfrak { A } _ { \lambda } \qquad \quad \widehat { \mathfrak { N } } _ { \lambda } = \frac { { 4 ^ { { \rho _ { 0 } } } } ^ { 3 } } { 3 \nu \mathfrak { u } _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } \mathfrak { L } ^ { \kappa \lambda } , } \\ { \mathfrak { L } _ { \lambda } = \frac { { 4 { \rho _ { 0 } } } ^ { 3 } } { 3 \cdot \mathfrak { u } _ { 0 } \zeta ^ { - 1 } } \times \mathrm { p a r t , ~ c l a r a c t e r i s t i c } \lambda , \mathrm { ~ i n - ~ } \left\{ ( { D ^ { 2 } } + { 2 \mathrm { m } } D ) \Sigma \mathfrak { u } _ { * } + \frac { \widehat { \sigma } \Omega _ { 1 } } { \widehat { \sigma } \mathfrak { s } } \right\} \zeta ^ { - 1 } \ , } \end{array}
$$

where $A _ { \lambda }$ is the right-hand member of equation (17), Chap. I.

\* The method for obtaining the results given in the section referred to has been published in the Camb.Phil. Trans.vol. xvii.pp. 94-Io6,under the title,“On the Solution of a Pair of Simultaneous Linear Differential Equations,which occur in the Lunar Theory."

In the case of $z$ wehave

$$
{ \cal A } _ { \lambda } { = } { \cal K } _ { \lambda } { + } { \cal L } _ { \lambda } ,
$$

where Axis the right-hand member of equation (18), Chap.I., Kx is the part arising from the expansion of xz/r,and Lx is the part arising from the expansion of $- D ^ { 2 } \pmb { \Sigma } _ { \alpha _ { \mu } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathbf { \Sigma } _ { \partial _ { \mathcal { Z } } } ^ { \partial \Omega } .$

The numbers are the coefficients corresponding to the power of ζ(that is, the argument) which is placed at the head of each eolumn cr pair of columns.

The suffix of i is omitted in thc tables. Further details concerning the resuits will be found in Chaps. I.-1V.
