Haverford Colleye, Pu.,U.S.A.: 1900 May 14.

Theory of the Motion of the Moon； containing a New Calculation of the Expressions for the Coordinates of the Moon in terms of the Time. By ERNEsT W. BRoWN, M.A., Sc.D., F.R.S.

[Received 1905 January 21 ; read 1905 March 10.]

THE previous parts of this memoir have been published in the Memoirs of the Royal lstronomical Society under the same title in 1897,1899,19oo. The solution of the problem undertaken-the motion of the Moon as disturbed by tle Sun supposed to move in a fixed eliptic orbit-is completed in the present part.

It was stated in the introduction to Part I.that the main object in view was a new and accurate calculation of every coeffcient in longitude, latitude, and parallax which is as great as one-hundredth of a second of arc, the result not to be in error by more than this amount. So far as I am able to see this plan has been carried out. A careful examination of the magnitude of the coefficients which would arise with characteristics higher than those calculated herc, and a comparison with the results of DELAUNAY and HANsEN, seem to show that no charactcristics which would give coefficients so great as $\mathrm { { 0 ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot _ { \mathrm { { O 1 } } } } } }$ have been omitted. There are possibly four or five terms whose characteristics are of the sixth order, which approach o"o1 quite closcly. These omissions are, however, quite nnimportant from a practical point of view. In a comparison between theory and observation a few such terms produce nothing sensible in the differences ； it is only when the number of them is great that any cffect is shown.

The coeficients in longitude and latitude in every characteristic calculated have actually bcen found to $\mathrm { \odot ^ { \prime \prime . } { o o I } }$ , and in parallax to $\mathrm { { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { { \cdot } _ { 0 0 0 I } } }$ . In the former case a large number of coefficients between $\mathrm { { 0 ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot _ { \mathrm { { O 1 } } } } } }$ and $\mathrm { { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { { O O 1 } } } }$ are included；and, in fact,it is not diffculto see that there are comparatively few coeffcients lying between $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { O O } 2 }$ and $\mathrm { { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot _ { \mathrm { { O I } } } } }$ which are not included in the tables at the end of Chapter IX.Thus the thcory， for purposes of comparison with observation,is considerably more accurate than was contemplated in the original plan. Moreover,a similar remark applies to the coefficients in parallax lying betwecn $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { O O O } 2 }$ and $\mathrm { \Omega } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \bullet } _ { \mathrm { O O I } }$ ； thus the parallax of the Moon can certainly be found theoretically from the new table within o"o1,so far as the solar perturbations are concerned.

To complete the whole problem of the lunar motion, inequalities arising from other sources have still to be considered. These consist of the very minute terms arising from the parts of the solar disturbing function which are noted in Chap. I. 4, the terms arising from the figure of the Earth,and perhaps from that of the Moon, the indirect planetary inequalities and the direct planetary inequalities. The last is the only set which presents serious difficulties at the present time,and an investigation of them has already been started. HiLL's work \* on the inequalities produced by the figure of the Earth probably needs but little supplementing,while a new method+ for investigating indirect planetary inequalities should render the task of calculating their coeffcients comparatively easy.

An important question which cannot be lcft aside is that of the accuracy of the computations by which the results have been obtained. It appears unlikely that the problem will be again completely solved in the near future, and some assurance is needed that the new coefficients, espeecially where they differ from those of HANsEN or DELAUNAY,are the correct ones. Fortunately the three theories are so entirely independent in their methods that agreement between them all amounts to practical certainty. The differences between HANsEN and DELAUNAY have given rise to much discussion in the past. In general my theory confirms the results of DELAUNAY in these cases ； the coefficients in which all three theories differ are those which are diffcult to determine owing to the presence of very small divisors. The older methods,which approximate along powers of m,are theoretically less likely to be correct than a method which approximates along powers of the other parameters where the convergence is quite rapid. This, however, is a rather different question than that of the actual accuracy of the numerical work. For the latter very numerous tests have been used, covering almost every detail, as well as large masses of calculations. These tests are discussed below in Chap. VII. Sect. (iv),as well as in other papers to which reference will there be found.

To return to the special part of the work now published. For the terms of the fifth order in u the homogeneous equations were used for the first time. In spite of the fact that this was a change of method involving much extra work the computations were thus kept within reasonable bounds ； the expansion of ku/r’ would have nearly doubled the actual work done. The non-homogeneous equation was still used for z,as most of the multiplications had been obtained in calculating the fourth order terms. But for the sixth order in both u, $z$ the non-homogeneous equations were used,and the work was much less than had been expected.

The final steps consisted in the transformation to polar coordinates, the change of the arbitrary constants to the DELAUNAY system, and the insertion of their numerical values to reducc the results to seconds of arc. For the first of these the formulae were found to permit of such arrangements that much previous work could be usefully utilised. In the final reduction to numbers， values for the constants were used which were neither those of HANsEN nor those of DELAUNAY. A selection was made from modern determinations which I believe will be found to be very close to the more accurate values to be found when a thorough comparison of the completed theory with the observations has been undertaken.

Owing mainly to the complicated character of the work which is embodied in the results below I have been obliged to do much more actual calculation myself than heretofore. All computations which could with advantage be turned over to a computer have again becn done by Mr. IRA I. STERNER， A.M.\* His speed and accuracy have been fully maintained,and have contributed in no small degree to an earlier conclusion of the work than I had hoped. He has in all spent some three thousand hours on these calculations, extended over seven and a half years ；my own share I estimate at five or six thousand hours since the work was begun on a complete plan in 1895.

The following is the table of contents for the whole memoir :-

Chapter I.-General Development of the Theory.

Section (i). An investigation of the disturbing function used, with the necessary corrections.

Section (ii). The two forms of the equations of motion.

Section (i). Development of the disturbing function according to powers of $\mathbb { i } / a ^ { \prime } , z , \ell ^ { \prime }$

Section (iv). Thc form of the solution. The general system of notation adopted to represent the coefficients, arguments, &c.

$$
\overset { \partial } { \partial g } ( D \widetilde { s } _ { 2 } ) = \widetilde { s } _ { 2 } { ' } , \overset { \partial } { \partial g } ( D z _ { 1 } ) = \widetilde { s } _ { 1 } { ' } , \widetilde { s } _ { 1 } { ' } D z _ { 2 } + z _ { 2 } { ' } D \widetilde { s } _ { 1 } = z _ { 1 } { ' } D z _ { 2 } - \widetilde { s } _ { 1 } \mathbf { D } z _ { 2 } { ' } + D ( z _ { 1 } \widetilde { s } _ { 2 } { ' } ) .
$$

$$

Determination of ${ \bf C } _ { 4 }$ -In those terms of the ffth order containing $\zeta ^ { 2 i \pm \mathrm { c } }$ a new part, ${ \mathrm { G } } _ { 4 }$ ,of c arises for dctermination, and one of the unknown coeffcients is indetcrminate ； the definition of thc latter $( \ S 2 5 )$ is such that thc coefficients of $\zeta ^ { \pm \mathfrak { c } }$ in $\mathcal { U } _ { 5 } \zeta ^ { - 1 }$ are to be equal. however,be quite differently formed from those for the other unknowns. The main reason for this is that in the case of ζ=° we havc

$$

since co is put for c after thc opcration F-1 (β 13O)； the divisor is therefore very small. If all powers of m had bcen included in F the divisor would have been zero and approximation impossiblc. Hence the formula must bc so arranged that F-1 is not present.

giving

137.These are well adaptcd for solution by continued approximation.Equating the coeffieients of ° to zero in each of them, it is to be notiecd that the unknowns in A', B", ∑(uasb-saus) have the faetor m² at lcast (§ 132). Thc first approximation completed, the resulting valucs arc used for us, ss to obtain a second approximation to C4 from (16) and to λ from (17),and then to the other unkliowns from § 132，and so on.

There is less disturbance of the computation sheets than would appear. The first approximation to A is found, exactly as in § 132, by omitting all unknowns. In that to B we omit all unknowns and the term $2 { \bigl ( } 1 + \mathrm { m } { \bigr ) } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \mathrm { F } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { A }$ in the coeficient of $\xi ^ { \mathrm { { c } } }$ and then all the quantities for (16), (17) are to hand.A diference oceurs in the second approximation, due to the fact that

138. Small Divisors-The operators $\mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } , ~ \mathbb { F } ^ { - 1 }$ introduce small divisors in the cases of long-period and monthly terms, respectively ; each set of terms whose arguments differ by 2i involves one of the former or two of the latter. As the basis of the continued approximation was the ability to neglect terms having m² as a factor, approximation may become impossible, or is at least very slow, when the small divisor is of order $\mathbf { m ^ { 2 } }$ The method used for this case is the same as that of $\ S$ 29.

Taking the case of a long-period term, suppose that the corresponding coefficients are $\lambda _ { 0 }$ ， ${ \lambda } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ .The first approximation to the other $\lambda _ { i } , \lambda _ { - i } ^ { \prime }$ is first obtained with $\lambda _ { 0 } , { \lambda _ { 0 } } ^ { \prime }$ considered as unknowns, so that they are expressed as linear functions of λo, λ and a known part. The first approximation to $\lambda _ { 0 } , \lambda _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ^ { \prime }$ is then obtained by using these values of X, X_: in us, 9us, &c., instead of neglecting them. The process thus leads to two new simultaneous linear equations for $\lambda _ { 0 } ; { \lambda _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } .$ When these are solved $\mathbf { a }$ substitution gives the first approximation to the other unknowns. In the application of this rule it was found suffcient to determine $\lambda _ { \pm 1 } , \lambda _ { \mp 1 } ^ { \prime } ,$ in terms of $\lambda _ { 0 } , { \lambda _ { 0 } } ^ { \prime } $ ,a second approximation giving the required aceuracy， and the other coefcients being determined by thc ordinary method.

For the two monthly terms, corresponding to coefficients $\lambda _ { 0 } .$ , ${ \lambda } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ , $\lambda _ { - 1 } , \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ ,the same process was followed with respect to that pair of them, say $\lambda _ { 0 } , { \lambda _ { 0 } } ^ { \prime }$ , which had a divisor containing $\mathbf { m ^ { 2 } }$ as a factor ; the divisor of the other pair has then only m asa factor.

Section(iv). Nature of the Computations. Tests for Accuracy.

140. The Computations for $u _ { \lambda } , s _ { \lambda }$ -The equations, prepared for computation, have been given in $\ S _ { \textbf { I } 3 ^ { 1 } }$ The principal part of the labour is the formation of the products

$$
u _ { a } u _ { b } , u _ { a } s _ { b } , \widehat { \triangledown } u _ { a } . \widehat { \triangledown } s _ { b } , u _ { a } \widehat { \triangledown } s _ { b } , D z _ { a } . D z _ { b } , z _ { a } z _ { b } , ( a = 4 , 3 ; \widehat { b } = 5 - a )
$$

in which $s _ { b } = \overline { { u } } _ { b } ,$ $9 \mathcal { S } _ { \mathcal { b } } = - \sqrt { \mathcal { A } u _ { \mathcal { b } } }$ .

For calculating $\mathrm { { \mathbf { L } } } ^ { \prime }$ , $\mathbf { A } ^ { \prime }$ $\{ \ S _ { \textrm { I } 3 3 } )$ we require the products where $a + b \leq 4$ in the first three and $a + b + c \leq 4$ in the last two. Most of these, however,are deducible by briefer multiplications from the results obtained in calculating the fourth order terms for which the functions $\Sigma _ { } { u _ { a } } { u _ { b } } / { u _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 }$ , $\Sigma { u _ { a } } { s _ { b } } / u _ { 0 } { s _ { 0 } } ,$ &c., were obtained； the multiplications of these by ${ u _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 }$ , $u _ { 0 } s _ { 0 } ,$ &c., are easy on account of the rapid convergence of all functions of $u _ { 0 } , s _ { 0 } .$ ,

For computing 8f,8（Df） （134) we require thefirst,third,andfthof thesehadbeenpreviouslyobtained.Thc produts in $\ S 1 3 5$ which contain an accented $z$ are deducible by mere changes of sign from those formed without an accent.

Of the remaining calculations which do not consist of additions or subtractions there are multiplieations of series by constant factors (e.g.G by ${ \bf \frac { 3 } { 2 } m ^ { 2 } }$ inA)The operation $\mathcal { D }$ consists in multiplying each coeffcient by the corresponding index of &, while the operations $D ^ { - 1 }$ D-2 F-1consist of similar divisions.In many cases the factors are small and the divisors greater than unity, while the number of significant figures is small, so that these operations can frequently be done without the use of logarithms. It is, in fact, much easier to divide mentally bya number of,say，three figures than to multiply by such $\mathbf { a }$ number.CRELLE's Or TAMBORREL's tabIes might have been used for both operations. As a matter offaet these aids were not employed. I believe that where the numbers to be multiplied consist of only three significant figures, and do not run at all consecutively,it takes lesstime to use a four-place table oflogarithms (which can be mounted on a single card) than to be obliged to turn over the pages ofa bulky volume of multiplication tables.

141. The Computations for $\mathscr { z }$ -The processes were the same as those of previous chapters. Of the $\kappa _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } , \textit { B } _ { i }$ allbut $\kappa _ { \mathrm { ~ \scriptsize ~ 4 9 ~ } } ^ { \prime \prime } B _ { \scriptscriptstyle 4 }$ had been previously found; $\kappa _ { \mathrm { ~ 4 ~ } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ is obtained from the brief series multiplications of $z _ { 4 }$ by $- 3 \kappa / 2 \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 3 }$ while all the products and most of the sums $\mathrm { i n } \ \mathcal { B } _ { 4 }$ had been obtained $\operatorname { i n } \cdot$ compnting ${ _ { \circ } } \mathcal { U } _ { 4 }$ The only multiplications remaining were the $\kappa ^ { \prime \prime } { } _ { i } \mathcal { B } _ { 5 - i } ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 )$ ,and the uz i $\mathrm { ~ r ~ } \partial \otimes _ { \mathrm { i } } / \partial { z } _ { \mathrm { i } }$ most of which latter products were at hand. The long-period terms do not produce small divisions, and the initial number of places of decimals adopted was such that special methods for the monthly terms were not required. The approximations also were suffciently rapid.

142. Tests.-All multiplications and additions were tested by the addition test explained in Chap. I., Sect. vii. Practically every operation comes under one of these heads. The apparent exceptions are the operations $\mathcal { D }$ , $\mathcal { D } ^ { - 1 } , \mathbb { F } ^ { - 1 }$ For $\mathcal { D }$ wehave

If we put $\pmb { \zeta } = \mathbf { i }$ we obtain a test easy to compute, since $\dot { \iota }$ is $\mathbf { a }$ small integer. The same process performed on the result tests for $D ^ { - 1 }$ and a slight extension enables us to test similarly for $\mathrm { F } ^ { - 1 }$ ,,

This method works well for the details of the calculations. The calculations en masse can be verified by going back to the differential equations and using the above method for the operator D with ζ= 1. This was not used very extensively on the ffth-order terms,for reasons which will appear in the rest of this section. Such a test is very laborious and it seemed hardly necessary to make it.

143.An indirect test in fact arises with every set of terms with a given characteristic. Each sueh set is u,s,and every other set in ε has one (long-period) or two (monthly) terms whose coefficients possess small divisors, and the process of division by these ispractically the last step in the determination of thecoeffcents. Now,in thefifth-order terms the chief danger of error arises not in the details of the calculations, which can be tested by the method just deseribed, but inthe possible omission ofa whole set of terms, or in the use of a wrong set, owing to the very large number of sets tobe dealt with.It is just such errors that would be detected,for even though they miglit not be very large thenumbers which go to makeup the longperiod or monthly terms are always formed of the differences of numbers large in comparison with their algebraical sum, so that an error, after the process of division by a small divisor, may cause a coefficient to appear sevcral times its aetual valuc ；a rough comparison with DELAUNAY's or HANsEN's rcsults Would rcveal the error at once. It must be remembered that such errors would practically run through the whole series of ealculations,but would be mainly revealed by a few terms. Thus one of the chief causes of the extent of the caleulations furnishes the most valuable test of their accuracy. Even if DELAUNAY's results were not available the test would often still work,owing to the faet that negative powers of m cannot be prescnt,and therefore that the maximum order of magnitude of any coeffcient can be roughly stated in advance.\*

144. A Special Set of Tests.-In a paper + published in 1896 I gave some cxtensions of ADAMs's theorems which connected the mean motions of the perigee and node, not only with the eonstant term of the parallax but with one another. In fact, equations werc obtained relating the parts of e,charaeteristics $\mathrm { k ^ { 2 } }$ ， $\mathrm { c ^ { 2 } k ^ { 2 } }$ ， $\mathrm { k } ^ { 4 }$ ， ${ \it e } ^ { \prime \mathrm { { a } } } \mathrm { { k } ^ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } }$ rcspeetively,with those of g, eharaeteristics c², e4,c²k²,e²2. The former set is determined in connection with $u$ from the equations for $u , \ \cdot \ \left( \ S ^ { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } } 3 ^ { 6 } \right)$ ，the latter sct from the non-homogeneous equation for z. Therc were thus four separate tests from these relations. Further, the coeffcients of certain characteristies in the constant term in the expression for the parallax are related by separate equations to parts of the motions of the perigee and node ; these charaeteristies in the parallax are $\mathrm { e ^ { 2 } , k ^ { 2 } , \ c ^ { 4 } , e ^ { 2 } c ^ { 2 } , \ k ^ { 2 } \ell ^ { 2 } , k ^ { 4 } , e ^ { 2 } k ^ { 2 } }$ ，furnishing seven other tests which， however， with the exception of the first two， are not very searching, owing to the small number of places of decimals used in the results for the fourth-order characteristics of the parallax.

The relations in question are ineluded in the equations

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \beta _ { 2 } \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } \pi _ { 1 } } { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } \mathbf { e } ^ { 2 } } + \beta _ { 3 } \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } \theta _ { 1 } } { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } \mathbf { e } ^ { 2 } } = \vdots \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } } { \hat { \partial } \mathbf { e } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { E + M } { r } \right) ^ { \circ } } & { { } \quad \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad } & { ( \mathbf { 1 } \otimes ) , } \\ { \beta _ { 2 } \hat { \omega } _ { 1 } + \beta _ { 3 } \frac { \hat { \partial } \theta _ { 1 } } { \hat { \partial } \mathbf { k } ^ { 2 } } = \vdots \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \omega } } } { \hat { \partial } \mathbf { k } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { E + M } { r } \right) ^ { \circ } } & { { } \quad \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad } & { \ldots \quad } & { ( \mathbf { 1 } \boldsymbol { \omega } ) , } \end{array}
$$

wherc $\beta _ { 1 } , \ \beta _ { 2 } , \ \beta _ { 3 } \ ( c _ { 1 } , \ c _ { 2 } , \ c _ { 3 }$ in my prcvious papers） is the sct of canonical constants coinplementary to the constants giving the position of the mcan Moon, its perigee and its node at time $t { = } \infty$ ； $\pi _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 1 }$ are the mean motions of the perigee and node ；and $( \mathcal { Q } ) ^ { \mathfrak { \mathrm { 0 } } }$ represents the mean value of a function $Q$

145.I have already developed \$ a inethod for thc calculation of $\beta _ { 3 } , \beta _ { 3 }$ solely from the coefficients in $u , s , z$ .The following are the rcsults :

146. Differentiate (18) with respet to $k ^ { 2 }$ and (19） with respect to $\mathrm { e } ^ { 2 }$ ； the two Ieft-hand members should then be equal. Calculate the terms with characteristics I, e², k²， $e ^ { \prime 2 }$ in these members ; the results, which should be the same, give the following differences :

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \qquad ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = - \cdots \circ \circ 6 _ { 3 } 7 \ 8 9 4 { \textrm { \textmu } } \Biggr \} { \mathrm { d i f f . } } = { \textrm { \ o } \ o \ o \ o \ \ } \ \circ \ \ \circ \ 2 ~ ; } \\ & { \qquad ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { - \mathrm { i } } } = - \cdots \circ \circ 6 _ { 3 } 7 \ 8 9 2 } \\ & { \qquad ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { - \mathrm { i } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { k } } \mathfrak { e } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } } = - \cdots \circ \circ \circ 2 9 \mathrm { I } } \\ & { \qquad 2 ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = - \cdots \circ \circ \circ 2 9 0 } \\ & { \qquad ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { i } } } = - \cdots \circ _ { 3 } 2 5 2 } \end{array} } )  \\ &  \qquad ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { g } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } } = - \cdots \circ 3 2 5 2 \atop \qquad 2 ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { k } } \mathfrak { e } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } } = ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { k } } \mathfrak { e } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { c } } } = - \cdots \circ 
$$

here $( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } }$ denotes the coeffcient of $\mathrm { e } ^ { 2 }$ in $\beta _ { \mathrm { 2 } } / n a ^ { 2 }$ &c.

These differences should properly be divided by 2because $( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { { k ^ { 2 } } } }$ which is accurately determined, is very nearly equal to 2.

The first result tests certain terms of the third order ; the other thre results test terms of the fifth order.The latter involve a large proportion of the forms for computation of the ffth-order terms and of the results for the terms of the fourth and lower orders.

147. Next, if we equate to zero the coefficients of ${ \tt I } , \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } , \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } , \mathrm { ~ } e ^ { \prime 2 }$ in （18），（19）,thecoefficients of 1, $\ell ^ { \prime 2 }$ in the constant term of $\tau / r$ should be zero,and,since $E + { \mathcal { M } } = n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } ;$ ,

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } = - ( \mathrm { r } + \mathrm { m } ) n \alpha ^ { 3 } ( { \frac { \mathrm { T } } { r } } ) ^ { 0 } \mathrm { e } ^ { * _ { 3 } } { \frac { \mathrm { T } } { 3 } } ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } } = - ( \mathrm { r } + \mathrm { m } ) n a ^ { 3 } ( { \frac { \mathrm { T } } { r } } ) ^ { 0 } \mathrm { k } ^ { \ast } } \\ & { \qquad { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } ( \beta _ { 2 } ) _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { E } ^ { 2 } } = { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } ( \beta _ { 3 } ) _ { \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { g } _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } = - ( \mathrm { r } + \mathrm { m } ) n a ^ { 3 } ( { \frac { \mathrm { I } } { r } } ) ^ { 0 } \mathrm { e } ^ { * _ { 3 } } } \end{array} } \}
$$

From the results of the transformation to polars (Chapter IX.) I find

+0040 e²k²+000 e/2k2-0049 k4.

It will be noticed that the differences from zero of the coefficients of $\mathrm { e } ^ { 2 }$ , $\mathrm { k } ^ { 2 }$ ， $\mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } e ^ { \prime 2 }$ ,ek² do not exced one unit in the last places calculated. A corresponding agreement will be found for the coeficients of e,e²k²,k4, when calculated from (2o).This degree of aceuracy should be expected, since the number of places of decimals used in transforming to parallax is smaller than that actually obtained in u, z. These tests apply to the results for the fourth and lower orders.\*

148. The following tables show the characteristics and arguments of the terms of the fifth order whicl have been caleulated, together with the §§ in which the results are given. I have not set forth the types of coefficients, since these are sufficiently evident from the arguments and characteristics according to the scheme adopted in Section (iv)， Chapter I., and illustrated in the later chapters.

The terms with the following arguments and characteristics required the method of138 toobtain theapproximationswithsuffcient rapidity

For other terms where the approximations were slow it was found to be sufficient to calculate the third approximation, since a regular law of decrease then appeared which permited the remainders to be writen down from inspection.

The results for A/ax, B/a²λ, ux-1/ax are given in the following section under the columns headed A, B, ux&-1/aλ. The last is a change of plan from previous chapters, intended to inerease the clearness of the reading, since the results are always referred to their arguments and not to the special notations adopted for the coeffcients. The choice of subsidiary results for publication at the present time was less easy with the homogeneous equations than with the non-homogeneous. It may be stated,in this connection, that long-period small divisors do not appear in A or B except in terms which have the explicit factor m², while the monthly small divisors occur in B but not in A. Thus certain coefficients in the B-tables are not accurate to the last figure set down, but it is better to retain the same number of places for every coeffcient under a given characteristic up to the final results. The work easily shows the extent to which the latter are correet,and in the tables for uxξ-1/aλ none are given beyond this point.

It is to be noted that A=A, B=-B, so that, given the coeffcients in these functions for $\zeta ^ { 2 i + \tau }$ it isnotnecessary towritedown thosefor $\zeta ^ { 2 i - \tau } .$ ,

Thecoeficients of inBare enclosed insquare brackets,to signify that they are those parts of B denoted by $\mathbf { B } ^ { \prime \prime }$ in $\ S \ 1 3 6 .$

In Chap. VI. the A-tables for the z-equation were given in two parts (see § 98); here the parts are not separated, sincc the Lλ are generally quite small and easy to compute, and, further,they were not separated on the computation shets.

All the other arrangements of the tables are the same as those of Chap. VI.

# CHAPTER VII

TERMS OF THE SIXTH ORDER.

Section (i). Formule and Methods of Procedure for u.

176. Terms in only two characteristics, $\mathrm { e ^ { 4 } k ^ { 2 } , e ^ { 2 } k ^ { 4 } }$ , have been calculated； for those in $\mathrm { e } ^ { 6 }$ ,in which vcry small divisors do not occur, the elliptic values can be substituted; those in $\mathbf { k } ^ { 6 }$ are insensible. No terms with arguments 2i are calculated, as no small divisors are present; the constant $\mathrm { C } ^ { \prime }$ enters only with these terms,and it may therefre be neglected.

The method is that of Chapter VII., with $\Omega _ { 1 } = \mathrm { L } ^ { \prime } = \Lambda ^ { \prime } = \circ .$ Als0 $^ { a }$ takes the values 6, 5,4, 3, and $b = 6 - a .$ for $u _ { 3 } u _ { 3 } , s _ { 3 } s _ { 3 } , z _ { 3 } z _ { 3 } , D z _ { 3 } D z _ { 3 } , u _ { 3 } s _ { 3 } + s _ { 3 } u _ { 3 } = 2 u _ { 3 } s _ { 3 }$ &c., we must substitute the halves of these functions when dealing with the general formule.

177. The main difference arises in the development of Sf, $\ S ( D \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime } )$ Here

$$
\mathrm { \hat { \ s f } = c _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \hat { \bf f } _ { 4 } } { \partial \mathbf { \hat { c } } } + g _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \hat { \bf f } _ { 4 } } { \partial \mathbf { g } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \bf c } _ { 2 } ^ { \ 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial { \bf c } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } g _ { 2 } ^ { \ 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } + c _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial \mathbf { \hat { c } } } + g _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial \mathbf { g } } }
$$

with a similar expression for $\delta ( D \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime } ) ; \partial ^ { 2 } \mathbf { f } _ { 2 } / \partial \mathbf { c } \partial \mathbf { g } , \partial ^ { 2 } ( D \mathbf { f } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) / \partial \mathbf { c } \partial \mathbf { g }$ are zero.

For the frst two and last two terms the formule of $\ S 1 3 4$ are available with $a = 4 , 3 , 2$ and $b = _ { 4 } - a ,$ , $a = 2$ ,Iand $b { = } 2 - a$ ;when $a = b$ the remark at the close of $\ S \_ { 7 6 }$ must be noted.

For the other two terms I find,for the coefficients of $\zeta ^ { \pm ( 2 i + 2 \mathrm { c } ) }$ only,

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } = 6 u _ { \mathrm { e } } s _ { \mathrm { e } } + 8 ( u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { a } } } s _ { 0 } + s _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { a } } } u _ { 0 } ) , \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \mathrm { e } ^ { 2 } } ( D \mathrm { f ^ { \prime } } _ { 2 } / = - 8 ( u _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { a } } } s _ { 0 } - s _ { \mathrm { e } ^ { \mathrm { a } } } u _ { 0 } ) ;
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \hat { \bf f } _ { 2 } } { \partial { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } = ( 8 u _ { { \bf k } ^ { 2 } } \delta _ { 0 } + s _ { { \bf k } ^ { 2 } } u _ { 0 } ) \qquad , \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial { \bf g } ^ { 2 } } ( D \hat { \bf f } ^ { \prime } _ { 2 } ) = - ( 8 u _ { { \bf k } ^ { 2 } } \delta _ { 0 } - s _ { { \bf k } ^ { 2 } } u _ { 0 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

No general formule for the derivatives of $\mathrm { Q }$ were obtained,owing tothe difficulty of expressing them in convenient forms. The cases are

ROYAL ASTRON. SOC., VOL. LVII.

For these cases we have, using the notation of $\ S { \textbf { I } } 3 4$ for $\Sigma ^ { \prime }$ and of135 for z', where (2'xe)g+e denotes that the terms whose arguments are ±(2i+g+c) only are to be used,and similarly for $( z _ { \mathrm { k e } } ^ { \prime } ) _ { \mathrm { g - c } } ,$

178. The calculations were made on the plan outlined in Section (iv.) ot Chapter VII., with certain abbreviations. As in most cases only three significant figures were necessary, it was found possible, in forming the products of series, for the computer to add each pair of logarithms from the slips and look out the number corresponding to their sum from a four-place table (printed on a card) without writing anything down (see§ 14o). Only this last number was actually written down, and thus the shcets on which the-various series were added together could be entered straight from the multiplication slips. The great majority of the products consisted of numbers with one or two significant figures, and long practice has madc us so familiar with the logarithms of numbers from I to 99 that a glance at the table was rarely necessary for these.

Nearly all thc other operations were so arranged that the use of logarithms was not necessary and the computation sheets were much abbreviated. In thc few cases where logarithms had to be used they were written down on a spare corner of the shcet,so as not to disturb the general plan.

Only those sets of coeficients corresponding to the arguments 2i±(2c-2g) required the special method of $\ S \textsuperscript { i } 3 8$ .

# Section (i). The Homogeneous Equation for z.

179. As the calculation of Bg (see § 139) would have been long, the homogeneous equation (8) of § 7 was used. The terms calculated werc those with characteristics ke, $\mathrm { k } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { e } ^ { 3 }$ , $\mathrm { k e } ^ { 5 } .$ so that we have $\Omega _ { 1 } { = } 0 .$ The equation is then

$$
D ( u , D z - z D u ) - 2 \mathrm { m } z D u - \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } u z - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } z ( u + s ) = 0 .
$$

Put $u ^ { \prime } { = } u \xi ^ { - 1 } , s ^ { \prime } { = } s \xi ,$ and divide by $\zeta .$ The equation may be writen

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime \prime } + \delta \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime \prime } \equiv ( D ^ { 2 } - 1 - 2 \mathrm { m } - \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } ) z u ^ { \prime } - \boldsymbol { \mathrm { \ell } } 2 ( D + \mathrm { r } + \mathrm { m } ) z D u ^ { \prime } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } z \mathrm { s } ^ { \prime } \zeta ^ { - 2 } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

$$
( D ^ { 2 } - g _ { 0 } ^ { ~ 2 } ) z u ^ { \prime } - 2 ( D + g _ { 0 } ) z D u ^ { \prime } + ( g _ { 0 } ^ { ~ 2 } - 1 - 2 \mathbf { m } - \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } ) z u ^ { \prime } + 2 ( g _ { 0 } - \mathbf { 1 } - \mathbf { m } ) z D u ^ { \prime } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathbf { m } ^ { 2 } z s ^ { \prime } \zeta ^ { - 2 } = 0 ,
$$

The constant coefficients in the last three terms of this equation are small. In fact,

$$
\ g _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } - 1 - 2 \mathrm { m } - \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } = - \cdot \mathrm { o o o } 4 4 , \quad \begin{array} { c } { { g _ { 0 } - \mathrm { r } - \mathrm { m } = + \cdot \mathrm { o o } 4 3 ^ { 2 } , \quad \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } = + \cdot \mathrm { o o g } 8 \circ 5 . } } \end{array}
$$

Also, since $D u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ has the factor $\mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }$ , the term of principal importance in the determination of $z _ { 6 }$ is thc first. If then the operation $( D ^ { 2 } - { g _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 }$ be performed on the equation, the first approximation to $z _ { 6 }$ is given by

$$
a z _ { 6 } = - z w ^ { \prime } + \left( \frac { 2 } { D \mathrm { - } g _ { 0 } } - \frac { 2 \left( g _ { 0 } - 1 - \mathrm { m } \right) } { D \mathrm { - } g _ { 0 } } \right) z D w ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 + { 2 \mathrm { m } } + \frac { 5 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } - g _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 2 } - { \mathrm { g } } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } z a ^ { \prime } + \frac { \mathrm { T } } { D ^ { 2 } - { \mathrm { g } } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \frac { 3 } { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } z { \mathrm { g } } ^ { \prime } \hat { z } ^ { - 2 } = 0 , \ldots
$$

where we neglect $z _ { 6 }$ on the right. For the second approximation we substitute $- z _ { 6 } ^ { \ ( 1 ) } \ \left( u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { a } \right) \ \left( \ S \ 1 \frac { } { } 3 2 \right)$ for the first term on the right and $z _ { \theta } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ , ${ D u _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } }$ for $z .$ $D u ^ { \prime }$ in the other terms ; and so on.

180. As in Chapter VII., ${ \bf c } _ { 0 } , \mathrm { g } _ { 0 } ^ { * }$ are to be used for ${ \mathfrak { c } } , { \mathfrak { g } }$ in performing operations involving $\boldsymbol { D }$ Let $\ S f ^ { \prime }$ denote the parts of (1) due to $\mathrm { c - c _ { 0 } , \ g - g _ { 0 } . }$ Then

$$
\delta \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime \prime } { = } \mathbf { c } _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \mathbf { f } _ { 4 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \mathbf { c } } + \mathbf { g } _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \mathbf { f } _ { 4 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \mathbf { g } } + \mathbf { c } _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \mathbf { f } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \mathbf { c } } + \mathbf { g } _ { 4 } \frac { \partial \mathbf { f } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \mathbf { g } } ;
$$

the terms involving second order derivatives are found to be negligible, owing to the smallness of $\mathbf { C _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 }$ ， $\mathrm { c _ { 2 } g _ { 2 } , \ g _ { 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } }$

Let $q$ denote an index of $\zeta$ in $u ^ { \prime }$ Then, using the notations of $\ S \ S ~ 1 3 4 , \mathrm { ~ } 1 3 5 .$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \partial \mathbf { f } _ { - } ^ { \prime \prime } = \Sigma ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial p } { \partial \mathbf { c } } \Big \{ { } _ { 2 } D ( z u ^ { \prime } ) - z z D u ^ { \prime } \Big \} - \mathbf { \nabla } _ { 2 } ( D + \mathbf { r } + \mathbf { m } ) z \Sigma ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial q } { \partial \mathbf { c } } u ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathrm { _ { I } + m - g _ { 0 } }$ is small enough to benegleted when multiplied by $\mathrm { c - c _ { 0 } , \ g - g _ { 0 } }$ (there are no small divisors), this may be written :

$$
\frac { \partial \mathbf { f } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \mathbf { c } } = \mathfrak { z } \mathfrak { L ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial p } { \partial \mathbf { c } } \mathfrak { u } ^ { \prime } D \mathfrak { z } - \mathfrak { z } ( D + \mathbf { g } _ { 0 } ) \mathfrak { z } \mathfrak { L ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial q } { \partial \mathbf { c } } \mathfrak { u } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

with a similar expression for $\mathrm { { \hat { \alpha } f ^ { \prime \prime } } } / \mathrm { { \hat { \alpha } g } }$

Hence, after putting ${ \bf c } = { \bf c } _ { 0 } ,$ $\mathrm { g } = \mathrm { g } _ { 0 }$ in the coefficients of $\pmb { \zeta }$ in (4), we must add

$$
- \frac { \tt { I } } { D ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { { g _ { 0 } } } ^ { 2 } } \delta { \bf { f } } ^ { \prime \prime }
$$

to its right-hand member ; this expression separates into two parts, involving the operators $( D ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { g } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 }$ and $( D - \mathtt { g } _ { 0 } ) ^ { - 1 }$ respectively.

181. The actual computations are comparatively short. The products are obtained according to the plan explained in $\ S \_ \Psi \cup \cup$ ， and the other remarks there made apply here also. Moreover, as there are no monthly terms, smalldivisors are not present. In most cases the terms multiplied by(3)can be altogether negleeted； when they are not quite insensible a simple inspection shows what additions arise from them. It was also found that a second approximation to the value of $z _ { 6 }$ was not necessary.

# CHAPTER IX.

RESULTS IN POLAR COORDINATES.

Section (i). Formule for Transformation.

For the terms of the ffth order, in which the characteristics $\mathrm { e } ^ { 5 }$ , $\mathrm { e } ^ { 3 } \mathrm { k } ^ { 2 }$ ,ek4 were alone needed,it was shorter to use the Jacobian integral， equation(5)，§7. Here $\Omega _ { 1 } { = } _ { 0 }$ and C' is not present in odd order terms,so that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { { 2 } \binom { k } { r } _ { 5 } = \left[ - D u \cdot D s - ( D z ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 3 } { 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } ( u + s ) ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } z ^ { 2 } \right] _ { 5 } . } \end{array}
$$

All the products had been obtained in finding $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { U } } _ { 5 }$ .An examination of the errors produced by using Co, g for c, g showed that they were insensible. The terms factored by $\mathrm { { m } } ^ { 2 }$ are also insensible in most cases.

The order of accuracy for the parallax is found by dividing the characteristics required in the latitude byk.

Section (ii). Numerical Values of the Constants.

193. The following are the values of the constants used in reducing the results to seconds of arc :

$$
\begin{array} { l l l l } { { n = \mathrm { r } \gamma _ { 3 } \ { } ^ { 2 } \ 2 5 5 9 4 ^ { \prime \prime } { } { } { \circ } 6 \ , \quad } } & { { n ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { 1 } 2 \ 9 5 9 7 7 ^ { \prime \prime } { } ^ { * } \mathrm { 4 } \mathrm { 1 } 5 \ , \quad } } & { { \mathrm { ~ m } \ = \mathrm { \cdot } \circ 8 \circ 8 _ { 4 } \ 8 _ { 9 3 3 } 8 , } } \\ { { e = \mathrm { \cdot } \circ 5 4 9 \dot { \circ } \ \circ 5 6 \ , \quad } } & { { e ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { \cdot } \circ \mathrm { r } 6 _ { 7 7 } \ \mathrm { 7 } \ \mathrm { 9 } \mathrm { r } \ , \quad } } & { { \gamma = \mathrm { \cdot } \circ 4 4 8 8 \ \mathrm { 7 } \mathrm { \pi } 6 \ , \quad } } \\ { { \frac { \mathrm { r } } { a \ } = 3 4 \mathrm { r } 9 ^ { \prime \prime } { } { } \mathrm { 5 } \mathrm { 9 } 6 \ , \quad } } & { { \frac { \mathrm { I } } { a ^ { \prime } } = 8 ^ { \prime \prime } { } \mathrm { \cdot } 7 8 \circ 0 \ , \quad } } & { { E = 8 \mathrm { r } { } \mathrm { \cdot } 5 \circ \mathrm { o } } } \end{array}
$$

giving

The value of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { e } }$ corresponds to $\mathrm { a }$ coefficient $2 2 6 3 9 ^ { \prime \prime } 5 8 0$ of the principal elliptic term in longitude ；that of $\gamma$ to a coefficicnt $\yen 80$ of the principal term in latitude； that of $\textbf { \em a }$ to the value $3 4 2 2 ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } 7 0 0$ for the constant term in the sine of the equatorial horizontal parallax of the Moon.

Section (iv). Numerical Values of the Parts of the Arguments and Coefcients arising from the Various Characteristics.

194. The coefficient of each periodic term in longitude and latitude is of the form $\lambda P ( e ^ { 2 } , \epsilon ^ { \prime 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 } , { \alpha _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } ) ,$ where $\lambda$ is the characteristic of the principal part and the factor of λ is a quadruple power series proceeding according to powers of $e ^ { 2 } , e ^ { \prime 2 } , \gamma ^ { 2 } , a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ with numerical coefficients ; in the sine of the parallax each coefficient is of the same form with the additional factor I/a. In the longitude and parallax only even powers, and in the latitude only odd powers, of $\gamma$ are present. In this section will be given the coeffcients of all those periodic terms for which $P$ is not limited by the calculations to a single term. The part due to each characteristic in a coefficient of $\mathfrak { a }$ given periodic term is separately shown. Those periodic terms which have had the parts due to the principal characteristics alone calculated are not set down in this section, since these parts are the final values for these coeffcients given in the next section.

The characteristics in al cases are composed of the new constants e, e, y, $a _ { 1 } ,$ that is to say, they are the same as those of DELAUNAy, with the exception a1 for a. A direct comparison of each part with the corresponding part given by DELAUnAY is thus possible, when allowance has been made for the slight difference in the numerical values for the constants used by DELAunAY and myself,and for the change from a to a..

196. In §§ 197-262 are given the coefficients of those periodic terms in longitude, latitude, and parallax which contain more than one characteristic. The arrangement is primarily according to the orders of the principal characteristics, each principal characteristic being attached to a definite set of arguments which differ only by multiples of 2D and in the signs of the multiples of l,t,F.

The notation for the arguments is that of DELAUNAY $( \ S \ O _ { 1 0 } )$

In the first column of each Table is placed the characteristic, and in the succeding columns the coeffcients corresponding to the multiple (i) of 2D placed in the first row of each Table.

In the last row is given the sum for each column, and therefore the final value for the coefficient ofeach argument.

The coeffcients in longitude are given in $\ S \ S _ { 9 } = 9 7 - 2 2 5 ,$ in units of $\mathrm { { \odot ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } \mathrm { { o o 1 } } } }$

T'he coefficients in latitude are given in $\ S \ S$ 226-248,in units of o"·001.

The coefficients in parallax are given in $6 6 2 4 9 - 2 6 2$ inunits of $0 ^ { \prime \prime } 0 0 0 1$

All coefficients have been caleulated so as to be correct to the last figure given, with the exception of those depending on e\$, for which the elliptic values have been substituted. This remark applies also to the Tables in section (v).

# Section(v)TealVaesofeentLgide,Ludedll

263. The following tables, giving the final values of the coeficients, are arranged, first, according to the:order and composition of the principal characteristics; second, according to the signs of the multiples of l,l, F; and third,according to multiples of D in descending order.

In the first column, headed “ P.C.,”is given the principal characteristic. In the second,third,fourth,and fth columns, headed “l,” “𝑉,”“F,” “D,”are given the multipIes of those arguments (DELAUNAY's notation) to which the coefficients in the last column correspond. The characteristic is understood to belong to all coeffieients down to the next printed characteristic ; a similar remark applies to the multiples ofl.', F.

As stated earlier, the system of axes used is the same as that in which DELAUNAY expressed his final results.

These coeffients are all definitive for the corresponding arguments, with the following exceptions :-

(a) Smallchanges due to possible changes in the values of the arbitrary constants. (β) Small additions due to the terms arising from the perturbations noted in (a), (b),(c)of§4, Chap. I. These are very minute and are easily obtained. They are, however, more simply treated by the method of the variation of arbitrary constants, and will therefore be given with the treatment of the planetary inequalities.

The results have been discussed, and a comparison has been made with those of HANSEN elsewhere.\*

\* E. W. Brown,"The Final Values of the Coeffcients in the New Lunar Theory,” Monthly Notices, January 1905:

264. Longitude. Coefficients of Sines.

# Errata (additional to those given on p. 2oz in Part II.)

Part I.(vol.li.), p. 43,line I2,for “equal masses"read “ masses equal to their actual masses."

” 46,, 18,for “ parallactic inequality”read “the principal parallactic inequality in longitude.”

Theory of the Motion of the Moon； containing a New Calculation of the Expressions for the Coordinates of the Moon in Terms of the Time. By ERNEsT W. BROWN, M.A., Sc.D., F.R.S.

[Received April 13 ; read May 8, 1908.]

THE previous parts of this Memoir have been published in the Memoirs of the Royal Astronomical Society under the same title in 1897, 1899, 190o, I905.They contained the solution of what I have called the main problem-the motion of the Moon under the attraction of the Earth and the Sun,which are supposed to move round one another in a fixed eliptic orbit in the plane of reference,the three bodies being treated as spheres.This fifth and last part treats of the effects of all other gravitational causes--the direct and indirect attractions of the planets,the deviations of the masses of the Earth and Moon from mechanical sphericity，and various minor perturbations which had been specifically excluded.

The problem presented by these additional perturbing forces is a complicated one. In order to clear the ground,it was first assumed that each force contained a small factor whose square could be neglected,so that the perturbations could be separately considered； then the perturbations were supposed to be expressible by a series of secular and periodic terms,each of which, umder the same assumption， could also be separately considered.Chap. X. therefore contains the method for finding the effect of a perturbation expressed by a periodic or secular term， and in Chaps. XI., XII., XIII. this method is successively applied to direct and to indirect planetary actiol and to the action of the figures of the Earth and Moon. In Chap. XIV., in addition to minor perturbations, the effcts of including the squares and higher powers of the small factor is considered : a few very small terms were found. In Chap. XV. the results from all these perturbations are gathered together, so that the final expresions for the coordinates of the Moon in terms of the time are obtained by adding the results in Chap. XV. to those previously given in Chap. IX.

The whole question of these perturbations,in spite of this division into parts, is still complex,and,in preparing the results for publication,the choice of the portions to be presented in detail was an embarrassing one. It was made more so by the conditions which rendered necessary a previous publication of the work for the direct planetary inequalities in separate form.\*This work included a fullinvestigation of the equations of variations which are needed for inequalities arising from all sources,and there was thus a choice between repeating this investigation and leaving the present Memoir incomplete. The sanie difficulty arose with the direct inequalities and with some other subjects which I have discussed in previous papers.I finally adopted the plan of inserting this previous work either when it was essential for clear presentation, or when it was suficiently brief to occupy but litle space, or when the proofs could be considerably improved.Thus, in Chap. X.，the equations of variations,the idea of which is due to G. W. HiLL,are rapidly put into the required form $( A . P . E .$ ，Sect. I.)； the formulae for obtaining derivatives with respect to n from a theory in which the numerical value of $n _ { / \mathscr { n } } ^ { \prime }$ has been substituted (Trans. Amer. Math. Soc., vol. iv.）are deduced in a few lines； the methods for dealing immediately with non-periodic terms (Proc. Lond. Math. Soc., vol. xxvii.，and Trans. Amer. Math. Soc.,vol. v.)are partly developed,but the results ( $i b .$ ,and Monthly Notices,vol.lvi.) are only quoted. In Chap.XI. the proof of the tleorem on which the method for the direct inequalities is based (A.P.E.，Sect.II.) is exhibited in a simple form,but the full algebraical results are merely quoted； for the rest of this part of the subject the methods are described in general terms and the final results alone given. In Chap. XII.a theorem for finding quickly the effects of long-period and secular inequalities in the Earth's motiou is simply quoted (Trans. Amer. Math. Soc.， vol. vi.)； this theorem was also used several times in Chap.XIV.；but the brief derivation of the disturbing function for the motion of the ecliptic (Monthly Notices, vol. lxvii.） is given in full.

It was found to be impracticable to present within reasonable limits' much of the work actually performed. Numerous rough computations were made to find out whether coefficients or classes of coefficients were sensible ； when they were insensible, a simple note, often the result of days or weeks of work,is made to that effect,but the organised plan of procedure always used in such cases is generally described ； this is the case with most of the results in Chap. XIV.

Owing to the indications furnished by observation of an inequality or inequalities with a coefficient or coefficients of the order of $\mathrm { ~ I ~ O ~ } ^ { \prime \prime }$ of arc and of very long period, one of the chief objects in view has been an investigation of such terms,and the “ sieves” used in Chaps. XI., XIL., XIV. were devised for this purpose.No large coefficients beyond those already known have been found.Moreover, the search has led more and more to the conclusion that no such terms can possibly arise with the laws of motion and of gravitation on which this theoretical investigation is based. If these inequalities have a real existence,it would seem that the causc must be sought in some action not purely gravitational.

No part of the numerical work,except some of the multiplications of series which were necessary to find thc derivatives with respcct to $\mathscr { n }$ in Chap. X., Sect. (ii),has been turned over to computers. There are so many delicatc points to consider,and so many terms and classcs of terms havc special peculiarities which permit the calculations to be much abbrcviated, that to obtain the accuracy at which I have aimed by a general plan which could not pernit thesc peculiarities to be uscd, would havc involved an amount of computation out of all proportion to the final results. In fact, not more than one-third of the time occupied by thcse investigations has been spent on accurate numerical work. But all such work has been gonc over at least twice,in many cases tliree times,and tested by comparisons and various methods, whenever possible.

This part concludes the theoretical investigation of thc motion of the Moon under the attraction of gravitation. Its natural sequence--the formation of tables to facilitate thc accurate computation of the position of the Moon at any time or for the purposes of an ephcmeris-has already been arranged for,and will be undertaken at an early date.But here also it seems advisable not to set the computers at work until an extended examination of methods which will best serve the purpose,and of the properties of the final results, has becn madc, so that thc higlest possible accuracy may be obtaincd within the limits set by practieal necessities.

The table of contents of Part V. follows.

Chapter X.--Methods for finding the remaining Lunar Perturbations.

Section (i). The equations of variations.   
Scction (ii). Rcduction of the equations to numerical form.   
Section (iii). Derivatives with respcct to n.   
Section (iv). The final form of the equations of variations.   
Section (v). Numerical valucs of functions of the lunar coordinates.

Chapter XI.-The Direct Action of the Planets.

Section (i). Thc disturbing function.   
Section (ii). The computation of the coefficients $P _ { i }$ Section (ii). The sieve.   
Section (iv). Numcrical valucs of the elcments.   
Section (v). The final results for the dircct action.

Chapter XII.-The Indirect Action of the Planets.

Section (i). The disturbing function.   
Section (ii). The computation of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \delta V ^ { \prime }$   
Scction (ii). Second method. Application to non-periodie changes. Section (iv). The inotion of the ecliptic. Section (v). Numerical values of the Earth's perturbations.   
Section (vi). A sieve for the rejection of insensible coefficients.   
Section (vii). Computation of the lunar perturbations.   
Section (vii). Final results for the indirect action.

Chapter XIII.-Action of the Figures of the Earth and Moon.

Section (i). The disturbing function for the figure of the Earth.   
Section (ii). Numerical results.   
Section (ii). The action of the figure of the Moon.

Chapter XIV.-The Remaining Perturbations.

Section (i). Corrections due to the masses of the Earth and Moon.   
Section (ii). The terms of the second and higher orders.   
Section (ii). Calculation of the terms.   
Section (iv). Perturbations with unknown constants.

Chapter XV.-The Final Expressions for the Moon's Coordinates.


<!-- chunk 0005: pages 281-350 -->
# METHODS FOR FINDING THE REMAINING LUNAR PERTURBATIONS.

Section (i). The Equations of Variations.

$2 6 7$ . The Canonical Equations.--The problem solved in the preceding chapters may be stated as a solution in series of the equations

$$
\frac { d \mathrm { x } _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { d \mathrm { H } } { d \mathrm { y } _ { i } } , \quad \frac { d \mathrm { y } _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \iota / \mathrm { H } } { d \mathrm { x } _ { i } } , \quad H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \mathrm { y } _ { 1 } ^ { ~ 2 } + \mathrm { y } _ { 2 } ^ { ~ 2 } + \mathrm { y } _ { 3 } ^ { ~ 2 } ) - F \quad .
$$

where ${ \bf x } _ { i } \left( \dot { \boldsymbol { i } } = 1 , \ 2 , \ \ 3 \right)$ are the coordinates of the Moon referred to fixed axes, and $F$ is the force function under the hypotheses stated in Chap. I., Sect. (i).

Let $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 1 } - w _ { 2 } , w _ { 1 } - w _ { 3 }$ be the Moon's mean longitude, and the arguments of the principal elliptic term,and of the principal term in latitude, respectively, so that $w _ { 2 }$ $w _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ are the “ mean longitudes of the perigee and node" resulting from the solution of (1). Then Jacobi's method shows that, if a quantity $\mathcal { h }$ be added to $\vec { F }$ ， and if it be expressed in terms of $w _ { 1 } , \ w _ { 2 } , \ w _ { 3 }$ and $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ ,the other three arbitraries of the solution of (1), the latter three may be so chosen that the solution of the differential equations

$$
i \stackrel { d \bar { c } _ { i } } { \bar { t } } = \frac { d R } { d \bar { v } _ { i } } , \stackrel { d w _ { i } } { \bar { d t } } = - \frac { d R } { d c _ { i } } + \bar { b } _ { i } \qquad , \qquad . \qquad . \qquad . \qquad . \qquad .
$$

will give variable values of the $c _ { i } , \ i , v _ { i } ,$ which, wlen substituted in the expressions for the Moon's coordinates and velocities instead of their former valucs, will give the Moon's position and motion under the force function $F + R$ Here $\boldsymbol { l } _ { 1 } , ~ b _ { 2 } , ~ b _ { 3 }$ are the coeficients of $t$ in the angles $w _ { \mathrm { i } }$ $, w _ { 2 } , w _ { 3 }$ Hence, when ${ \mathcal { k } } _ { } ^ { 2 } = o$ $b _ { 1 } = \varkappa$ ,the mean motion, aud $b _ { 2 }$ ， $b _ { 3 }$ are the mean motious of the perige and node.They are functions of $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ and the constants present in the differential cquations ； and $b _ { \mathrm { 1 } } d c _ { \mathrm { 1 } } + b _ { \mathrm { 2 } } d c _ { \mathrm { 2 } } + b _ { \mathrm { 3 } } d c _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ isa perfect differential.

is a perfect differential,and therefore,since $b _ { 1 } = \mathscr { n }$ ，

269. Solution of the Equations.-It is supposed that R contains a small factor whose square may be neglected,and consequently that we may substitute the undis-turbed values of n, C, Cg, Wi in R, that is,the values furnished by the solution of the main problem. In all the cases to be considered under this method,R can then be expressed as the sum of non-periodic and periodic terms, and each of these terms may be separately treated. First, for periodic terms, put

$$

$$

where q",q" are constants independent of n,C2, Cg, W, and A' is a function of n, C2, Cg only. Substituting in the first three of equations (3) and integrating,we obtain

\delta n = - \ \frac { A ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { 2 } \beta q } \ \frac { d q } { d \hbar } \cos \left( q t + q ^ { \prime } \right) , \quad \delta e _ { 2 } = \frac { i _ { 2 } A ^ { \prime } } { q } \cos \left( q t + q ^ { \prime } \right) , \quad \delta e _ { 3 } = \frac { i _ { 3 } A ^ { \prime } } { q } \cos \left( q t + q ^ { \prime } \right) .
$$

where dn,Scz,&cg are the additions to n, C, cg due to R. The arbitrary constants due to integration are given zero values.

If 8b1,db2, Sbg are the corresponding changes in b1 = n,b, b, due to these changes im $n , \mathrm { ~ } c _ { 2 } , \mathrm { ~ } c _ { 3 } .$ we have $\delta b _ { 1 } = \delta n$ and from $( 2 a )$ and (4)

$_ { 2 7 2 }$ . Derivatives with respect to $c _ { 2 }$ ${ \mathcal { C } } _ { 3 } ,$ -These may be obtained from the deriva-tives with respect to e, k by solving the equations

where $Q$ is any function under consideratioll. Inserting the numerical values of e,k obtained from $\ S \ S ~ 1 9 2 , ~ 1 9 3$ of Chap. IX., I find

$$
-  n \alpha ^ { \underline { { { q } } } } \frac { d Q } { d \epsilon _ { 2 } } = + [ 2 ^ { * } 5 4 2 3 ] ^ { \underline { { { e } } } } \frac { d Q } { d \Theta } - [ \overline { { \bf { r } } } \cdot 7 7 3 { \bf { r } } ] \mathrm { k } \frac { d Q } { d \mathrm { k } } , - n \alpha ^ { \underline { { { q } } } } \frac { d Q } { d \epsilon _ { 3 } } = + [ { \bf { 2 } } \cdot 9 9 2 9 ] \mathrm { k } \frac { d Q } { d \mathrm { k } } - [ \overline { { \bf { r } } } \cdot 7 3 8 6 ] \mathrm { e } \frac { d Q } { d \Theta } .
$$

I $\ S 1 4 5$ , Chap. VII., will be found the materials to obtain $b _ { 2 } , \ b _ { 3 }$ (there denoted by $\pi _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 1 } )$ .From them I obtain, with tlie help of equations (12),

$$
\alpha ^ { 2 } \frac { d \hat { l } _ { 2 } } { d c _ { 2 } } = + \left[ \vec { \bf \ " 2 } \cdot 3 { \bf \ r } 7 5 \right] , \quad \alpha ^ { 2 } \frac { d \hat { l } _ { 2 } } { d c _ { 3 } } = + \left[ \vec { \bf \ " 2 } \cdot 3 9 6 \circ \right] = \alpha ^ { 2 } \frac { d \hat { l } _ { 3 } } { d c _ { 2 } } , \quad \alpha ^ { 2 } \frac { d \hat { l } _ { 3 } } { d c _ { 3 } } = - \left[ \vec { \bf \ " 3 } \cdot 5 6 9 8 \right] \qquad .

273. Derivatives of $b _ { 2 } , \ b _ { 3 }$ with respect to $n$ .-These might have been obtained by finding $c _ { 1 }$ from the first of equations $( 9 a )$ and using the first and second of equations $\left( 2 a \right)$ .But it was found to be much more simple and sufficiently accurate to use the existing literal developments in combination with the numerical developments. The method for doing this I have given in a former paper.tIt is as follows :-

Let

$$
f ( \mathrm { m } ) = a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } \mathrm { m } + a _ { 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 } + \mathrm { ~ . ~ . ~ } .
$$

and denote by $f _ { i } ( \mathrm { m } )$ tle sum of the first $i$ terms of this series,and by an accent the derivative with respect to m. Then

$$
\mathrm { m } f ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { m } ) = \mathrm { m } f _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { m } ) + i \biggl [ a _ { i } \mathrm { m } ^ { i } + \frac { i + \mathrm { \bf ~ I } } { i } a _ { i + 1 } \mathrm { m } ^ { i + 1 } + \frac { i + 2 } { i } a _ { i + 2 } \mathrm { m } ^ { i + 2 } + \dots \biggr ] .
$$

1f $i$ be not too small,and the series not converging too slowly,the error committed by putting

$$
\mathrm { m } f ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { m } ) = \mathrm { m } f _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { m } ) + i \big [ f ( \mathrm { m } ) - f _ { i } ( \mathrm { m } ) \big ]
$$

will be small compared with the true value of $\mathrm { n } f ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { m } )$ .When the series appears to be diminishing with fair regularity,the use of $i + \mathrm { p r o p e r }$ fraction instead of $\mathbf { \chi } _ { i } ^ { \cdot }$ in the last formula will probably give greater accuracy.As a matter of fact, I have only used this to find the greatest possible error which could have been committed,so as to avoid any sensible error in the result.

for the part of bg depending on m only,and of ApAMs \* for the corresponding part of bg； and for the other portions from the literal values of DELAUNAYt after a test by a special method which I have given earlier,t combined with my numerical values.

J Thcse latter portions are expressed in terms of e, x. Since the derivatives of e/e, k/γ are insensible to the degree of accuracy required, we can make the change, after diffrentiating DELAUNAY's series, by the formulae of § 192, Chap. 1X.

The required derivatives with respect to n are obtained from equations (1 combined with

$$
{ \frac { d Q } { d m } } = \left( { \frac { d Q } { d n } } \right) - { \frac { d Q } { d c _ { 2 } } } \left( { \frac { i k _ { 2 } } { d n } } \right) - { \frac { d Q } { d c _ { 3 } } } \left( { \frac { d c _ { 3 } } { d n } } \right) ~ .

where the brackets denote that the enclosed functions are expressed in terms of n,e, k. The derivatives of cy, C are obtained from NEwcoMB's transformation § of DELAUNAY's literal values for G-L=cg, H-G=g, combined with the numerical values of cz, C given above. They are only needed to two significant figures.ll

The other derivatives with respect to n are found in the next section.

These apparently complicated processes are constructed to avoid the slow convergence which occurs with certain of the literal series arranged in powers of m, and I believe they have achieved the object in view.

# Section (ii). Derivatives with respect to n.

275. The equations for finding n-derivatives with the system n, Cg, Cg, Wo--The problem here is to find these derivatives from a theory in which the numerical value of n has been substituted.T [ shall show that these derivatives may all be made to depend on derivatives with respect to the other five elements,and therefore with respect to e, k, $w _ { i }$ ·

# Section (iv). The Final Form of the Equations of Variations.

279. The Equations of Variations.-The equations are now put into a form which is to admit of direct application to any term of the disturbing function. The coeffcient of each cosine or sine which constitutes the variation of an element is to be expressed in seconds of arc. $\mathrm { P u t }$

These formulae are not easily comparable with tlose of HILL,\* RADAU $^ \dagger$ or NEWCOMB $^ { \ddagger }$ because I use derivatives with respect to $\mathscr { n }$ on the assumption that $A ^ { \prime }$ is expressed in terms of $\mathcal { \hat { H } }$ ， $c _ { 3 } , \ c _ { 5 } ,$ , while they suppose that the coefficient is expressed in terms of $\textstyle \mathcal { 1 } i , c , \gamma .$ The chief gain here is the avoidance of the doubtful derivative $d c _ { 2 } / d \ i$ , which in my method is certain to the degree of accuracy required；and further, the method in Sect. $\underset { \infty } { \underbrace { \mathcal { Q } } } \underset { \bigstar } { \big \mathrm { \otimes } } \big \odot$ Jethod of using the equations and abbreviations.-The numbers $f , f ^ { \prime }$ are the same for all the direct perturbations of a given planet ； for all indirect perturbations;they have two values for terms dependent on the motion of the ecliptic ； and three values for terms depending on the figure of the Earth. The numbers $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 3 } ,$ $u _ { \because } , \mu _ { \vec { \mathrm { 3 } } } \mathrm { , }$ are the same for a particular Moon argument in a given class of perturbations ; the first and third terms of $\mu _ { \mathrm { 2 } }$ and the first and second terms of $\mu _ { 3 }$ can nearly always be neglected. Only two numbers, $C , \ C _ { 1 } ,$ have to be computed for each coefficient required, and one of these is generally very small.

282.Theorem.t-If $\delta w _ { \mathrm { 2 } }$ be confined to the term $\mu _ { 2 } C _ { 1 } \sin { ( g t + q ^ { \prime } ) }$ and $\alpha$ coordinate $\lambda$ to the term $\mathcal { P }$ sin $( i _ { 2 } ^ { \ \prime } w _ { 2 } + \Psi ^ { \prime } )$ ,where $\psi ^ { \prime }$ is independent of $w _ { 2 }$ and $i _ { \mathrm { 2 } } ^ { \prime }$ has the same sign as $i _ { 2 } ,$ and $i _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = + = 0$ ， $i _ { 2 } = \neq 0$ , then the variation of the coordinate $\lambda$ due to $\delta w _ { 2 } , \delta c _ { 2 }$ is

$$
\frac { d \lambda } { d c _ { 2 } } \delta ^ { \prime } _ { 2 } + \frac { d \lambda } { d w _ { 2 } } \delta \iota v _ { 2 } = \mu _ { 2 } C _ { 1 } p i _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \sin \{ \eta t + q ^ { \prime } - i _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } w _ { 2 } - \psi ^ { \prime } \} \ .
$$

That this theorem is true when we neglect all but the lowest power of $\boldsymbol { \mathcal { C } }$ present in the coefficients and when $\ddot { \mathrm { ~ o ~ } } n$ is negligible is immediately seen.For, in such cases, $j _ { 2 } = | i _ { 2 } |$ $\Bot { \delta c } _ { 2 }$ depends on $d R / d w _ { 2 }$ ,and $\delta w _ { \mathrm { z } }$ 01 $d R / d c _ { 2 }$ But it appears to hold even more accurately.An exactly similar theorem holds for $\delta c _ { 3 } , \delta v _ { 3 } .$ ,and the terms depending on $w _ { 3 }$ in a coordinate. In the case of the principal term in latitude due to the figure of the Earth it holds within one-tenth of one per cent. of the whole.

# Section (v). Numerical Values of Functions of the Lunar Coordinates.

${ } _ { 2 } 8 3$ . These functions are chiefly products of the second order in $\mathcal { U } , \mathcal { S } , \mathcal { Z }$ The computation of them by niultiplications of series is quite like those necessary in previous chapters,and indeed the great majority of them had been obtained in the solution of the main problem. The formation of the derivatives with respect to $\mathscr { n }$ ，e,k has been developed in previous sections.It is understood that the $\mathscr { n }$ derivatives are formed with

$$

# CHAPTER XI.

THE DIRECT ACTION OF THE PLANETS.

# Section (i). The Disturbing Function.

293. Axes and Notation.-The value of the disturbing function and the equations of variations are the same whatever axes be chosen. For the expansion of the former I take for plane of xy the ecliptic of $1 8 5 0 ^ { \cdot } 0$ , and for axis of x a line parallel to that joining the Earth and Sun, that is,a line parallel to the Earth's true radius vector on the assumption that the Sun moves in an elliptic orbit. With these directions, $x , y , z , r$ will now represent the coordinates and distance of the Moon, $\xi , \eta , \zeta , \bigtriangleup$ those of the planet, the Earth being the origin.

For the elliptic coordinates of the Earth and planet with the Sun in the focus,I take $i ^ { \prime } , \stackrel { \prime \prime } { \gamma ^ { \prime } }$ (the distances); $V ^ { \prime }$ $V ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the true longitudes)；T, $\mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the mean longitudes); $\varpi ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the longitudes of the perihelia)；O, $h ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the longitudes of the nodes)；O, $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the sines of half the inclinations; $e ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the eccentricities）; $2 a ^ { \prime } , 2 a ^ { \prime \prime }$ (the major axes).All these longitudes are measured in the usual way,that is, from a fixed line in the plane of xy to the node,and then along the plane of the orbit.

The perturbations of the planet's orbit,like those of the Earth,are neglected in this chapter. In order that the motion of the Earth round the centre of mass of the Earth and Moon may be taken into account, the terms depending on $\mathrm { a } / a ^ { \prime }$ in the disturbing function must be multiplied by the ratio of the difference of the masses of the Earth and Moon to their sum (Chap. I., $\ S 4 )$ ；we must therefore use $a _ { \mathrm { 1 } } \mathrm { a } / \alpha$ instead of $\mathrm { a } / a ^ { \prime }$ (Chap. IX., $\ S ^ { \mathrm { ~ I ~ } 9 3 }$ ),or,with sufficient accuracy, ${ \mathfrak { a } } _ { \mathfrak { j } }$ instead of $\alpha$

The masses of a planet and of the Sun are denoted by $m ^ { \prime \prime }$ ， $m ^ { \prime }$ ，and the mean longitudes of the planets,measured like the other longitudes,as follows \*:Mercury,Q; Venus,V；Earth， T；Mars, M；Jupiter, $\mathbf { J }$ ；Saturn，S； the other planets and the asteroids will not be considered in this chapter.

294. The Disturbing Function $R$ and its Transformation.--From Chap. I., Sect. (i),we obtain, on changing from the Sun to a planet as the disturbing body,

$$
\frac { R } { m ^ { \prime \prime } } = \frac { \mathrm {  ~ r ~ } } { \{ ( \xi - x ) ^ { 2 } + ( \eta - y ) ^ { 2 } + ( \zeta - z ) ^ { 2 } \} ^ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } - \frac { x \xi + y \eta + z \zeta } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } , \Delta ^ { 2 } = \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } \quad .
$$

\* Mean longitudes are denoted by roman capitals.

↑A.P.E.,sect.ii., where the full details of the transformation will be found.

If we denote by $\hat { v } / \hat { v } Q$ the operator,

\frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \imath } } } { \hat { \boldsymbol { \jmath } } \ell } = \langle \boldsymbol { \imath } _ { \hat { c } \hat { \xi } } ^ { \hat { \boldsymbol { \imath } } } + \boldsymbol { y } \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \imath } } } { \hat { \boldsymbol { \jmath } } \eta } + \boldsymbol { z } \frac { \hat { \boldsymbol { \imath } } } { \hat { \boldsymbol { c } } \zeta } ,
$$

$$

into the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { [ \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } ) \binom { \hat { \eta } ^ { 2 } } { \hat { \eta } \hat { \xi } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \hat { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } { \hat { \epsilon } \eta ^ { 2 } } ) + \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) \binom { \hat { \eta } ^ { 2 } } { \hat { \epsilon } \hat { \xi } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { \hat { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } { \hat { \epsilon } \eta ^ { 2 } } ) + x y \frac { \hat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } } { \hat { \epsilon } \hat { \xi } \hat { \epsilon } \hat { \eta } } + ( x _ { \hat { \epsilon } \hat { \xi } } ^ { \hat { \epsilon } } + y \frac { \hat { \omega } } { \hat { \epsilon } \eta } ) z _ { \hat { \sigma } \hat { \zeta } } ^ { \hat { \eta } } + \mathrm { t e r m s ~ o f ~ h i g h e r ~ d e g r e e } ] \frac { \mathrm { ~ \mathrm { { t } ~ } } } { \Delta } } \end{array}

\frac { \hat { \bar { \sigma } } } { \hat { c } \hat { \zeta } } \frac { \mathrm { \bf ~ I } } { \Delta } = - \frac { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { ( \mathrm { \bf ~ I } - \gamma ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { \frac { } { } 2 } } \frac { e ^ { i ( \mathrm { \bf ~ \hat { r } } ^ { \prime } - \mathrm { \bf ~ \hat { ~ } \chi } ^ { \prime } ) } } { r ^ { \prime } } \left\{ \frac { \hat { \bar { c } } } { \hat { c } V ^ { \prime } } + \frac { \mathrm { \bf ~ I } } { 2 \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { \hat { \bar { \sigma } } } { \hat { c } h ^ { \prime \prime } } + \iota ( \mathrm { \bf ~ I } - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } ) \frac { \hat { \bar { c } } } { \hat { c } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \right\} \frac { \mathrm { \bf ~ I } } { \Delta } \mathrm {  ~ \Omega ~ } .

Finally

$$
\eta ^ { \prime } \frac { \hat { \partial } } { \hat { c } \eta ^ { \prime } } = a ^ { \prime } \frac { \hat { \partial } } { \hat { \partial } a ^ { \prime } } , \quad \hat { \frac { \partial } { \partial } } = \frac { \hat { \partial } } { \hat { c } \mathrm { T } } \qquad , \qquad \cdot \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } \cdot \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } \cdot \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } \cdot \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } \cdot \mathrm { ~  ~ \Omega ~ } \cdot
