and thereforc $i f { \textrm { 1 } } / \Delta$ be expanded in terms of the elliptic elements of the Earth and the planet, the functions needed are all expressed as derivatives of $1 / \Delta$ with respeet to the elements present in the development. It is true that these derivatives are to be multiplied by $1 / r ^ { \prime 2 }$ and that the lunar coordinates have to be transformed so as to be referred to the true instead of the mean place of the Sun；but the work needed to perform these two opcrations is very small,especially when compared with the labour of making developments of sevcral different planetary functions, such as

$$
\frac { \mathbf { I } } { \Delta ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 3 \zeta ^ { 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } , \frac { \boldsymbol { \xi } ^ { * } - \eta ^ { 2 } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } , \frac { \boldsymbol { \xi } \boldsymbol { \zeta } } { \Delta ^ { 5 } } , \mathrm { e t c . }
$$

295.The transformed Disturbing Funetion.-I omit the algebraical details necessary to follow out this method, so as to present the results in a form convenient for numerical application. The result is a form for the disturbing functiou expressed as a sum of products : the first factor in each product is a function of the Moon's coordinates, $u , s , z ,$ ，multiplied by a certain function of $r ^ { \prime } , \ V ^ { \prime }$ ； the second factor consists of derivatives of $1 / \Delta$ with respect to $ { \boldsymbol { \alpha } } ^ { \prime }$ ,T, $h ^ { \prime \prime }$ ， $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 }$

The notation for the first factors has already been given in $\ S 2 8 5$ of the last chapter. If an angle $\theta$ is present in the first factor, then the corresponding term is $\mathcal { M } _ { i } \cos \theta + \iota \mathcal { M } _ { i } \sin \theta$ , where such constant factors have been taken out that the $M _ { i }$ may be numerical quantities.

296. For the second factors,let a term with argument $\phi$ in $1 / \Delta$ be

$$
\frac { \mathbf { r } } { \Delta } = P \cos \phi .
$$

If $i , i ^ { \prime }$ be the multiples of $\mathrm { T }$ ， ${ _ { 2 } h ^ { \prime \prime } }$ present in $\phi$ ,the operators $\hat { o } / \hat { c } V ^ { \prime } , \hat { o } / \hat { c } h ^ { \prime \prime }$ give rise to the factors $i , i ^ { \prime }$ ,and the cosiue is changed to a minus sine.

Also $P$ is of degree -1 in length,and may be expanded in the form $f ( a ) / a ^ { \prime }$ ,where $a = { a } ^ { \prime } / { a } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，or in the form $f ( a ) / \alpha ^ { \prime \prime }$ ，wlerc $a = a ^ { \prime \prime } / a ^ { \prime }$ ，according as the orbit of the disturbing planet is outside or inside that of the Earth. If, then,we put $I = a d / d a$ for a derivative with respect to $\log a$ only in so far as $\alpha$ occurs explicitly in $P$ after $P$ has been cxpressed in one or other of these two forms, we have :

$$
\begin{array}{c} \begin{array} { l l l } { { \mathrm {  ~ \ v ~ } _ { \mathrm { o r \ o u t e r \ p l a n e t s , } } } } & { { { \displaystyle \imath ^ { \prime } { \displaystyle } { \mathit { d } } ^ { \prime } = a { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d a } } = I } , } } & { { a = { \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { { \displaystyle a } ^ { \prime } } } , } } & { { P = { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm {  ~ \tau ~ } } { a ^ { \prime } } } f ( a ) ~ ; } } \\ { { \mathrm {  ~ \ v ~ } _ { \mathrm { o r \ i n n e r \ p l a n e t s , } } } } & { { { \displaystyle r ^ { \prime } { \displaystyle } { \mathit { d } } ^ { \prime } = - a { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d a } } - \mathrm {  ~ \tau ~ } { \mathrm {  ~ \scriptstyle ~ I } } = - I - \mathrm {  ~ \tau ~ I } , } } } & { { a = { \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { \prime \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } } ~ , } } & { { P = { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm {  ~ \tau ~ } } { \partial { \displaystyle } { \mathit { d } } ^ { \prime } } } f ( a ) } } \end{array}  . .  \end{array}
$$

Whel we combine the two factors,we obtain the product of two cosines or two sines multiplied by a constant factor； tlis product is expressed as the cosine of the sum or difference of the two angles $\theta , \ \phi$ The notation for the planet coefficients is as follows :-

Let $J _ { + } , J _ { - }$ denote the operators

$$
\big ( \mathrm {  ~ I ~ } - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } \big ) \frac { \widehat { \mathrm {  ~ c ~ } } } { \widehat { c } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \pm \left( i + \frac { i ^ { \prime } } { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime 2 } } \right) ,
$$

and let $i , i ^ { \prime }$ denote the multiples of $\mathrm { T }$ ${ } _ { 2 } h ^ { \prime \prime }$ present in $\phi$

For outer planets put

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { P _ { 1 } = ( I ^ { 2 } - i ^ { 2 } ) P , \quad P _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } P _ { 1 } - I P + i ^ { 2 } P , \quad P _ { 3 } = - i ( I - \imath ) P , \quad P _ { 4 } \pm P _ { 5 } = - ( I \pm i ) J _ { \pm } P \quad . } \\ & { P _ { 6 } \pm P _ { 7 } = ( I - 2 \mp i ) P _ { 1 } , \quad P _ { 8 } \pm P _ { 9 } = ( I \mp 3 i - 6 ) P _ { 1 } + 4 ( \tau \pm i ) ( 2 \pm i ) ( I \mp i ) P , } \\ & { P _ { 1 0 } \pm P _ { 1 1 } - J _ { \pm } ( - P _ { 1 } + 2 I P \pm 2 i P ) , \quad P _ { 1 2 } \pm P _ { 1 3 } = - J _ { \pm } \{ P _ { 1 } + ( \pm 2 i - 2 ) I P + ( 2 i ^ { 2 } \mp 2 i ) P \} \quad . } \end{array}
$$

I the upper or all the lower signs in any equation being taken together.

The product of the pairs of factors gives for any term of argument $\theta \pm \phi$ in

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { \cal I } = \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { 4 m } \pi ^ { \prime \prime } { \bf a } ^ { 2 } { \bf a } ^ { 2 } \cdot \alpha \alpha ^ { \prime \prime } \biggl [ M _ { 1 } { \cal P } _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } { \cal P } _ { 2 } \mp M _ { 3 } { \cal P } _ { 3 } - \frac { 2 \gamma ^ { \prime } } { \left( 1 - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \right) } M _ { 4 } \bigl ( { \cal P } _ { 4 } \pm { \cal P } _ { 5 } \bigr ) } \\ & { \qquad + \frac { 1 } { 4 } a _ { 1 } \bigl ( M _ { 6 } { \cal P } _ { 6 } \mp M _ { 7 } { \cal P } _ { 7 } + M _ { 8 } { \cal P } _ { 8 } \mp M _ { 9 } { \cal P } _ { 9 } \bigr ) - \frac { \gamma ^ { \prime } a _ { 1 } } { 2 \left( 1 - \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } \right) } \bigl \{ M _ { 1 0 } \bigl ( { \cal P } _ { 1 0 } \pm { \cal P } _ { 1 1 } \bigr ) + M _ { 1 \tau } \bigl ( { \cal P } _ { 1 2 } \pm { \cal P } _ { 1 0 } \bigr ) \bigr \} \biggr ] \cos \left( \theta \pm \phi \right) ~ . } \end{array}
$$

all the upper or all the lower signs being taken according as it is convenient to use $\theta + \phi$ or $\theta - \phi$

For inner planets, we replace $a \boldsymbol { \mathcal { O } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ by $a ^ { \prime }$ in this formula, and $\boldsymbol { I }$ by $- I - 1$ in (12), (13).

It is to be noticed that $\boldsymbol { I } _ { \mathrm { ~ } i } ^ { \prime } \boldsymbol { a } ^ { \prime \prime }$ for outer planets and $P _ { i } a ^ { \prime }$ for inner planets are numerical coefficients when the value of $\alpha$ has been substituted.

297. Method of computation of the coeffcients in $R .$ The terms in $R$ with sufixes I,2,3 give rise to nearly all the sensible perturbations of the Moon's orbit. In the great majority of cases $M _ { \mathrm { 2 } } P _ { \mathrm { 2 } } { \mp } M _ { \mathrm { 3 } } P _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ is small compared with either of the two terms,and this is due to the approximate numerical equality of $M _ { 2 }$ and $M _ { 3 }$ and of $P _ { 2 }$ and $P _ { 3 }$ .It is therefore better (and the computations were so made) to use these two terms in the form

$$
{ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( { \cal M } _ { 2 } \mp { \cal M } _ { 3 } ) ( P _ { 2 } + P _ { 3 } ) + { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( { \cal M } _ { 2 } \pm { \cal M } _ { 3 } ) ( P _ { 2 } - P _ { 3 } ) ~ ;
$$

and then again one of these two expressions was generally small compared with the other. Another advantage of this form arose from the fact that it was sufficient to have $M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ to four significant figures and $P _ { 2 } , P _ { 3 }$ to six, instead of both to six. And, moreover,the near equality of $P _ { 2 } , \ P _ { 3 }$ can be foretold by the theory,when it exists. A similar circumstance holds with the pairs with suffixes $4 , \ 5 \ ; \ 6 , \ 7 \ ; \ 8 , \ 9 \ ; \ \ 1 0 , \ \mathrm { ~ I ~ I ~ } \ ;$ 12,13.

There is not a large number of terms depending on the terms in $\mathcal { R }$ with suffixes 4,5,fewer still with 6, 7,8, 9,and none sensible with 1O,I1,12,13. It did not seem necessary to carry the computations to the next term of $R$ ，depending on ${ { a } _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 }$ ； this coefficient gives a factor $6 \times \mathrm { ~ I O ~ } ^ { - 6 }$ compared with the first terms of $R$ , and it gives rise to no arguments which are not present in the first terms.

# Section (ii). The Computation of the Coefficients $\boldsymbol { { \mathit { I } } } ^ { \prime } { } _ { i }$

298. Leverrier's Expansion of 1/△.t-LEvERRIER’s literal expansion in powers of the eccentricities and mutual inclinations, with coeficients depending on a and arguments on T,u',@", h", was used. Here the Earth is supposed to move in the plane of reference，and the notation is slightly different. I have， therefore， put in the development--

For LEVERRIER's symbols,

the symbols

$$
\begin{array} { l l l l l l } { { \tau , } } & { { \tau ; } } & { { \mathit { l } , } } & { { \lambda , } } & { { \omega , } } & { { \epsilon , } } & { { \mathit { \Omega } ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \mathit { h } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \mathit { h } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \mathrm { T } , } } & { { \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \widehat { \omega } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \rho ^ { \prime \prime } , } } & { { \mathit { d } ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \end{array} \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }
$$

for inner planets,and have then interchanged the accents of α,ε,e, T for outer planets.

LEVERRIER's development contains functions of $\alpha$ through certain coeffcients $\beta _ { \boldsymbol { s } , \boldsymbol { p } } ^ { ( i ) } ,$ which are defincd as follows. Put

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \mathrm { } } & { { } \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { \circ } = { \mathfrak { s } } + a ^ { 2 } - 2 a \cos ( \mathrm { T } - \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } ) , } & { } \\ { \xrightarrow [ { a ^ { \circ } } ] {  { } } } & { { } \therefore \alpha \stackrel { i = \gamma } { \sum _ { i } } \alpha \stackrel { \rho \to \infty } { \rho } \beta _ { s } ^ { \wedge \mathrm { i } } \cos i ( \mathrm { T } - \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } ) , } & { \beta _ { s , p } ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { \mathrm { I } } { p ! } a ^ { p } \frac { d ^ { \prime \prime } } { d a ^ { p } } \beta _ { s } ^ { [ i \wedge \mathrm { i } ] } } & { { } . } \end{array}
$$

Instead of $\beta _ { s } ,$ he uses the letters $A , B , C , D , \tilde { \mathcal { A } } , \tilde { \mathcal { A } } , \tilde { \Psi } , \tilde { \Psi }$ according as $\begin{array} { r } { \mathcal { S } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \frac { \hat { \mathcal { I } } } { 2 } , \frac { \tilde { \mathcal { I } } } { 2 } , \frac { 7 } { 2 } , \frac { 9 } { 2 } , \frac { 1 } { 2 } , } \end{array}$ I adopted the same notation,as well as the following :

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { E ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \big ( B ^ { ( i - 1 ) } + B ^ { ( i + 1 ) } \big ) , } & & { G ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 3 } { 8 } \big ( C ^ { ( i - 2 ) } + 4 O ^ { ( i ) } + O ^ { ( i + 2 ) } \big ) , } \\ & { H ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 5 } { 1 6 } \big ( D ^ { i - 3 } + 9 D ^ { ( i - 1 ) } + 9 D ^ { ( i + 1 ) } + D ^ { ( i + 3 ) } \big ) , } & & { L ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \big ( C ^ { ( i - 2 ) } + C ^ { ( i ) } \big ) , } \\ & { S ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } \big ( D ^ { ( i - 3 ) } + 3 D ^ { ( i - 1 ) } + D ^ { ( i + 1 ) } \big ) , } & & { T ^ { ( i ) } = \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } \big ( D ^ { ( i - 5 ) } + D ^ { ( i - 1 ) } \big ) , } \end{array}
$$

to whicl the suffix P may be attached according to the previous definition. But I dropped the brackets round the indexcs to the letters， since powers of these functions do not arise ; and the indexes themselves were also dropped whenever they were all the same,cqual to $i _ { : }$ ，in a given equation.

299. Formulx for computing the coeffcients.-LevERRIER gives the numerical values in most cases up to i= 1o,but they are needed much further in many cases of the lunar problem. Hence,all of them were newly computed by the formulae which are fully set forth in Section III. of my Adams Prize Essay.These formulae are constructed for several purposes : first,for finding isolated coefficients for special values of i； second, for making tables of coefficients for many consecutive values of i；third,for the avoidance of those small coefficients which appear as the difference of two large numbers. The third point is a dificulty which arises chicfly in computing (l²-i²)βs,, the two parts of which are large compared with their difference, especially for large values of i； but the formule for these completely surmounted the diffculty.

It arises in a less troublesome form in Pz, P, but there the number of plaees of deeimals computed was always sufficient for the degree of aeeuracy required.

$3 0 0$ Numerical values of $\mathcal { A } _ { p } ^ { i }$ ， $\it { B _ { p } ^ { i } }$ . .. .--These were computed to six significant figures in tables \* as follows :-

Venus : $\mathcal { A } _ { 0 } ^ { \ i } , \ \mathcal { B } _ { 0 } ^ { i }$ $i \ \mathrm { t o } \ i = 4 3 \ ; \ A _ { \prime \prime } ^ { i } , \ B _ { \rho } ^ { \imath } \ \mathrm { t o } \ p = 4 , \ i = 3 0 \ ; \ C _ { \rho } ^ { \imath } \ \mathrm { t o } \ p = 2 , \ i = 3 0 \ ;$ $D _ { y }$ $D _ { 1 } , \mathcal { T } _ { \mathfrak { g } }$ to $\dot { \imath } = 3 0 \ ; \ \{ ( I + \mathrm {  ~ r ~ } ) ^ { 2 } - i ^ { 2 } \} A _ { \ p } ^ { i } \ \mathrm { t o } \ \mathcal { p } = 3 , \ i = 3 0 \ ;$ $\{ ( I + { \bf \delta I } ) ^ { 2 } - ( i + \bf \delta I ) ^ { 2 } \} B _ { I } ^ { i } ,$ to $ \mathcal { I } ^ { ) } = z$ ,from $i = - 3 0$ to $i = 3 0$ ； $\left\{ ( I + { \bf \Phi } _ { \bf I } ) ^ { 2 } - i ^ { 2 } \right\} C _ { \phantom { } \jmath } ^ { \prime } ,$ to $p = \mathrm { { I } } , \ i = 3 \circ$   
Jupiter to $i = 6$ for $A _ { i } ^ { i } ,$ to $\lambda ^ { ) } = = 3$ $B _ { \ell ^ { \prime } } ^ { i }$ to $\mathit { l } ^ { \prime } { = } 3 , \mathit { \Omega } ^ { { i } }$ t $\mathcal { P } = \textbf { I }$ ， $\mathcal { D } _ { \mathrm { 0 } } ^ { i }$   
Mars: $A _ { \mathfrak { G } } ^ { i } , \ B _ { 0 } ^ { i }$ to $i = 3 0$ ；and to $i = 6$ for $A _ { 1 } ^ { i } , A _ { 2 } ^ { i } , B _ { 1 } ^ { i } , B _ { 2 } ^ { i } , C _ { 1 } ^ { i }$   
Mercury to $\ i = 8$ for $A _ { i } ^ { i } , B _ { i } ^ { i } ,$ t $\mathit { l } ^ { ) = } 4 , \mathit { \Omega } \mathcal { C } _ { \mathit { p } } ^ { i }$ to $\ j ) = 2$ $D _ { 0 } ^ { \ast }$ .

All other coeffieicnts required with these planets,and those for Saturn， were separately computed as the necds for them arose.

# Seetion (i). The Siere.\*

301. The larger number of the terms in $\mathit { \Pi } _ { \mathit { l } \mathrm { { 0 } } }$ whieh give rise to sensible coefieients iu the eoordinates have periods which are eomparable with the month or the year. For such terms the obvious plan was to take the suecessive values of $\theta$ (the argument arising from the lunar factors) according to the magnitudes of the eoeficients which accompanied this faetor.The arguments O, $l , \ 2 \mathrm { D } - l , \ 2 \ l ) , \ \ldots .$ were successively eombined with all the possible arguments $\phi$ until the terms became insensible and it was unnecessary to procccd further.In cach case the argument $\phi$ was divided into the series $i ( \mathrm { T } - \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ ， $i ( \mathrm { T } - \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } ) \pm \varpi ^ { \prime }$ , and so on, the magnitude of eaeh serics mainly depending on the power of $e ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime \prime }$ $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ which aceompanied it ；and again each of these series was computed with a sufficient number of values of $i$ with each Moon argument. A little practice quickly enabled one to choose out the largest eoefficient in each set,and a rough caleulation was sufficient to show whether the term would be sensible. This rough calculation had to be made for both $\delta w _ { 1 } , \delta w _ { 2 }$ and sometimes $\vartheta w _ { 3 } ,$ for,with terms of short period, the secondary incqualities $\displaystyle \left( \ S \ 2 8 2 \right)$ are frequently sensible when the primary might be negleeted.

This method could not fail as long as the periods were short. These periods only arise in the equations of variations $\displaystyle { \big ( } \ S \quad 2 6 9 { \big ) }$ through the divisors s, $\mathrm { S } ^ { 2 }$ But if any period was long, then s, $\mathrm { S } ^ { 2 }$ would be comparatively small, and a large eoeffieient might result. Such eases as oceurred during the progress of the calculations were naturally dealt with as they arosc； the only mater whieh called for attention was the neessity for a larger number of signifieant figures in the coeffieients $M _ { i } , P _ { i }$ If,in the general method,the numbers were Ⅱot suffieiently accurate, the special coeffcient was separately computed again with more plaees of deeimals.

But the lunar terms contain multiples of four diferent arguments, $w _ { 1 } - \mathrm { T }$ ， $w _ { 2 }$ $w _ { 3 } ,$ $\mathrm { T } - \varpi ^ { \prime }$ , that is,combinations of fourdiffrent periods,and theplanct terms two periods, T,T.Heneewemayhavecombinationsoffivediferent periods,andthere willbe many long periods arising therefrom. It was therefore necessary to sift out those which would give sensible coefficients.

302. The Sieve.--The method was essentialiy the same as that for the short-period terms,but,as there were thousands of possible combinations,some plan had to be devised to find an upper limit to each coefficient so rapidly that every coefficient might be examined within a reasonable time.

The limitations were as follows.Only primary terms were examined.It was shown \* that the secondary would not be greater than the primary unless 𝑖=o (§ 279),s>6o" or i1= 1，s>1ooo"； the very few of the former were separately examined,and the latter had been treated in the short-period terms. Periods greater than 35Oo years (s<1") or coefficients <o"O1 were to be excluded；neither could sensibly affect the motion of the Moon within historic times. But one or two longer periods with coefficients greater than o"O1 which appeared in the course of the work were retained.

The possible long periods were then constructed by finding all up to the largest values of i,j in i(T-T")+jT,±w1+i(T-T")+jT,... It was soon seen that only a dozen or so in each set need be retained,and at the most three multiples of w1. Then a table was formed for the multiples of w, wg, giving the periods,the lowest orders with reference to e, k which would accompany caeh multiple,and the multiple of T which would occur in the lunar argument with this lowest order. Thus,for a given multiple of $w _ { 2 } ,$ Ws, the various long-period combinations with the former sets could be seen at a glance.

303. Next, very simple formule were constructed for the primary coeficient, depending only on the power of e, k present in the lunar factor, the multiple of T-T" and the coefficient present in the expansion of $1 / \Delta ^ { 3 }$ orof ${ \mathfrak { I } } / { \Delta } ^ { 5 }$ These formule arose from transformations of the disturbing function somewhat similar to those of $\ S$ 294, but depending on derivatives vith respect to T only. The values of the coefficients in the expansions of △-，△-5 were obtained from NEwcoMB's table† of these coefficients in the case of Venus；partly from his incomplete table, $^ { \ddagger }$ and partly by extrapolation and by approximate computations, in the case of Mars； for the other planets,which presented little difficulty，a table for △ was roughly computed, and simple formulae depending also on the order of the eccentricities and inelination were constructed.

The various coeffcients were examined according as they arose from terms whose eharacteristics were of orders O, 1, 2,... With each order was associated a maximum value of s which could give sensible coefficients ； after the frst three or four orders the work went very rapidly,as these maximum values of s became small, and the great majority of the terms could be excluded without computatiou.

About ioo long-period terms were retained out of several thousand exaniined,and their coefficients were accurately computed. In no case did these coefficients exceed the preliminary estimates found by means of the sieve. No new terms of any great importancc werc found,and the corections to RADAu's valucs \* of those previously computed wcre small from tlc observational standpoint,as far as the long-period primaries were concerned.

# Section (iv). Numerical Values of the Elements.

304. Most of the observed quantities required are known with more than suffcient accuracy. The most doubtful is the mass of Mereury, which may be in error by as mucli as 5O per cent.；but thc largest coefficient with the adopted value is less than $\mathrm { o } ^ { \prime \prime \cdot } \mathrm { o } 8$ ,and the term has a period of 39 years. The mass of Venus may be in error by 1 or 2 pcr cent.，giving a maximum possible error in the largest coefficient (period 273 ycars) of $\mathrm { o } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } \mathfrak { I }$ from this cause.

The values of thc elements used are shown in the following tables :--

# Section (v). The Final Results.

305. The detailed results arising from each term of the disturbing function are fully set forth in A.P.E., Section vi.,and they will not be reprinted. Many of the resulting terms in the Moon's coordinates, especially those arising from the short-period terms in R,have the same arguments,and must be combined. The final results only will be given here.

There are two methods of expressing the perturbations. The first is to add them to the true longitude,latitude,and parallax of the Moon ； the second is to leave them as additions to the elements w1,wz, Wg,α,e,γ which would be tabulated with these additions. This latter method is only of special advantage for tabular purposes when the variations of a,e, γ may be neglected,and this happens only with terms of long period in which u is present. If w1 is absent from the primary,and the period of the term is not very long compared witl the periods of the Moon's node or perigee, the variation of e produces an effect of the same order as the variation of wz, and that of γ as Wg； in fact, the statement in S282 has to be remembered. In these cases the variations of α (or n) are insensible,and it may be convenient to retain the variations of w1 (or of W1,wz) as elemental inequalities,adding the parts due to the other elements to the coordinates. No periodic variations of $e$ have been retained as elemental inequalities.

For certain other cases in which $w _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ is present, with $\delta e , \delta a$ insensible,it is best to retain δwi, Suz, δwg as elemcntal terms,and account for those arising from δy by multiplying the final value of tle latitude by the variable factor I+δγ/γ,and the terms in longitude containing the argument 2F by I+2dγ/γ. But in setting forth the results such terms are left as perturbations of $\gamma .$ Hence the terms are placed in two classes,those added to the coordinates,and,il addition,those added to the elements.

The original limit set was $\mathrm { { \Omega } } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { { \cdot } } _ { \mathrm { { O 1 } } }$ ,but all short-period terms and niost of the longperiod terms have been computed to O"oo1,and they are so retained here. A star replaces the last figure in the cases where the computations were only made to $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot _ { O I } }$ ·

$3 0 6$ ，The tables are arranged according to the lunar arguments so that $\theta$ remains the same until a new value is set down,and then according to the multiple $j$ of T(or $j ^ { \prime \prime }$ of $\uparrow ^ { \dag \prime }$ ),which again remains the same until a new value is set down，and finally,according to multiples of T-T". The coefficients are set down in units of $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \overbrace { O O I } }$ ,the angle $\alpha$ being so chosen $\left( < 3 ^ { 6 0 ^ { \circ } } \right)$ that they are all positive.

The value of the angle $\alpha$ is also not generally repeated when it is the same for a long series of terms.

# THE INDIRECT ACTION OF THE PLANETS.

Section (i). The Disturbing Function.

$3 ^ { \textrm { f } 7 }$ Transformation to coordinates used in the direct action.-The disturbing function for the action of the Sun on the Moon is (Chap. I. $\ S \ O _ { 3 }$ ）

$$
\frac { m ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { 7 3 } } \left\{ r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } + { \textstyle \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \rho ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \big ( V - V ^ { \prime } \big ) + { \textstyle \frac { 5 } { 8 } } \frac { \rho ^ { \frac { 5 } { 3 } } } { r ^ { \prime } } \cos 3 \big ( V - V ^ { \prime } \big ) + { \textstyle \frac { 3 } { 8 } } \frac { \big ( \bar { r } ^ { 2 } - 5 z ^ { 2 } \big ) } { \bar { r } ^ { \prime } } \rho \cos \big ( V - V ^ { \prime } \big ) \right\} .
$$

to a suffcient approximation.Let $\ 8 r ^ { \prime }$ $\ S V ^ { \prime }$ be the perturbations of $r ^ { \prime } , \ V ^ { \prime }$ from elliptic motion, the plane of reference being the same as before. Put $\delta \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime } / \boldsymbol { r } ^ { \prime } = \delta \boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { \prime }$ and neglect powers of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \vartheta V ^ { \prime }$ beyond the first. Then the disturbing function due to $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \delta V ^ { \prime }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { R = \frac { 3 m ^ { \prime } } { 4 ^ { \prime } ^ { 3 } } \left[ - \ \delta \rho ^ { \prime } \left\{ r ^ { 2 } - 3 z ^ { 2 } + 3 \rho ^ { 2 } \cos { 2 ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } \right\} + \delta V ^ { \prime \prime } \left\{ 2 \rho ^ { 2 } \sin { 2 ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } \right\} \right] } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \overline { { { r ^ { \prime } } } } \nonumber \nonumber \nonumber \nonumber \nonumber \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \sum \delta \rho ^ { \prime } \left\{ \frac { 5 } { 4 \beta } \rho ^ { 5 } \cos { 3 ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } + ( r ^ { 2 } - 5 z ^ { 2 } ) \rho \cos { ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } \right\} + \delta V ^ { \prime } \left\{ \frac { 5 } { 4 } \rho ^ { 5 } \sin { 3 ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( r ^ { 2 } - 5 z ^ { 2 } ) \rho \sin { ( V - V ^ { \prime } ) } \right\} } } \end{array}
$$

Replace the functions of the coordinates of the Moon and Sun by the expressions given in Chap. X., $\ S \ 2 8 5$ ,so that $R$ will now denote that part of the disturbing function which depends on the lunar angle $\boldsymbol { \theta }$ Weobtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R = \frac { 3 7 M ^ { ' } } { 2 } \frac { { \mathrm {  ~ a } } ^ { 2 } } { { \dot { a } } ^ { 2 } } \frac { { \dot { a } } ^ { \prime } } { 2 ^ { 7 } } \left[ - \delta \rho ^ { \prime } ( M _ { 1 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } M _ { 2 } ) \cos \theta + \delta V ^ { \prime } M _ { 3 } \sin \theta - { \delta \rho ^ { \prime } } \frac { \mathrm {  ~ a } } { { \dot { a } } ^ { \prime } } ( M _ { 6 } + 5 M _ { 5 } ) \cos \theta + \delta V _ { - \dot { a } ^ { \prime } } ^ { \mathrm { \tiny ~ a } } ( \frac { 1 } { 4 } M _ { 7 } + \frac { 1 5 } { 4 } M _ { 9 } ) \right] } \end{array}
$$

We can therefore obtain the required lunar functions directly from the results given in $\$ 202$ if we multiply all the series there tabulated by $a ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$

318. Final form of the disturbing function.-Denote the coeffcients of tese functions by accented letters when the multiplication α'/r has been made, so that 0 is now an angle in the products of the series of § 292 by d/r'. Let an angle in δp', δV be Φ, so that we have

\hat { \delta } \rho ^ { \prime } = \rho _ { c } \cos \phi , \quad \hat { \delta } V ^ { \prime } = v _ { s } \sin \phi , \quad m ^ { \prime } = \pi ^ { \prime 2 } \alpha ^ { \prime 3 } .

\begin{array} { r } { R = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \pi ^ { 7 2 } \mathrm { a } ^ { 2 } \bigl ( - 3 \bigr ) \bigl [ ( M _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \frac { 5 } { 2 } M _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) \rho _ { c } \pm M _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } v _ { s } + a _ { 1 } \bigl ( M _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } + 5 M _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } \bigr ) \rho _ { c } \pm a _ { 1 } \bigl ( \frac { 1 } { 4 } M _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 5 } { 4 } M _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } \bigr ) v _ { s } \bigr ] \cos \left( \theta \pm \phi \right) \ . } \end{array}

which is in the required form $\big ( \ S \ z _ { 7 9 } \big )$

For terms $\rho _ { \vartheta } \sin \phi$ in $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ ， $v _ { c } \cos \phi$ in $\delta V ^ { \prime }$ ,replace $\rho _ { c }$ by士 $\rho _ { s }$ 土 $v _ { s }$ by $v _ { e s }$ cos $( \theta \pm \phi )$ by sin $\left( \theta \preceq \phi \right)$

The values of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \delta V ^ { \prime }$ will be taken from NEwcoMb's tables of the Sun \*(with some corrections).He tabulates $\mathrm { I O } ^ { 9 } \log _ { 1 0 } \left( \mathrm { I } + \hat { \vartheta } \rho ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathrm { I O } ^ { 9 } \log _ { 1 0 } e , \hat { \vartheta } \rho ^ { \prime }$ If $\rho _ { c } , \mathrm { ~ \ } \rho _ { s }$ denote his numbers, the parts in $\mathit { l } _ { \mathit { t } } ^ { - }$ which depend on these quantities must be multiplied by $\mathrm { I O } ^ { - 9 } \log _ { e } \mathrm { I O }$ The coefficients ${ \mathfrak { V } } _ { \mathfrak { g } }$ $v _ { s }$ are expressed in seconds of arc； I shall consider them as expressed in units of $\mathrm { { \odot } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { \bigcirc \bigcirc \mathrm { { I } } } }$ ,so that the parts of $\mathcal { R }$ which depend on $\vec { \tau } _ { \mathrm { c } } , \ \vec { \tau } _ { \mathrm { g } }$ must be multiplied by $\mathrm { ~ I ~ O ~ } ^ { - 3 } / 2 \mathrm { O } 6 2 6 5$ The formulae of $\ S \ S \ S$ , Chap. X., will then be available if we put $m ^ { \prime \prime } { } ^ { \prime } m ^ { \prime } = - 3 . 1 0 ^ { - 9 } \log _ { e } { \tt I O } = - [ { \tt \bar { g } } { \cdot } 8 3 9 3 4 ] ,$ so that

$$
f = { \bf \Phi } - \left[ 4 ^ { * } { \bf I } 3 2 9 2 \right] , f ^ { \prime } = { \bf \Phi } - \left[ \overline { { { \bf I } } } ^ { * } 4 \widetilde { \bf \Phi } ^ { 6 } 8 2 \right]
$$

and multiply ${ { \vec { \ell } } _ { c } } , \ { \vec { \upsilon } } _ { g }$ by the factor

$$
\mathtt { I O } ^ { - 3 } / 2 0 6 2 6 5 \div \mathtt { I O } ^ { - 9 } \log _ { e } \mathtt { I O } = \left[ \cdot 3 2 3 3 5 \right]
$$

Then $\mathcal { A }$ is the portion of $\left( 4 \right)$ within square brackets, after $v _ { s }$ has been multiplied by this last factor.

# Section (ii). The Computation of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \ \delta V ^ { \prime }$

319. Forms of expression.--In this chapter perturbations of the first order relative to the masses of the disturbing bodies are alone retained. If we had used the method of the variation of arbitrary constants to find $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \vartheta V ^ { \prime }$ ,the variations of the six elements of the solar orbit would have been obtained in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { a t + \beta + \Sigma \ker \cos \left( \lambda t + \mu \right) , } \end{array}
$$

where $x , \beta , k , \lambda ,$ ， $\mu$ are constants;in the coordinates we have a similar form,with the exception that in the elliptic terms $\mathit { \Pi } _ { \mathcal { k } } ^ { \mathcal { \tilde { \chi } } }$ is of the form $k ^ { \prime } t + k ^ { \prime \prime }$ and $a = 0$ im $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ Further, we can put $a = \beta = 0$ in $\delta V ^ { \prime }$ .All the periodic terms,except those which are independent of the argument of the disturbing planet, have therefore constant coefficients and are taken care of by the preceding method. Hence we have to consider only the terms

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \hat { \delta } \rho ^ { \prime } = \hat { \delta } \alpha ^ { \prime } + \Sigma ( \rho _ { i } + t \rho _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) \cos \left( \dot { \imath } l ^ { \prime } + a _ { i } \right) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \hat { \delta } V ^ { \prime } = \qquad \Sigma _ { i } ( r _ { i } + t v _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) \sin \left( \dot { \imath } l ^ { \prime } + a _ { i } \right) , } } \end{array} \quad \mathrm { ~ . ~ }
$$

320. The non-periodic changes of the solar elements.-Now the solar eccentricity is an observational quantity,and we can therefore choose our arbitraries such that $\tau _ { 1 } = 0 .$ The other $v _ { i }$ and $\rho _ { 2 } , \ \rho _ { 3 }$ are then so small that they may be neglected,and all the portions of the coeficients which depend on $\boldsymbol { \mathit { \Sigma } } _ { \mathcal { \ell } } ^ { t }$ may be expressed by a term $e _ { \mathrm { ~ i ~ } } ^ { \prime } t$ additional to $e ^ { \prime }$ .We have therefore ouly to add to the previous values of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ the term $\rho _ { 1 }$ cos $( l ^ { \prime } + a _ { 1 } )$ ， which is treated in exactly the same way； $\delta \boldsymbol { \alpha } ^ { \prime }$ ，which gives a constant term to $R$ and which is treated as in $\ S \ O _ { 2 7 0 }$ of Chap.X.；and, finally, the effect of a variation of $e ^ { \prime }$ .The mean motion of the solar perigee is not quite zero,and therefore $d l ^ { \prime } / d t$ is not quite equal to $d \mathrm { T } / d t$ ；the only term sensibly affected in the Moon's motion is that with argument ${ { \mathit { l } } ^ { \prime } }$ ,for which the divisor $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { n } } ^ { \prime }$ instcad of $n ^ { \prime } - d \varpi ^ { \prime } / d t$ has been uscd.

The treatment of the variations of $e ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ require special methods； that of $e ^ { \prime }$ produces the wcll-known secular variations of $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , w _ { 3 }$ ；these have been many times computed,and their theoretical values are not in doubt so far as tle lunar equations are concerned. It also produces terms of the form $\mathit { a t } + \mathit { b }$ in the coefficients of the periodic terms. These might bc computed by means of the equations of variations, but I shall,in the next section,give another method which is much more simple for computation.

321. Corrections to Neweomb's values.-The values used in the solar tables $( \ S \ S ^ { \ 8 } )$ are taken from his memoir\* giving the computations. There are two sets of values in the memoir, obtained by independent computations,and the values of LEVERRIER are also givcn for comparison. These four sets of valucs were compared, and thosc in which the results agreed within the limits of accuracy required were accepted. But certain of the coefficients (1) in which NEWcoMB and LEvERRIER did not agree,(2) in which NEwCoMB's two sets of computations differed,and LEVERRIER's results were not given,(3)in which the degree of accuracy was not suficiently high,or (4) in which the coeficients had not been obtained,have been recomputed. For this purpose the ordinary direct method was used-a method so well known † that it is unnecessary to do more than give the results；these are included in the tables of Sect. (v) below. Nearly every one of the few errors found was typographical and easily detected.

NEWcoMB has expressed doubts as to the sufficient accuracy of $\ 8 { a ^ { \prime } }$ and the coefficients independent of the planetary arguments, and he has recomputed these portions.† I have thought it worth while also to recompute these parts by a modification of the direct method, shown in the following section，which gives the required formule rapidly,instead of follwing the method of the variation of constants adopted by NEWCOMB.

322. Computation of $\delta \rho ^ { \prime }$ for the portion independent of the planetary arguments.-We shall only need terms of the second order with respect to the planetary eccentricities and inclination in the constant term,and terms of the first order in the coefficient of the principal eliptic term. In order to get the former,we do not need the second elliptic term, since it can only produce a non-periodic term in combination with a term of the same argument and therefore one of the fourth order.

Dropping accents temporarily, we have for $\delta r$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } \left( \tau \ddot { \delta } r \right) + n ^ { 2 } \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \gamma \dot { \delta } r = a \frac { d R } { d \alpha } + 2 n \int \frac { d R } { d w _ { 1 } } d t = ( A + B \cos l + C \sin l ) n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 2 } \ .
$$

where $R$ is the disturbing function of the Earth's motion due to a planet,and $l$ is the Earth'smeananomalyAlltheletersexet $t$ aresupposed tobeaccentedand $A , B , C$ are quantities whose squares may be neglected.

\* Amer. Eph. Papers,vol. ii. + See,e.g., Cheyne's Planetary Theory, chap. vii.

Putting $a ^ { 3 } / r ^ { 3 } = 1 + \textstyle { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } c ^ { 2 } + 3 e \cos l$ ， we obtain by continued approximation for the particular integral corresponding to the terms on the right,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \hat { \eta } ^ { \alpha } } { q ^ { 2 } } = \varLambda \big ( \mathrm { I } - \frac { \hat { \mathfrak { H } } } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \big ) + \frac { \hat { \mathfrak { H } } } { 4 } e C t + \frac { \mathrm { 1 } } { \sharp } t \big ( B - 3 \mathcal { A } e \big ) \sin \mathrm { ~ \it l - \frac { 1 } { 2 } { \it t } C \cos \mathrm { ~ \it l ~ \phi ~ } ~ } \quad \cdot \qquad \mathrm { ~ \it ~ \cdot ~ } \quad } \end{array} .
$$

No arbitraries are necessary, since they will disappear in connection with corresponding arbitraries in $\delta V$

323. The equation for the longitude is

$$
n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { \mathbf { \bar { r } } - e ^ { 2 } } \hat { \delta } V - 2 \frac { d } { d t } ( r \hat { \delta } r ) + \frac { d r } { d t } \hat { \delta } r ^ { * } = - 2 a \int \frac { d R } { d \alpha } d t - 3 n \int \int \frac { d R } { d w _ { 1 } } \mathcal { H } ^ { 2 } = ( D \sin l + E \cos l + F t ) n a ^ { 2 } \quad .
$$

suppose.Substituting for $\ S ^ { \gamma } ,$ we obtain, amongst others,terms of the form $a t \cos { \mathit { l } _ { \mathit { \tau } } }$ $\beta t \sin l _ { * }$ These terms can be eliminated by supposing that $\varpi , \textit { e }$ receive increments $\delta \varpi , \delta e$ proportional to the time；as we are not computing these increments they may now be neglected. The constant term only adds to the observed value of the mean longitude for $t = 0$ ；it may therefore be dropped.Lct Sn, Se, $\delta l _ { 0 }$ be the changes necessary in $\mathscr { n } , \mathscr { e } , \mathscr { l }$ for $\ell = \ O$ ,in order that the mean motion and the principal elliptic term may have same form as in undisturbed motion.

Then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \big ( \mathrm {  ~ r ~ } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varrho ^ { 2 } \big ) \mathring \partial V = \dot { \iota } \big ( \bar { F } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \bar { B } \varrho + \frac { 3 } { 4 } \bar { A } \varrho ^ { 2 } + \mathfrak { d } n - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varrho ^ { 2 } \mathring \partial \pi \big ) + \cos \mathcal { l } \big ( \bar { E } - C + 2 \varrho \mathring \partial _ { \mathfrak { t } _ { 0 } } \big ) + \sin \mathcal { l } \big ( D + \bar { B } - 4 \bar { A } \varrho + 2 \hat { \partial } \varrho \big ) . } \end{array}
$$

The coefficients of $t$ cos $\it l$ sin $\it l$ , equated to zero, give $\ S _ { \mathcal { \hat { H } } }$ $\vartheta { \mathit { l } } _ { \vartheta }$

Finally, substituting in

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \big ( \mathrm { t } + e ^ { \frac { \alpha } { 2 } } + 2 e \cos { \it I } \big ) ^ { \gamma } \frac { \delta r ^ { * } } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \delta \alpha } { \alpha } - \hat { \delta e } \cos { \it 7 } + e \hat { \delta } \boldsymbol { \eta } _ { 0 } \sin { \it l } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e \hat { \delta } e , } \end{array}
$$

which is the total addition to log $\vec { \pmb { j } } ^ { \ast }$ (that is, the required $\delta \boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { \prime }$ )，, we find the terms

$$
\begin{array} { r } { A ( \mathrm { r } + \varrho ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { 2 } { 3 }  { F ( \mathrm { r } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varrho ^ { 2 } ) } - \frac { 5 } { 1 2 } B e - \frac { 1 } { 4 } D e + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( B + D ) \cos \ell + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( C - B ) \sin \ell \ , } \end{array}
$$

Let

$$
R = R _ { 0 } + R _ { c } \cos { l } + R _ { s } \sin { l } = R _ { 0 } + R _ { 1 } ,
$$

and denote by $I$ the operator ad/da. Then

$$

$$

a simple form which it is easy to compute.

(iv), Chap. XI. The final results are included witl the other terms in $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \delta V ^ { \prime }$

# Section (ii). Second Method. Application to Non-periodic Changes.

325. Statement and Solution.§-The method may be regarded as a particular case of the general problem of four bodies,or of three bodies,or as a general method for treating any motion which is transmitted through one body to another,according to the view we wish to adopt. The last view will be that most convenient for our immediate purposes.

Suppose that we lave been able to solve,in terms of $t$ and arbitrary constants, a dynamical problem which has a force function $F _ { \mathrm { . } }$ This function,expressed initially in terms of the coordinates,may also contain $t$ explicitly and given constants.I shall suppose that it contains $t$ explicitly only through certain functions of the given constants, $\mathcal { U } _ { h }$ ， some of which may therefore be constant and some variable. Now suppose that, owing to some external agency, tle $u _ { h }$ are not the complete values of these given functions, but require certain additions, $\delta \boldsymbol { \mathcal { U } } _ { h }$ , whose values in terms of the time are given. The ordinary method of treatment consists in substituting these new values in $\ b { F }$ and obtaining a disturbing function \* $\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { ~ : ~ } \sum _ { h } ( \delta u _ { h } \partial { \cal F } / d u _ { h } ) } \end{array}$ ; this is accounted for by finding what variable values must be given to the arbitraries, so that when these values are substituted instead of the constant values in the expressions for the coordinates and velocities,we shall have the complete solution of the problem. In this method no account is taken of the fact that F retains the same form with respect to the Uk whatever values may be given to these functions.

In the memoir referred to,an attempt was made to utilise the absence of change in the form of $\mathcal { F }$ by considering the problem in the following way:--To find the variations of the arbitrary constants when not only their variations,but also those of the ${ \mathcal { U } } _ { \hbar }$ ，are substituted in the expressions for the coordinates and velocities.I proved that if this plan were folowed,the solution was equivalent to adding a disturbing function

$$
R = \Sigma _ { h } U _ { h } \bigg ( { \frac { d } { d t } } \widehat \delta u _ { h } - \widehat \delta { \frac { d u _ { h } } { d t } } \bigg ) , \qquad 
$$

where the $\left. U _ { k } \right.$ are defined by the differential equations

$$
\frac { d U _ { h } } { d t } = - \frac { \hat { v } F } { \hat { v } u _ { h } } - \Sigma _ { \scriptscriptstyle k } U _ { \scriptscriptstyle k } \frac { \hat { v } } { \hat { \sigma } u _ { h } } \frac { d u _ { \scriptscriptstyle k } } { d t } - \frac { \hat { v } B } { \hat { v } u _ { \scriptscriptstyle h } } , .
$$

it being supposed that $\mathcal { F }$ is expressed in terms of the coordinates and the $u _ { h }$ that dun/dt is expressible in terms of the Un, and that B is expressed as a function of the coustants and of those $u _ { h }$ which are independent: of $t$

326. Application to the secular changes of $e ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime } ,$ -We have initially $e ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ coulstant. Let $\delta e ^ { \prime } = e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } e ^ { \prime } t$ ， $\delta \varpi ^ { \prime } = { \varpi _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } t$ The ${ \mathcal { U } } _ { \mathcal { h } }$ are $n ^ { \prime }$ ， $\pi ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime } ;$ and therefore $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ ， $e ^ { \prime }$ are independent of the other up and of dun/dt. Also (loc.cit.) Ue, U' contain no nonperiodic terms. Hence

$$
R = -  e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \int e ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \mathit { F } } { \partial e ^ { \prime } } \mathit { \mathcal { A } } t -  \overline { { \omega } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } / \frac { \partial \mathit { F } } { \partial \overline { { \omega } } ^ { \prime } } \mathit { d } t \quad .
$$

in which the nou-periodic term arising from $\hat { o } F / \hat { o } e ^ { \prime }$ must be dropped.

If we substitute this value of R in the equations of Chap. X., αs we have no non-periodic part of R, δc:=O, dwi=O, and therefore the secular accelerations are obtained by putting e'(1 +e1't) for e' in the values of c1， Cz, Cg expressed as functions of $n , \ e , \ \gamma , \ e ^ { \prime } , \ n ^ { \prime }$ ，and finding the values of $n , \ e , \ \gamma$ which result.f The motion of $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ produces nothing in this connection, since it is not present in the $c _ { i } .$

I have shown $^ \ddagger$ that if we neglect ${ a _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 }$ ,a quantity which is quite insensible,the variations of n,e, γ can also be obtained from the equations

$$
c _ { 2 } \widetilde { \partial } \frac { d b _ { 2 } } { d a _ { i } } + c _ { 3 } \widetilde { \partial } \frac { d b _ { 3 } } { d a _ { i } } = \frac { 3 } { 2 } \widetilde { \partial } \frac { d } { d a _ { i } } \bigg ( \frac { \mu } { r } \bigg ) _ { 0 } , \quad a _ { i } = n , e , \gamma \quad . \qquad . \qquad . \qquad . \qquad .
$$

\* For simplicity,only variations of the first order are retained, but the methods are applicable when we take in higher powers.

This is NEwcomB's theorem, Amer.Eph. Papers, vol. v., pt. 3,p.19:

Proc. Lond. Math. Soc. vol. xxvii. p. 154.

where $( \mu / r ) _ { 0 }$ denotes the non-periodic of $\mu / r$ , the functions being expressed in terms of $n , c , \gamma , e ^ { \prime } , n ^ { \prime }$ ,the first four only receiving variations, that of $e ^ { \prime }$ being given.

$3 2 7$ . Thcre remain tle periodic terms of $R .$ Since $e ^ { \prime }$ ， $\varpi ^ { \prime }$ occur in $R$ only through $r ^ { \prime } , \ V ^ { \prime }$ ，we have only to put the periodic terms

$$
\delta \rho ^ { \prime } = - e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cdot e _ { \ell } ^ { \prime } \frac { d \rho ^ { \prime } } { d \ell } - \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \frac { d \rho ^ { \prime } } { d \bar { \omega } ^ { \prime } } , \quad \delta V ^ { \prime } = - e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } e \frac { d V ^ { \prime } } { d \ell ^ { \prime } } - \varpi _ { 1 } \frac { d V ^ { \prime } } { d \bar { \omega } ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \cdot \qquad .
$$

in the formule of $\ S 3 ^ { \intercal } 7$ ， and,after rejecting all non-periodic terms, intcgrate ； the resulting disturbing function consists only of periodic terms.

The variations of the elements are then substituted in the coordinates.In accordance with the principle of the method, we must also put $e ^ { \prime } ( \mathbf { 1 } + e _ { \mathrm { 1 } } ^ { \mathbf { \prime } } t )$ ， $\varpi ^ { \prime } + \varpi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } t$ for $e ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ ,in order to obtain the truc values of the coordinates.

No other secular terms can be produced from the secular variations of the solar elements. It will be shown in Sect.(iv) that those of the inclination and node only produce periodic variations.

The method of this section might have been used for all the indirect perturbations.

# Section (iv). The Motion of the Ecliptic.

328. Choice of the Mean Ecliptic.-Owing to the action of the plancts on the Earth,the plane of the Earth's orbit is not fixed,but has a motion which can be expressed as secular and periodic variations of the inclination and longitude of the node with reference to some fixed plane.I choose as fixed plane the ecliptic at thc date $\mathrm { ~ \it ~ { ~ I ~ } ~ } 8 5 0 ^ { \cdot } \mathrm { { O } }$ ，and refer the motion of the Moon to the mean ecliptic at time $t$ \*The periodic perturbations arc then included in the terms of the Sun's disturbing function which depend on $z ^ { \prime }$ ，portions which have been previously ncglected (Chap. I., $\ S 3 )$ .To be included in this mean ecliptic are one or two minute inequalities of very long period which then give rise to no terms in the Moon's motion, but which would do so if included in $z ^ { \prime }$

329. Thc disturbing function for the moving ccliptic.-This is most easily found in a general manner. Let $x , y , z , \ u , \ v , \ w$ be the coordinates and velocities of a particle of mass ${ \mathit { i } } { \mathit { \partial } } \partial _ { \mathit { i } }$ , referred to rectangular axes which have velocities $\theta _ { \mathrm { { i } } } , \theta _ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } , $ $\theta _ { 3 }$ about themselves, and let the force function be denotcd by $\hbar \hbar F .$ The equations of motion of $m$ are then given by

$$
\frac { d u } { d t } = - \frac { \hat { v } H } { \hat { o } x } , \frac { d v } { d t } = - \frac { \hat { v } H } { \hat { o } y } , \frac { d w } { d t } = - \frac { \hat { o } H } { \hat { o } z } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \hat { o } H } { \hat { o } u } , \quad \quad \quad \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { \hat { c } H } { \hat { c } v } , \quad \quad \quad \frac { d z } { d t } = \frac { \hat { c } H } { \hat { \alpha } w } ,
$$

where

$$
H = \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \big ( u ^ { 2 } + v ^ { 2 } + w ^ { 2 } \big ) - F - R ~ ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R = v x \theta _ { \mathrm { s } } - w x x \theta _ { \mathrm { i } } + u x y \theta _ { \mathrm { i } } - u y \theta _ { \mathrm { s } } + u z \theta _ { \mathrm { i } } - v z \theta _ { \mathrm { i } } , } \end{array}
$$

if we assume that $\theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \theta _ { \mathrm { { 3 } } }$ are independent of $x , y , z , u , v , w .$

$^ \ast \mathrm  ~ \}$ have discussed this point in vol.Ixviii. p. $4 5 ^ { \mathrm { 0 - 4 } 5 5 }$ ,of the Monthly Notices,and have also given there the substance of this section.

When $R { = } 0$ ， the equations become the same as those referred to fixed axes, and therefore $R$ is the disturbing function for the motion of the axes.

Let $\it { i } ^ { \prime }$ be the inelination of the moving eeliptic (xy plane) to that of $1 8 5 0 \mathrm { { \cdot } } 0$ ： $\tau$ the longitude of the node on the fixed ecliptic,and for the origin of longitudes on the moving ecliptic take the “departure point” whose distance from the node on the moving ecliptic is the same as the distance from the node of the origin of reckoning on the fixed ecliptic. Then by EuLER's equations

$$
\theta _ { 1 } = \frac { d i ^ { \prime } } { d \ell } \cos \tau - \frac { d \tau } { d \ell } \sin i ^ { \prime } \sin \tau , \theta _ { 2 } = \frac { d i ^ { \prime } } { d \ell } \sin \tau + \frac { d \tau } { d \ell } \sin i ^ { \prime } \cos \tau , \theta _ { 3 } = \frac { d \tau } { d \ell } ( \cos i ^ { \prime } - \tau ) \qquad .
$$

The values of $\therefore d i ^ { \prime } / d t , d \tau / d t$ are small quantities of the first order, so that their squares and products may be neglected. On substituting the values,so limited, in $\mathcal { R }$ ， we find the factor $d i ^ { \prime } / d t$ common to all the terms,and therefore, since $u , v , w$ differ from $d x / d t , d y / d t , d z / d t$ by quantities of the same order,

$$
R = \frac { d \bar { \lambda } } { d t } Q \ , \quad Q = \left( y \frac { d z } { d t } - z \frac { d { \mathcal { Y } } } { d t } \right) \cos { \tau } - \left( x \frac { d z } { d t } - z \frac { d x } { d t } \right) \sin { \tau } \qquad .
$$

This disturbing function is available for any moving ecliptic so long as we may neglect the squares of its perturbations, but under the assumptions of $\ S \ S = 8$ we substitute for $i ^ { \prime }$ only its secular part.The resulting disturbing function I denote by $R _ { 1 }$

$3 3 \mathrm { O }$ The disturbing function for perturbations of the Earth out of the plane of reference is, if we neglect squares of $z ^ { \prime }$ and the terms dependent on $a$ ，

$$
R = \frac { m ^ { \prime } } { p ^ { \prime 3 } } , \frac { 3 ( x x ^ { \prime } + y y ^ { \prime } ) z z ^ { \prime } } { q ^ { \prime 2 } } . \qquad , \qquad . \qquad . \qquad . \qquad .
$$

by $\ S \ O 3$ , Chap.I.With the notation and limitations of the previous section,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z ^ { ' } = \dot { \iota } ^ { * } ( \dot { y ^ { \prime } } \cos \tau - \dot { x ^ { \prime } } \sin \tau ) \qquad . \qquad \cdot \qquad , \qquad . } \end{array}
$$

Since this expression has the small factor $\vec { \it { \tau } }$ we consider $\curvearrowleft$ as a constant. In the paper referred to in $\ S \ 3 ^ { 2 8 , \ 1 }$ have shown that the expression (20) for $R$ can be transformed into

$$
R = i ^ { \prime } \left\{ \left( z \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } - y \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } \right) \cos \tau - \left( z \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } \right) \sin \tau \right\} = - i ^ { \prime } \frac { d Q } { d t } \qquad . \qquad .
$$

which again is a perfectly general expression for the disturbing function when we can neglect squares of the perturbations. The value of $Q$ is that given in the previous paragraph,and therefore the computation of one function serves for both disturbances. Under the assumptions of $\ S \ 3 2 8$ we substitute for $\bar { \mathcal { k } ^ { \prime } }$ in (22） only its periodic part, neglecting the minute perturbations of $\dot { \ i } ^ { \prime }$ which are of long period relatively to that of the Moon's node. The disturbing function thus limited will be denoted by $R _ { 2 }$

331. Computation of $Q .$ -We have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { Q = \displaystyle \frac { d z } { d t } \rho \sin \left( V - \tau \right) - z \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left\{ \rho \sin \left( V - \tau \right) \right\} } \ ~ } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = \mathrm { r e a l ~ p a r t ~ o f ~ } \left( n - n ^ { \prime } \right) \left[ D z \cdot u \zeta ^ { - 1 } e ^ { w _ { 1 } \iota } \cdot z D ( u \zeta ^ { - 1 } e ^ { w _ { 1 } \iota } ) \right] e ^ { - \tau \iota } } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = \displaystyle \quad , \quad \quad \displaystyle \frac { n a ^ { 2 } } { \iota } \mathrm { k } \left[ \displaystyle \frac { 2 u \zeta ^ { - 1 } \cdot D ( \iota z ) - ( D + \textup { \bf r } + \textup { \bf m } ) ( \iota z \cdot u \zeta ^ { - 1 } ) } { \mathrm { k } ( \textup { \bf r } + \mathfrak { m } ) a ^ { 2 } } \right] e ^ { ( w _ { 1 } - \tau ) \iota } . } } \end{array}
$$

The expansion of the portion in square brackets has been given in $\ S \ O \ 2 9 1$ , Chap. X. Let $\mathcal { M }$ exp. $\theta _ { 1 } \iota$ be one of the terms of this expansion. Then one of the terms of $Q$ is given by

$$
Q = { \tt m a } ^ { 2 } { \tt k } M \sin \left( \theta _ { 1 } + w _ { 1 } - \tau \right) = { \tt m a } ^ { 2 } { \tt k } M \cos \left( \theta _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 1 } - \tau - { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \right) .
$$

332. Computation of $R _ { 1 }$ -Let ${ d i ^ { \prime } } / { d t } = p$ .Then

$$
R _ { 1 } { = } \frac { 4 p } { n ^ { \prime } } \cdot \frac { \mathrm { k } } { m } \bigg [ \frac { 1 } { 4 } n ^ { \prime 2 } { \mathrm { a } } ^ { 2 } M \cos \bigg ( \theta _ { 1 } + w _ { 1 } - \tau - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \bigg ) \bigg ] ,
$$

and therefore if we put $m ^ { \prime \prime } / m ^ { \prime }$ for the factor outside the square brackets the equations of $\ S \ S \ S$ ,Chap.X.,can be immediately applied ； here $A = M .$

The value of $\mathcal { p }$ is $^ { * } \mathrm { ~ O ~ } ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { 4 7 } \mathrm { ~ I ~ O ~ }$ per annum, $n ^ { \prime } { = } 3 5 4 8 ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { 2 }$ per diem,and therefore

giving

$$
\frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 4 ^ { ( \ ` 4 7 1 0 ) \mathrm { k } } } { ( 3 6 5 ^ { \cdot 2 } 5 ) ( 3 5 4 ^ { 8 \cdot 2 } ) m } = \left[ \overline { { { 7 } } } { \cdot } 9 3 9 7 \right] ,
$$

$$
f { } = \left[ 6 ^ { \cdot } { } _ { 2 } 3 3 3 \right] , ~ f ^ { \prime } { } = \left[ { \tt I } ^ { \cdot } { } 5 { } 5 { } 7 { } ^ { 2 } \right] .
$$

In finding derivatives with respect to $\mathscr { n }$ ,we must use $d ( n \mathrm { k a } ^ { 2 } A ) / d n .$

333. Computation of $R _ { 2 }$ Let $P$ cos $\phi$ be any term of $\dot { \iota } ^ { \prime }$ reckoned in units of $\scriptstyle { \mathsf { O } } ^ { \prime \prime } { \mathsf { O O 1 } }$ ,and ${ \bf s _ { 1 } }$ the number of seconds in the daily motion of the angle $\theta _ { 1 } + w _ { 1 } - \tau = \theta$ Then, using the first form for $Q$ we have

where

$$
R _ { 2 } { = } \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } \frac { 1 } { 4 } n ^ { \prime 2 } \mathrm { { a } ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { { \varDelta } } \mathrm { { \varDelta } } \cos \left( \theta \pm \phi \right) ,
$$

$$
A = - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \mathtt { s } _ { 1 } M P , \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } = { \frac { 4 } { { \tt I O O O } } } { \frac { { \bf I } } { { \sf 2 0 0 } 2 6 2 6 5 } } { \frac { { \bf I } } { 3 5 4 8 ^ { \circ } 2 } } { \frac { { \bf k } } { m } } = + \left[ { \overline { { { \bf \Pi } ^ { 2 } } } } \cdot \xi { \bf I } 4 9 \right] .
$$

The computation of derivatives with respet to $\mathscr { n }$ must be made with $\mathrm { S } _ { 1 } M \mathrm { a } ^ { 2 }$

The last fact makes the computation easier with formulas (2o),(21).We have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { R _ { 2 } = \frac { \Im } { 2 } \pi ^ { \prime 2 } \hat { u } ^ { \prime } \Big ( \frac { \alpha ^ { \prime } } { \hat { r } ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { 3 } \rho z \big \{ \sin \big ( V - \tau \big ) - \sin \big ( V - 2 V ^ { \prime } + \tau \big ) \big \} } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \pi ^ { \prime 2 } { \mathrm { a } } ^ { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } \big \{ - M _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } P \cos \big ( \theta \pm \phi \big ) \mathrm { w i t h } h ^ { \prime \prime } = \tau , \qquad } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { + \frac { 1 } { 4 } { u } ^ { \prime 2 } { \mathrm { a } } ^ { 2 } \frac { m ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } \big \{ M _ { 4 } ^ { \prime \prime } P \cos \big ( \theta \pm \phi \big ) \big \} \mathrm { w i t h } h ^ { \prime \prime } = 2 V ^ { \prime } - \tau , } \end{array}
$$

using the third formula of $\ S \ 2 8 5$ ， Chap. X.,after multiplication by $a ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$ For these \* LEVERRtER, Ann.Obs. Paris, vol.iv.p.5o,after coretionfor the adopted masses of theplaets.

terms it is sufficiently accurate to neglect the solar eccentricity in finding the Moon factors ; this gives $M _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } { = } M _ { 4 } ^ { \prime \prime } { = } M _ { 4 } , \ P ^ { \prime } { = } \ P$ The value of $m ^ { \prime \prime } / m ^ { \prime }$ is

$$
\frac { \eta ^ { \prime \prime } } { m ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 3 } { \tt I 0 0 0 } \cdot \frac { \tt I } { 2 0 6 2 6 5 } = \tt [ \breve { 8 } ^ { \cdot } I 6 2 7 ] .
$$

The terms,arising from these disturbing functions,in the results at the end of this chapter, are those in longitude which contain the argument $w _ { 3 }$ explicitly， and those in latitude which contain the argument $w _ { 1 }$ explicitly. The most important are two of the latter with arguments $w _ { 1 } { \pm } ( \mathfrak { s } \mathrm { T } - 3 \mathrm { V } )$ and having coefficients $0 ^ { \prime \prime } { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { O } 7 7 }$ and $\mathrm { O } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot O } 3 0$ respectively.

# Section (v). Numerical Values of the Earth's Perturbations.

334. Sources.-The general values for $\delta \rho ^ { \prime } , \vartheta V ^ { \prime }$ are taken from NEwcoMB's tables of the Sun $( \ S \ 3 \mathbb { 1 } \ S )$ ,with some corrections and additions $\left( \ S \ 3 2 \operatorname { I } \right)$ ； the secular variations of $e ^ { \prime } , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ are from the same source. The terms independent of the planet's arguments are found in $\ S \_ 3 2 4$ The values of $i ^ { \prime } , \tau$ are from LEVERRIER,\* after correction for the masses of the planets adopted here ； the coeffcient of ${ } _ { 2 } , \int - 2 \tau$ in ${ \bf \chi } _ { \ell } ^ { \bullet \prime }$ was recomputed.

335. Notation.-The values of $\delta \boldsymbol { \rho } ^ { \prime }$ ， $\ S V ^ { \prime }$ are given by NEwcoMB as cosines and sines of the mean anomalies； it was convenient to retain them in this form. LEvERRIER gives the values of $\it { i } ^ { \prime }$ in terms of literal arguments ； these were combined and expressed in terms of the mean longitudes, which is the final form for all the inequalitics due to planetary action (Chap. XV.). Hence, I put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \delta \rho ^ { \prime } } { \delta V ^ { \prime } } = \rho _ { c } \cos \left\{ j ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { T } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) + j ^ { \prime \prime } ( \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime \prime } ) \right\} + \frac { \rho _ { s } } { v _ { c } } \sin \left\{ j ^ { \prime } ( \mathrm { T } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) + j ^ { \prime \prime } ( \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime \prime } ) \right\} , \quad \vec { \tau } = \dot { r } _ { c } \cos \left( j ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { T } + j ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { T } ^ { \prime \prime } + a ^ { \circ } \right) } \end{array}
$$

Also, $v _ { c } , \ v _ { s }$ are expressed in units of $\mathrm { \Omega } ^ { \prime \prime } \mathrm { \cdot } _ { \mathrm { O O I } }$ ，while $\rho _ { c } , \ \rho _ { s }$ are expressed in absolute units multiplied by $\operatorname { I O } ^ { 9 } \log _ { 1 0 } \varrho$ , that is,theyare the coefficients in $\cos { \log _ { 1 0 } \big ( \mathrm { { I } } + \delta \rho \big ) }$

For the secular terms, $d e ^ { \prime } / d t = e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } e ^ { \prime }$ ${ ( } \overline { { \omega } } ^ { \prime } / d t = \varpi _ { \mathrm { { i } } } { } ^ { \prime }$
